Summary: Missing Status-Based Restrictions in Cancellation API Allow Unauthorized Load/Offer Cancellation
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Missing Status-Based Restrictions in Cancellation API Allow Unauthorized Load/Offer Cancellation

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.4
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Confirmation Status Update API

*Affected Path:*
BE: {{PATCH /api/v3/confirmations/update/status}}

----

h2. Description

It is observed that the application does not enforce proper status-based business logic restrictions in the cancellation API. As per requirements, loader/trucker users are only allowed to cancel loads/offers when the status is *“In Process”*, *“Available”*, or *“Expiring”*.

During testing, it was possible to bypass these restrictions by directly invoking the backend API. By manipulating the request, loads/offers were successfully cancelled even when their status was *“Pending”*, *“Confirmed”*, or already *“Cancelled”*. This indicates that the backend does not validate the current state of the resource before processing cancellation requests. The same issue is also applicable to the offer-side flows.

----

h2. Impact

* Unauthorized cancellation of loads/offers in restricted states
* Violation of defined business workflows and process integrity
* Financial and operational discrepancies due to invalid cancellations
* Potential misuse by malicious users to disrupt logistics operations
* Data inconsistency across system states and reporting

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in as a loader or trucker user
# Identify a load/offer in restricted states:
#* {{Pending}}, {{Confirmed}}, or {{Cancelled}}
# Intercept or craft the API request:
{noformat}PATCH /api/v3/confirmations/update/status
{noformat}
# Modify the payload to trigger cancellation:
{noformat}{
  "status": "cancelled",
  "id": "<target-confirmation-id>"
}
{noformat}
# Send the request
# Observe that the load/offer is successfully cancelled despite being in a restricted state

----

h2. Root Cause

The backend does not validate the current status of loads/offers before allowing cancellation and lacks enforcement of business logic constraints on allowed state transitions.

----

h2. Solution

* Enforce *strict status-based validation* for cancellation requests
* Allow cancellation only for:
** {{In Process}}, {{Available}}, {{Expiring}} states
* Reject requests attempting cancellation from:
** {{Pending}}, {{Confirmed}}, {{Cancelled}}, or other restricted states
* Implement *state machine validation* for allowed transitions
* Apply *server-side business logic enforcement* independent of frontend
* Extend validation to *offer-side workflows* as well
* Log and monitor abnormal cancellation attempts

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-840: Business Logic Errors
* CWE-285: Improper Authorization

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP API Top 10:2023 – API6:2023 Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows*
Missing enforcement of workflow rules allows unauthorized state transitions and cancellation actions.
* *OWASP Top 10:2025 – A06 Insecure Design*
Lack of proper business logic validation and state transition controls reflects insecure design in critical operations.

----

Summary: Missing Capacity Validation Allows Exceeding Maximum Load Limits
Description:

It is observed that the application does not enforce proper backend validation for capacity constraints during load creation. The “Load Information” section requires selecting a predefined capacity range (e.g., 36–50 tons), and the total quantity defined across loading and unloading points should fall within this selected range.

During testing, it was possible to bypass frontend validations by directly manipulating the API request. A load request was successfully created with a total capacity of 59 tons, exceeding the maximum allowed range of 50 tons. This indicates that the backend does not validate the consistency between selected capacity range and the aggregated quantities of loading/unloading points.

----

h2. Impact

* Violation of business constraints for load capacity
* Incorrect load data affecting logistics operations
* Financial discrepancies in pricing, billing, and allocation
* Potential abuse to bypass operational limits
* Data inconsistency across dependent systems

{noformat}POST /api/v3/users/{loader-id}/loads{noformat}
# Modify the payload:

# Send the modified request
# Observe that the load request is successfully created despite exceeding the allowed capacity

----

h2. Root Cause

The backend does not enforce validation to ensure that the total loading/unloading quantity falls within the selected capacity range, relying solely on frontend validation.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement *strict server-side validation* for capacity limits
* Validate that *total loading/unloading quantity falls within selected capacity range*
* Enforce *maximum allowed limit (e.g., 50 tons)* at API level
* Apply *cross-field validation* between capacity range and point-wise quantities
* Reject requests violating defined business rules

* Log and monitor anomalous load creation attempts

Summary: IDOR on Ledger Data APIs Allowing Unauthorized Access to Other Users’ Ledger
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: IDOR on Ledger Data APIs Allowing Unauthorized Access to Other Users’ Ledger

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.6
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Ledger Data Retrieval API

*Affected Path:*

* {{GET /api/v3/ledgers/loaders/{id}}}
* {{GET /api/v3/ledgers/truckers/{id}}}

----

h2. Description

It is observed that the application is vulnerable to Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) in the ledger data retrieval APIs. The endpoint relies on a user-supplied {{id}} parameter to fetch ledger details but does not enforce proper ownership validation.

During testing, it was possible to access ledger data of other users within the same role (e.g., one loader accessing another loader’s ledger) by modifying the {{id}} parameter in the request. Although cross-role access (loader ↔ trucker) is restricted, horizontal access control is missing within the same role, allowing unauthorized data exposure.

----

h2. Impact

* Unauthorized access to sensitive financial/ledger data
* Breach of data confidentiality between users of the same role
* Potential exposure of transaction history and financial records
* Compliance violations (e.g., data protection regulations)
* Loss of user trust and platform integrity

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in as a valid loader/trucker user and capture the request
# Send a request to fetch ledger data:
{noformat}GET /api/v3/ledgers/loaders/{own-id}
Authorization: Bearer <valid_token>
{noformat}
# Modify the {{id}} parameter to another user’s ID (same role):
{noformat}GET /api/v3/ledgers/loaders/{other-user-id}
{noformat}
# Send the request with the same authorization token
# Observe that ledger data of another user is returned successfully

----

h2. Root Cause

The backend does not validate whether the requested {{id}} belongs to the authenticated user and lacks proper object-level authorization checks.

----

h2. Solution

* Enforce *object-level authorization (BOLA)* on all ledger APIs
* Validate that the requested {{id}} strictly belongs to the authenticated user
* Avoid relying on user-controlled identifiers without verification
* Implement *access control middleware* to enforce ownership checks
* Use *indirect references or scoped identifiers* where possible
* Log and monitor unauthorized access attempts

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
* CWE-284: Improper Access Control

----

h2. OWASP Category

The API allows access to other users’ ledger data by manipulating object identifiers without proper authorization checks.

Missing enforcement of access control policies leads to unauthorized data access across users.

Summary: Unauthorized Load Cancellation via Missing Business Logic and Authorization Checks
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Unauthorized Load Cancellation via Missing Business Logic and Authorization Checks

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.5
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Load/Offer Cancellation API

*Affected Path:*
BE: {{POST /api/v3/loads/cancel-remarks}} (with {{ids}} parameter for load/offer ID)

----

h2. Description

It is observed that the application does not enforce proper business logic and authorization checks for load/offer cancellation. As per defined requirements, loader/trucker users are only allowed to cancel loads when the status is *“In Process”*, *“Available”*, or *“Expiring”*. However, these restrictions are not enforced at the backend.

During testing, by manipulating the {{ids}} parameter (representing load/offer IDs), it was possible to cancel loads/offers in restricted states such as *“Expired”*, *“Confirmed”*, and *“Completed”*. Additionally, for confirmed loads where a {{ConfirmationID}} is assigned, the {{party}} parameter (indicating who initiated the cancellation) is required as per business logic. This validation is also missing, allowing cancellation of confirmed loads without specifying the {{party}} field.

----

h2. Impact

* Unauthorized cancellation of completed or confirmed loads
* Violation of critical business workflows and operational integrity
* Financial losses due to invalid cancellations
* Data inconsistency across logistics and tracking systems
* Potential abuse by malicious users to disrupt operations

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in as a loader or trucker user and capture the cancellation request
# Intercept the API call:
{noformat}POST /api/v3/loads/cancel
{noformat}
# Modify the request payload:
#* Set {{ids}} to a load/offer ID with status:
#** {{Expired}}, {{Confirmed}}, or {{Completed}}
# (Optional) Remove the {{party}} parameter even when {{ConfirmationID}} exists
# Send the request:
{noformat}{
  "ids": ["<target-load-id>"]
}
{noformat}
# Observe that the load/offer is successfully cancelled despite invalid status or missing required parameters

----

h2. Root Cause

The backend does not enforce status-based business rules and conditional parameter validation (e.g., {{party}} when {{ConfirmationID}} exists), allowing manipulation of request parameters to bypass intended restrictions.

----

h2. Solution

* Enforce *strict status validation* to allow cancellation only for permitted states
* Implement *server-side business logic validation* independent of frontend
* Validate *conditional parameters* (e.g., require {{party}} when {{ConfirmationID}} is present)
* Restrict cancellation based on *role and ownership checks*
* Reject requests attempting to cancel *completed/confirmed/expired loads*
* Implement *centralized validation layer* for business rules
* Log and monitor anomalous cancellation attempts

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-285: Improper Authorization

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP API Top 10:2023 – API6:2023 Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows*
Missing enforcement of business rules allows users to perform unauthorized cancellation of loads outside permitted states.

While the frontend enforces constraints on capacity selection (e.g., predefined ranges such as 36–50 tons), these validations are not enforced at the backend.

During testing, it was possible to manipulate the API request and submit loading/unloading values exceeding the selected capacity range. For example, even when the maximum allowed load capacity is 50 tons, a payload with 59 tons was accepted and processed successfully. Similarly, selecting a lower capacity bracket (e.g., 1–5 tons) still allows submission of higher quantities via API manipulation. This indicates that backend validation is missing for cross-field consistency and business logic enforcement.

----

h2. Impact

* Inconsistent and incorrect load data stored in the system
* Financial and operational discrepancies in load calculations
* Violation of business rules for load capacity constraints
* Potential abuse to bypass system limits for logistics operations
* Downstream impact on billing, allocation, and reporting systems

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in as a loader user and capture the request for load creation
# Initiate load creation via UI:
#* Select capacity range (e.g., {{36–50 tons}})
# Intercept the request:
{noformat}POST /api/v3/users/{loader-id}/loads
{noformat}
# Modify the payload:
#* Set loading/unloading quantities exceeding allowed limits (e.g., {{59 tons}})
# Forward the request to the server
# Observe that the load is successfully created despite exceeding defined capacity
# Repeat with smaller capacity brackets (e.g., {{1–5 tons}}) and inject higher values

----

h2. Root Cause

The backend does not validate consistency between selected load capacity and the sum or individual values of loading/unloading quantities, relying solely on frontend validation.

----

h2. Solution

* Enforce *server-side validation* for capacity limits
* Validate that *total loading/unloading quantity does not exceed selected capacity range*
* Ensure *consistency checks across related fields (cross-field validation)*
* Reject requests where payload values exceed allowed business constraints
* Implement *centralized validation logic* for load creation APIs
* Add *strict schema validation* (e.g., using DTOs or validation frameworks)
* Log and monitor anomalous load creation attempts

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-20: Improper Input Validation
* CWE-840: Business Logic Errors

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP API Top 10:2023 – API6:2023 Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows*
Lack of enforcement of business rules allows users to bypass critical constraints in load creation workflows.
* *OWASP Top 10:2025 – A04 Cryptographic Failures*
Although not cryptographic in nature, the issue stems from improper enforcement of data integrity and validation, leading to inconsistent system behavior.

----

Summary: Password Reset Token Reuse Due to Missing Single-Use Enforcement
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Password Reset Token Reuse Due to Missing Single-Use Enforcement

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.0
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Forgot Password

*Affected Path:*
FE: {{/authentication/forgot-password}}
BE: {{POST /api/v3/password/email}} ; {{POST /authentication/reset-password/{token}?email={user-email}}}

----

h2. Description

It is observed that the application allows password reset tokens to be reused multiple times and does not invalidate them after a successful password reset. A reset link generated via the forgot password functionality remains valid even after it has already been used once.

During testing, a password reset link was used to successfully change the user’s password. The same link was then reused again to reset the password multiple times without any restriction. This indicates that the application does not enforce single-use token validation or proper expiration after use, allowing reuse of sensitive reset tokens.

----

h2. Impact

* Unauthorized repeated password resets using the same token
* Increased risk of account takeover if reset link is exposed
* Loss of control over account recovery process
* Weak token lifecycle management and security controls
* Potential abuse in automated or malicious reset scenarios

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Navigate to the forgot password functionality:
{{FE: /authentication/forgot-password}}
# Submit a password reset request:
{noformat}POST /api/v3/password/email
{noformat}
# Capture the password reset link/token received via email
# Use the reset link to change the password:
{noformat}POST /authentication/reset-password/{token}?email={user-email}
{noformat}
# Observe that the password is successfully updated
# Reuse the *same reset link/token* again
# Observe that the password can be reset again using the same token

----

h2. Root Cause

The backend does not invalidate password reset tokens after successful use and does not enforce single-use validation for reset operations.

----

h2. Solution

* Enforce *single-use tokens* by invalidating the token immediately after successful password reset
* Maintain *token state (used/unused/expired)* in backend storage
* Implement *token expiration after first successful use*
* Ensure tokens cannot be reused even within their validity window
* Log and monitor repeated reset attempts using the same token
* Apply additional verification checks if reuse attempts are detected

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-640: Weak Password Recovery Mechanism for Forgotten Password
* CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP API Top 10:2023 – API2:2023 Broken Authentication*
Reusable password reset tokens allow repeated unauthorized credential changes, breaking authentication guarantees.
* *OWASP Top 10:2025 – A07 Authentication Failures*
Improper implementation of password recovery and token lifecycle leads to authentication weaknesses.

Summary: Previous Password Reset Tokens Remain Valid After Issuance of New Token
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Previous Password Reset Tokens Remain Valid After Issuance of New Token

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 7.8
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Forgot Password

*Affected Path:*
FE: {{/authentication/forgot-password}}
BE: {{POST /api/v3/password/email}} ; {{POST /authentication/reset-password/{token}?email={user-email}}}

----

h2. Description

It is observed that the application does not invalidate previously issued password reset tokens when a new reset link is generated. When a user initiates multiple forgot password requests, the system issues multiple valid reset tokens without revoking the earlier ones.

During testing, after triggering the forgot password functionality multiple times for the same user, all generated reset links remained valid. An older reset link was successfully used to reset the user’s password even after a newer token had been issued. This indicates that the application does not enforce single-use or latest-token-only validation for password reset operations, allowing reuse of stale tokens.

----

h2. Impact

* Unauthorized password reset using previously issued tokens
* Increased attack window due to multiple valid tokens
* Account takeover risk if older reset links are exposed
* Weak token lifecycle and session management
* Reduced reliability of password reset as a security control

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Navigate to the forgot password functionality:
{{FE: /authentication/forgot-password}}
# Submit a password reset request using a valid registered email:
{noformat}POST /api/v3/password/email
{noformat}
# Capture the password reset link/token received via email
# Repeat the forgot password request multiple times to generate additional reset tokens
# Use an *older reset link*:
{noformat}POST /authentication/reset-password/{old_token}?email={user-email}
{noformat}
# Observe that the password reset is successfully completed using the old token
# Confirm that multiple tokens remain valid simultaneously

----

h2. Root Cause

The backend does not invalidate previously issued password reset tokens when generating new ones and does not enforce single-use or latest-token-only validation.

----

h2. Solution

* Invalidate all previous reset tokens when a new token is generated
* Enforce *single-use tokens* with immediate invalidation after successful reset
* Implement *short expiration time* for reset tokens
* Store and validate tokens server-side with status tracking (active/used/expired)
* Bind tokens to user context and invalidate upon reuse attempts
* Log and monitor multiple reset requests for anomaly detection

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-640: Weak Password Recovery Mechanism for Forgotten Password
* CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP API Top 10:2023 – API2:2023 Broken Authentication*
Improper handling of password reset tokens allows reuse of stale tokens, leading to authentication bypass.
* *OWASP Top 10:2025 – A07 Authentication Failures*
Weak password recovery implementation and lack of token lifecycle control result in authentication weaknesses.

Summary: Active Sessions Not Invalidated After Password Change or Reset
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Active Sessions Not Invalidated After Password Change or Reset

*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 6.5
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Authentication and Session Management

*Affected Path:*

* {{/api/auth/change-password}}
* {{/api/auth/forgot-password}}
* {{/api/auth/reset-password}}
* All authenticated endpoints relying on previously issued tokens

----

h2. Description

The application does not invalidate existing active sessions or authentication tokens after a user changes or resets their password. Previously issued access tokens remain valid and continue to provide access to protected resources even after credential updates.

An attacker who has already obtained a valid session or token can exploit this by maintaining access despite the user performing a password change or reset. The application lacks mechanisms to revoke existing sessions, rotate tokens, or enforce re-authentication after sensitive credential changes.

----

h2. Impact

* Persistent unauthorized access even after password change/reset
* Increased risk of account takeover persistence
* Inability to terminate compromised sessions effectively
* Security control bypass in incident response scenarios
* Potential compliance risks due to weak session management

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application and capture a valid access token
# From another session or browser, perform:
#* Password change via {{/api/auth/change-password}}, or
#* Password reset via {{/api/auth/reset-password}}
# After password update, reuse the previously captured token
# Send a request to any protected endpoint:
{noformat}GET /api/user/profile
Authorization: Bearer <old_access_token>
{noformat}
# Observe that access is still granted using the old token

----

h2. Root Cause

The backend does not implement session invalidation or token revocation logic upon password change or reset, allowing previously issued tokens to remain valid until expiration.

----

h2. Solution

* Invalidate *all active sessions/tokens* upon password change or reset
* Implement *token revocation or blacklist mechanism*
* Use *short-lived access tokens* with enforced expiration
* Introduce *refresh token rotation with revocation on use*
* Maintain *session tracking (session IDs or jti claims)* for centralized invalidation
* Force *re-authentication across all devices* after credential changes

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration
* CWE-287: Improper Authentication

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP API Top 10:2023 – API2:2023 Broken Authentication*
Failure to invalidate sessions after credential changes allows continued unauthorized access.
* *OWASP Top 10:2025 – A07 Authentication Failures*
Improper session lifecycle management leads to weak authentication controls and persistent sessions.

Summary: Broken Function Level Authorization in Profile Settings Update API
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Broken Function Level Authorization in Profile Settings Update API

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.7
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* User Profile Settings Update API

*Affected Path:*

* {{PUT /api/v3/users/{user-id}/update-profile-settings}}

----

h2. Description

The application does not enforce proper function-level authorization on the profile settings update API, allowing users to modify profile configurations of other user types outside their permitted scope. Although the UI restricts loader operators from accessing trucker user settings (and vice versa), these restrictions are not enforced at the API level.

An attacker can exploit this by directly invoking the API and supplying a target {{user-id}} belonging to another role (e.g., loader operator modifying trucker user or vice versa). The application lacks server-side role validation and authorization checks, allowing unauthorized cross-role configuration changes through crafted API requests.

----

h2. Impact

* Unauthorized modification of user profile settings across roles
* Violation of business logic and role-based access control policies
* Potential abuse of configuration settings leading to privilege misuse
* Loss of data integrity and trust boundaries between user roles
* Increased risk of further privilege escalation scenarios

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in as a *loader operator* and capture the access token
# Identify a valid {{user-id}} belonging to a *trucker user*
# Send the following request:
{noformat}PUT /api/v3/users/{trucker-user-id}/update-profile-settings
Authorization: Bearer <loader_operator_token>
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "settings": {
    "some_config": "modified_value"
  }
}
{noformat}
# Observe that the request is successfully processed and settings are updated
# Repeat the same by logging in as a *trucker operator* and modifying a *loader user* profile
# Confirm that cross-role updates are allowed despite UI restrictions

----

h2. Root Cause

The backend fails to implement proper role-based authorization checks for function-level access and relies solely on frontend/UI restrictions, allowing direct API calls to bypass intended access controls.

----

h2. Solution

* Enforce *strict server-side role-based access control (RBAC)* for all sensitive APIs
* Validate that the requesting user has permission to modify the target {{user-id}} based on role and ownership
* Implement *function-level authorization checks* for profile settings updates
* Avoid relying on *UI restrictions* as a security control
* Introduce *access control middleware* to validate role and resource mapping
* Log and monitor unauthorized access attempts for anomaly detection

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-285: Improper Authorization
* CWE-284: Improper Access Control

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP API Top 10:2023 – API5:2023 Broken Function Level Authorization*
The API allows users to perform unauthorized actions (updating profile settings) across roles due to missing function-level access control.
* *OWASP Top 10:2025 – A01 Broken Access Control*
Access control enforcement is missing at the backend, allowing unauthorized cross-role actions beyond intended permissions.

Summary: Unauthorized Ledger Access After Access Revocation
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Unauthorized Ledger Access After Access Revocation

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.6
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Ledger Access Control API

*Affected Path:*

* {{GET /api/v3/ledgers/loaders/{id}}}
* {{GET /api/v3/ledgers/truckers/{id}}}

----

h2. Description

The application allows loader/trucker users to access ledger data even after an administrator has explicitly disabled their ledger access. The access control mechanism does not enforce updated authorization policies in real-time, and previously issued access tokens continue to grant access to restricted resources.

An attacker or restricted user can exploit this by using a valid access token obtained prior to access revocation to continue fetching sensitive ledger data via the affected endpoints. The application lacks proper server-side authorization checks tied to the current user permissions and does not invalidate or re-evaluate access tokens against updated privilege states.

----

h2. Impact

* Unauthorized access to sensitive financial/ledger data
* Violation of role-based access control policies
* Potential data leakage between loaders/truckers and restricted users
* Compliance and audit risks due to ineffective access revocation
* Loss of administrative control over user permissions

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in as a loader/trucker user and obtain a valid access token
# Access ledger data using:
{noformat}GET /api/v3/ledgers/loaders/{id}
{noformat}
or
{noformat}GET /api/v3/ledgers/truckers/{id}
{noformat}
# As an admin, disable ledger access for the same user
# Without re-authenticating, reuse the previously issued access token
# Send the same API request again with:
{noformat}Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
{noformat}
# Observe that ledger data is still returned despite access being revoked

----

h2. Root Cause

The backend does not enforce dynamic authorization checks based on current user permissions and continues to honor previously issued access tokens without validating updated access control states.

----

h2. Solution

* Enforce *real-time authorization checks* on every request based on current user permissions
* Implement *token invalidation or revocation* upon privilege changes
* Use *centralized access control (RBAC/ABAC)* to validate permissions dynamically
* Include *permission versioning or claims validation* in tokens to detect stale privileges
* Force *re-authentication or token refresh* after critical permission changes
* Log and monitor access attempts post-permission revocation

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-284: Improper Access Control
* CWE-285: Improper Authorization

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP API Top 10:2023 – API1:2023 Broken Object Level Authorization*
The application fails to enforce object-level access control, allowing users to access ledger data even after permissions are revoked.
* *OWASP Top 10:2025 – A01 Broken Access Control*
Access restrictions are not properly enforced after administrative changes, resulting in unauthorized data access.

Summary: Vulnerability: Access Tokens Remain Active After Logout
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Access Tokens Remain Active After Logout

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.2
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Authentication and Session Management (Web Panel)

*Affected Path:*

* {{/api/auth/logout}}
* {{/api/*}} (all authenticated endpoints relying on access tokens)
* Token validation mechanism

----

h2. Description

The application does not invalidate access tokens upon user logout, allowing previously issued tokens to remain valid and usable even after the session is terminated from the client side. The logout functionality only removes tokens from the client but does not revoke them on the server.

An attacker who has obtained a valid access token (via XSS, network interception, or local access) can continue to use the token to access protected API endpoints even after the legitimate user logs out. The application lacks server-side token invalidation, revocation mechanisms, or token blacklisting, resulting in persistent session access.

----

h2. Impact

* Persistent unauthorized access even after user logout
* Session hijacking with prolonged validity of stolen tokens
* Inability to enforce proper session termination
* Increased risk in shared or public device scenarios
* Potential regulatory and compliance violations due to weak session management

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the web panel and capture the access token (e.g., via browser DevTools or intercepting proxy)
# Use the application normally and then log out
# After logout, send a request to a protected endpoint using the same token:
{noformat}GET /api/user/profile
Authorization: Bearer <captured_access_token>{noformat}
# Observe that the request is still successfully processed
# Confirm that no token invalidation or revocation has occurred on the server

----

h2. Root Cause

The backend does not implement token invalidation or revocation mechanisms during logout, and access tokens remain valid until their natural expiration without server-side enforcement of session termination.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement *server-side token invalidation* on logout (e.g., token blacklist or revocation store)
* Use *short-lived access tokens* with strict expiration
* Implement *refresh token rotation and revocation*
* Maintain *token identifiers (jti)* to track and revoke tokens
* Invalidate all active sessions upon logout if required (global logout support)
* Ensure *logout endpoint actively communicates with authentication service* to revoke tokens

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration
* CWE-287: Improper Authentication

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP API Top 10:2023 – API2:2023 Broken Authentication*
 Failure to invalidate tokens after logout allows continued unauthorized access using previously issued credentials.
* *OWASP Top 10:2025 – A07 Authentication Failures*
 Improper session termination and token lifecycle management lead to authentication weaknesses.

Summary: Lack of Input Restrictions Leading to UI Instability
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Lack of Input Restrictions Leading to UI Instability

*Severity:* Low
*CVSS Score:* 3.7
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Client-Side Input Handling (Pricing, Service Name, and Description Fields)

*Affected Path:*

* Product/Service pricing input fields
* Service name field
* Description field
* Any UI component accepting uncontrolled user input

----

h2. Description

The application does not enforce proper input validation for pricing, service name, and description fields, including minimum and maximum length restrictions, acceptable value ranges, and character constraints. Users are able to enter excessively large values, negative numbers, or unsupported special characters, which the UI is not designed to handle.

An attacker or user can exploit this by submitting malformed or extreme inputs, causing UI rendering issues, crashes, or inconsistent behavior. The application lacks both frontend and backend validation controls and does not restrict unnecessary special characters in fields like service name and description, leading to improper handling of edge-case and potentially unsafe inputs.

----

h2. Impact

* UI crashes or rendering failures affecting usability
* Inconsistent or malformed data stored in the system
* Potential injection vectors if special characters are not properly handled
* Disruption of business workflows (e.g., incorrect pricing or display issues)
* Increased operational overhead due to unstable application behavior

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Navigate to a form containing pricing, service name, and description fields
# Enter abnormal values such as:
#* Extremely large numbers (e.g., {{999999999999999999999}}) in pricing
#* Negative values (e.g., {{-1000}}) in pricing
#* Long strings (e.g., 10,000+ characters) in service name/description
#* Special characters (e.g., {{<script>alert(1)</script>}}, {{@@@###$$$}}) in name/description
# Submit the form or trigger UI rendering
# Observe UI misalignment, broken components, or unexpected behavior

----

h2. Root Cause

The application does not enforce strict validation rules (length, numeric bounds, and character restrictions) at both frontend and backend levels, and lacks proper filtering of unnecessary special characters for specific input fields.

----

h2. Solution

* Enforce *min/max length validation* for service name and description fields
* Apply *numeric boundaries* for pricing (e.g., non-negative, upper limits)
* Implement *character whitelisting*:
** Service name: allow alphanumeric and limited safe characters (e.g., space, hyphen)
** Description: allow controlled set of characters based on business need
* Block or sanitize *unnecessary special characters* from both frontend and backend
* Implement *server-side validation as mandatory*, not relying only on frontend
* Add *graceful error handling* to prevent UI crashes
* Use *input validation libraries or schemas* (e.g., regex, validation frameworks)

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-20: Improper Input Validation

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP API Top 10:2023 – API8:2023 Security Misconfiguration*
Missing validation and filtering controls indicate improper configuration of input handling mechanisms.
* *OWASP Top 10:2025 – A06 Insecure Design*
The absence of defined input constraints and character handling reflects insecure design that fails to account for edge cases and input sanitization.

Summary: Storage of Access and Refresh Tokens in Local Storage
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Storage of Access and Refresh Tokens in Local Storage

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 7.5
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Client-Side Authentication Mechanism

*Affected Path:*

* Browser Local Storage ({{localStorage}})
* All authenticated application routes relying on stored tokens

----

h2. Description

The application stores sensitive authentication tokens, including access tokens and refresh tokens, in the browser's local storage. These tokens are accessible via JavaScript and persist even after browser sessions, making them vulnerable to unauthorized access.

An attacker can exploit this issue by leveraging Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) or malicious third-party scripts to read tokens directly from local storage. The application lacks secure storage mechanisms such as HttpOnly cookies or token binding, allowing attackers to extract and reuse tokens to impersonate users.

----

h2. Impact

* Unauthorized account takeover through token theft
* Persistent session hijacking due to long-lived refresh tokens
* Bypass of authentication mechanisms without requiring credentials
* Increased risk of large-scale compromise if XSS exists
* Potential compliance violations (e.g., GDPR, PCI DSS) due to improper handling of sensitive session data

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application using valid credentials
# Open browser Developer Tools → Application tab
# Navigate to Local Storage
# Observe stored {{access_token}} and {{refresh_token}} values
# Execute the following in console (simulating XSS):
{noformat}localStorage.getItem('access_token');
localStorage.getItem('refresh_token');{noformat}
# Copy the token and use it in an Authorization header:
{noformat}Authorization: Bearer <access_token>{noformat}
# Access protected API endpoints successfully without re-authentication

----

h2. Root Cause

Authentication tokens are stored in browser local storage, which is accessible via JavaScript and lacks protection mechanisms such as HttpOnly, Secure flags, or proper token scoping.

----

h2. Solution

* Store tokens in *HttpOnly, Secure cookies* instead of local storage
* Implement *SameSite=strict/lax* cookie attributes to mitigate CSRF
* Avoid storing refresh tokens on the client side where possible
* Implement *short-lived access tokens* with proper rotation
* Apply *Content Security Policy (CSP)* to reduce XSS attack surface
* Sanitize and validate all user inputs to prevent XSS
* Use *token binding or device/session-based validation*

----

h2. CWE-ID

* CWE-922: Insecure Storage of Sensitive Information
* CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (XSS)

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP API Top 10:2023 – API2:2023 Broken Authentication*
Tokens stored insecurely can be stolen and reused, leading to authentication bypass.
* *OWASP Top 10:2025 – A02 Security Misconfiguration*
Improper client-side storage of sensitive tokens reflects insecure configuration of authentication handling mechanisms.

Summary: Insufficient Rate Limiting on Unauthenticated and Sensitive Endpoints
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Insufficient Rate Limiting on Unauthenticated and Sensitive Endpoints

*Severity:* High  
*CVSS Score:* 7.6
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Authentication and Sensitive APIs

*Affected Path:*  
All unauthenticated API endpoints and sensitive authenticated endpoints (e.g., change-password, payment APIs)

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application enforces insufficient rate limiting on unauthenticated and sensitive endpoints. The current configuration allows approximately 100 requests per minute, which is higher than recommended thresholds for endpoints handling authentication, account management, and financial operations.

Sensitive endpoints such as sign-in, forgot password, and change-password, as well as payment-related APIs, are exposed without strict throttling controls. This allows an attacker to send a large number of requests within a short period without effective restriction. Although rate limiting is present, the threshold is too permissive to prevent abuse scenarios effectively.

Due to this configuration, attackers can perform automated attacks such as brute-force attempts, credential stuffing, or abuse of sensitive workflows with reduced resistance from the system.

----

h2. Impact

* *Brute Force Risk:* Attackers can attempt multiple password combinations rapidly.
* *Credential Stuffing Risk:* High request limits facilitate automated login attempts using leaked credentials.
* *Abuse of Sensitive Operations:* Attackers can repeatedly trigger password changes or payment-related actions.
* *Increased Attack Surface:* Weak rate limits reduce the effectiveness of other security controls.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Identify an unauthenticated or sensitive endpoint (e.g., login or change-password).
# Use a tool such as Burp Suite Intruder or a script to send multiple requests.
# Send requests at a rate approaching or exceeding 100 requests per minute.
# Observe that the application continues to process requests without effective throttling or blocking.
# Verify that no adaptive controls such as CAPTCHA, delay, or account lockout are triggered.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Overly permissive rate-limiting thresholds.
* Lack of endpoint-specific rate-limiting policies.
* Absence of adaptive protections such as CAPTCHA or progressive delays.

----

h2. Solution

* Reduce rate limits for sensitive endpoints (e.g., 5–10 requests per minute per user/IP).
* Apply stricter rate limits on unauthenticated endpoints.
* Implement adaptive controls such as CAPTCHA after repeated attempts.
* Introduce account lockout or exponential backoff mechanisms.
* Monitor and log abnormal request patterns for detection and response.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-770:* Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API4:2023 – Unrestricted Resource Consumption*

The application allows excessive request rates on sensitive endpoints, increasing the risk of abuse and automated attacks.

*A04 – Insecure Design*

*Justification:*  
The system implements rate limiting but with thresholds that are insufficient to effectively mitigate abuse of authentication and sensitive workflows.

Summary: Lack of Password History Enforcement Allows Reuse of Previous Passwords
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Lack of Password History Enforcement Allows Reuse of Previous Passwords

*Severity:* Medium  
*CVSS Score:* 6.5  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Forgot Password / Reset Password APIs

*Affected Path:*  
FE: /forgot-password ; /reset-password?token={token}  
BE: POST /api/v1/auth/forgot-password ; POST /api/v1/auth/reset-password

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application does not enforce password history restrictions during the password reset process. When initiating the forgot password workflow and resetting the password, users are allowed to reuse their current or previously used passwords without any validation checks.

During testing, a password reset request was triggered and the existing password was reused successfully. The application accepted the password without returning any validation error. Ideally, the system should prevent users from reusing recently used passwords (e.g., last 5 passwords) to maintain strong password hygiene.

Due to the absence of password history enforcement, users can repeatedly reuse the same password, reducing the effectiveness of password reset mechanisms and weakening overall account security.

----

h2. Impact

* *Weak Password Security:* Users can reuse previously compromised or weak passwords.
* *Reduced Effectiveness of Password Reset:* Resetting passwords does not improve account security if the same password is reused.
* *Increased Risk of Account Compromise:* Attackers can continue using previously known credentials.
* *Non-Compliance with Security Standards:* Lack of password history enforcement may violate organizational or regulatory security requirements.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Navigate to the forgot password functionality:

          /forgot-password

# Request a password reset link for a valid user account.
# Use the reset link to access:

         /reset-password?token={token}

# Enter the same password currently associated with the account.
# Submit the request to reset the password.
# Observe that the application accepts the password and resets it successfully.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Absence of password history validation during password reset.
* Lack of enforcement of password reuse policies.
* Missing backend controls to compare new passwords against previously used passwords.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement password history enforcement (e.g., prevent reuse of last 5 passwords).
* Store password history securely using hashed values.
* Validate new passwords against stored password history during reset.
* Enforce strong password policies (length, complexity, uniqueness).
* Provide clear error messages when users attempt to reuse old passwords.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-521:* Weak Password Requirements

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*

The application fails to enforce strong authentication controls by allowing reuse of previously used passwords.

*A07 – Identification and Authentication Failures*

*Justification:*  
The system does not enforce password reuse restrictions, weakening authentication security and increasing the likelihood of account compromise.

Summary: Previous Password Reset Tokens Remain Valid After Issuance of New Token
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Previous Password Reset Tokens Remain Valid After Issuance of New Token

*Severity:* Medium  
*CVSS Score:* 6.9  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Forgot Password / Reset Password APIs

*Affected Path:*  
FE: /forgot-password ; /reset-password?token={token}  
BE: POST /api/v1/auth/forgot-password ; POST /api/v1/auth/reset-password

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application does not invalidate previously issued password reset tokens when a new reset link is generated. When a user initiates multiple forgot password requests, the system issues multiple valid reset tokens without revoking the earlier ones.

During testing, after triggering the forgot password functionality twice for the same user, both reset links remained valid. The older reset link was successfully used to reset the user’s password even after a newer token had been generated. This indicates that the application does not enforce single-use or latest-token-only validation for password reset operations.

Due to this behavior, multiple valid reset tokens can coexist for a single user account, increasing the attack surface and weakening the security of the password reset mechanism.

----

h2. Impact

* *Unauthorized Password Reset Risk:* Previously issued tokens can be reused to reset passwords.
* *Extended Attack Window:* Older tokens remain valid even after new reset requests are initiated.
* *Account Takeover Risk:* If an attacker gains access to an older reset link, they can reset the password despite subsequent user actions.
* *Weak Token Lifecycle Management:* Failure to invalidate older tokens reduces the effectiveness of password reset controls.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Navigate to the forgot password functionality:

         /forgot-password

# Submit a password reset request for a valid user account.
# Capture the reset link received (Token 1).
# Trigger the forgot password functionality again for the same user.
# Capture the new reset link (Token 2).
# Use the first reset link (Token 1) to reset the password:

         /reset-password?token={Token1}

# Observe that the password reset is successful despite a newer token being issued.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Absence of token invalidation logic when generating new password reset tokens.
* Lack of enforcement of single-use or latest-token-only validation.
* Improper token lifecycle management for password reset workflows.

----

h2. Solution

* Invalidate all previously issued password reset tokens when a new token is generated.
* Enforce single-use tokens that become invalid immediately after successful password reset.
* Implement short expiration times for reset tokens.
* Store token state securely and validate against the latest issued token.
* Log and monitor multiple reset attempts for anomaly detection.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-613:* Insufficient Session Expiration

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*

The application fails to properly manage password reset tokens, allowing multiple valid tokens to exist simultaneously and enabling unauthorized password reset attempts.

*A07 – Identification and Authentication Failures*

*Justification:*  
The system does not properly manage the lifecycle of authentication tokens, allowing reuse of previously issued reset tokens and increasing the risk of account compromise.

Summary: Username Enumeration via Forgot Password Functionality
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Username Enumeration via Forgot Password Functionality

*Severity:* Low  
*CVSS Score:* 3.7
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Forgot Password (Frontend & Backend)

*Affected Path:*  
FE: /forgot-password  
BE: /api/v1/auth/forgot-password

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the forgot password functionality exposes a discrepancy in responses based on whether the provided user identifier exists in the system. When a password reset request is initiated using a valid (registered) user account, the application responds with a success message or status. In contrast, when the same request is made using an unregistered or invalid user identifier, the application returns an error response.

This variation in behavior allows an attacker to distinguish between valid and invalid user accounts. By automating requests to the forgot password endpoint, an attacker can enumerate registered users without authentication.

----

h2. Impact

* *User Enumeration:* Attackers can identify valid user accounts within the system.
* *Targeted Attacks:* Enumerated accounts can be leveraged for brute-force or credential stuffing attacks.
* *Privacy Risk:* Exposure of valid users may lead to phishing or social engineering attacks.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Navigate to the forgot password page:              

               /forgot-password

# Submit a password reset request using a valid registered user identifier.
# Observe that the application returns a success response.
# Submit the same request using an invalid or unregistered user identifier.
# Observe that the application returns an error response.
# Compare both responses and confirm that the discrepancy reveals user existence.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Inconsistent response handling for valid and invalid user identifiers.
* Exposure of account existence through distinguishable server responses.
* Lack of generic messaging in authentication-related workflows.

----

h2. Solution

* Return a consistent response for both valid and invalid user identifiers (e.g., generic success message).
* Avoid disclosing whether a user account exists in the system.
* Implement rate limiting on forgot password endpoints to prevent automated enumeration.
* Monitor and log repeated password reset attempts for anomaly detection.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-203:* Observable Discrepancy

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*

The application exposes differences in authentication-related responses, allowing attackers to enumerate valid user accounts.

*A07 – Identification and Authentication Failures*

*Justification:*  
The system reveals differences in behavior based on account validity, enabling attackers to identify registered users and facilitate further authentication attacks.

Summary: Improper Cache Control Allows Access to Authenticated Pages via Browser Back Navigation After Logout
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Improper Cache Control Allows Access to Authenticated Pages via Browser Back Navigation After Logout

*Severity:* Medium  
*CVSS Score:* 6.8  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Web Application (All Authenticated Pages including Payment Modules)

*Affected Path:*  
All authenticated UI pages accessible post-login (including payment gateway integration pages)

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application does not properly prevent caching of sensitive authenticated pages. After logging out from the application, previously visited pages remain accessible through browser back navigation. When the user navigates back using the browser’s back button, the application displays cached versions of authenticated pages, including sensitive sections such as payment gateway integration.

The application does not enforce appropriate cache control headers or session validation checks on page load. As a result, the browser renders previously stored content without revalidating the session with the server. Although the backend APIs may require authentication, the exposure of sensitive UI data through cached pages may lead to unintended information disclosure.

----

h2. Impact

* *Exposure of Sensitive Information:* Previously accessed authenticated content can be viewed after logout.
* *Privacy Risk:* Sensitive data related to user accounts or financial workflows may be exposed to unauthorized users on shared systems.
* *Session Termination Weakness:* Logout does not fully terminate access to previously viewed content.
* *Payment Data Exposure Risk:* Cached payment-related pages may reveal sensitive transaction or integration details.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application using a valid user account.
# Navigate to sensitive sections such as user dashboards or payment-related pages.
# Log out of the application.
# Use the browser back button to navigate to previously visited pages.
# Observe that the pages are displayed without requiring re-authentication.
# Verify that sensitive information is visible in the cached UI.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Missing or improperly configured cache-control headers for sensitive pages.
* Lack of session validation checks when rendering cached pages.
* Browser caching of authenticated content due to absence of no-store/no-cache directives.

----

h2. Solution

* Set strict cache-control headers for all authenticated pages:
** {{Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache, must-revalidate}}
** {{Pragma: no-cache}}
** {{Expires: 0}}
* Ensure that all sensitive pages validate the user session before rendering content.
* Implement frontend logic to prevent rendering of cached pages after logout.
* Redirect users to the login page when attempting to access protected content post-logout.
* Perform security testing to ensure no sensitive data is cached by the browser.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-525:* Information Exposure Through Browser Caching

----

h2. OWASP Category

*A02 – Security Misconfiguration*

*Justification:*  
The application fails to configure proper cache control mechanisms, allowing sensitive authenticated content to be accessible after logout via browser caching.

Summary: Missing and Misconfigured Security Headers Across Web, API, and Admin Interfaces
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Missing and Misconfigured Security Headers Across Web, API, and Admin Interfaces

*Severity:* Medium  
*CVSS Score:* 5.8  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Web Frontend, Backend APIs, Admin Frontend

*Affected Path:*  
All application responses across Web, API, and Admin interfaces

----

h2. Description

It was observed that multiple critical HTTP security headers are either missing or inconsistently configured across different components of the application, including the Web Frontend, Backend APIs, and Admin Frontend. Security headers play a crucial role in protecting applications against common web-based attacks such as cross-site scripting (XSS), clickjacking, MIME-type sniffing, and data leakage.

During testing, it was identified that the Web Frontend does not implement any of the recommended security headers. The Backend API has only partial coverage with limited headers configured, while the Admin Frontend has a subset of headers implemented but still lacks several critical protections. The absence of headers such as Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, and Permissions-Policy significantly weakens the application's defense-in-depth strategy.

Summary of Observations:

* *Web Frontend:* 0/8 security headers configured (all required headers missing)
* *Backend API:* 2/6 headers configured (Cache-Control, Access-Control-Allow-Origin)
* *Admin Frontend:* 4/8 headers configured (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, Cache-Control)

----

h2. Impact

* *Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Risk:* Absence of Content-Security-Policy increases exploitability of XSS vulnerabilities.
* *Clickjacking Attacks:* Missing X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors can allow UI redressing attacks.
* *Data Leakage:* Lack of Referrer-Policy may expose sensitive URLs and parameters.
* *MIME Sniffing Attacks:* Missing X-Content-Type-Options may allow browsers to interpret files incorrectly.
* *Transport Security Weakness:* Absence of Strict-Transport-Security may expose users to downgrade or MITM attacks.
* *Reduced Defense-in-Depth:* Missing headers weaken multiple layers of browser-enforced security controls.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Intercept HTTP responses from Web, API, and Admin endpoints using a proxy tool.
# Inspect response headers for each component.
# Verify the absence or misconfiguration of the following headers:
#* Strict-Transport-Security
#* Content-Security-Policy
#* X-Content-Type-Options
#* X-Frame-Options
#* Referrer-Policy
#* Permissions-Policy
#* Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy
#* Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy
#* Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy
# Compare the observed headers with secure configuration standards.
# Confirm inconsistent implementation across application components.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Lack of standardized security header policy across application layers.
* Incomplete or inconsistent security configuration in frontend and backend components.
* Absence of centralized middleware or gateway enforcing security headers.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement a standardized set of security headers across all application components.
* Enforce headers at a centralized layer (e.g., API gateway, reverse proxy, or middleware).
* Recommended headers to configure:
** Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS)
** Content-Security-Policy (CSP)
** X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
** X-Frame-Options: DENY or SAMEORIGIN
** Referrer-Policy
** Permissions-Policy
** Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy
** Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy
** Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy
* Regularly audit security headers using automated tools or security testing.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-693:* Protection Mechanism Failure

----

h2. OWASP Category

*A05 – Security Misconfiguration*

*Justification:*  
The application lacks proper security header configurations across multiple components, resulting in weakened browser-level protections and increased exposure to common web-based attacks.

Summary: Access Tokens Remain Valid After Logout Leading to Session Hijacking Risk
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Access Tokens Remain Valid After Logout Leading to Session Hijacking Risk

*Severity:* High  
*CVSS Score:* 8.1  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* All Authenticated APIs (Web and Admin)

*Affected Path:*  
All API endpoints requiring authentication

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application does not invalidate access tokens upon user logout. After a user logs out of the application, the previously issued access token continues to remain valid and can still be used to access authenticated API endpoints.

The backend does not implement token revocation or session invalidation mechanisms during the logout process. As a result, any access token obtained prior to logout can be reused until its natural expiration, even though the user has explicitly terminated the session. This indicates that logout functionality is handled only at the client or UI level without enforcing session invalidation at the server side.

----

h2. Impact

* *Session Hijacking Risk:* Stolen or intercepted tokens can be reused even after logout.
* *Unauthorized Access Persistence:* Attackers can maintain access to user accounts despite logout actions.
* *Violation of Expected Security Behavior:* Logout does not effectively terminate authenticated sessions.
* *Increased Attack Window:* The validity of tokens beyond logout increases exposure to replay attacks.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application and capture the access token used for authenticated requests.
# Perform logout from the application.
# Use the previously captured access token to send a request to any authenticated API endpoint.
# Observe that the request is processed successfully despite the user being logged out.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Absence of server-side token revocation or invalidation mechanism during logout.
* Stateless authentication design without compensating controls such as token blacklisting or short-lived tokens.
* Logout functionality implemented only at the client side without backend enforcement.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement server-side token invalidation mechanisms during logout (e.g., token blacklisting or revocation lists).
* Use short-lived access tokens combined with refresh tokens.
* Invalidate refresh tokens upon logout to prevent token re-issuance.
* Maintain a session store or token registry to track active sessions.
* Enforce re-authentication for sensitive operations.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-613:* Insufficient Session Expiration

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*

The application fails to properly manage session termination, allowing continued use of access tokens after logout.

*A07 – Identification and Authentication Failures*

*Justification:*  
The system does not properly invalidate authentication tokens upon logout, allowing unauthorized continued access using previously issued credentials.

Summary: Sensitive User Data Stored in Browser Local Storage Leading to Data Exposure Risk
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Sensitive User Data Stored in Browser Local Storage Leading to Data Exposure Risk

*Severity:* High  
*CVSS Score:* 7.9  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Client-Side Storage Mechanism (Web Application)

*Affected Path:*  
Browser Local Storage (all application modules storing user details)

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application stores sensitive user information such as first name, last name, contact details, and other personal data in the browser's local storage. Local storage is accessible via client-side scripts and persists even after the browser session is closed.

The application does not implement any encryption or secure handling mechanisms for the stored data. As a result, any script executing within the application context, including potentially malicious scripts introduced through vulnerabilities such as cross-site scripting (XSS), can access and exfiltrate this sensitive information. Additionally, local storage data can be accessed by other users on the same machine if proper system-level protections are not in place.

----

h2. Impact

* *Sensitive Data Exposure:* Personal user information can be accessed directly from the browser storage.
* *Increased Risk via XSS:* Any successful XSS attack can lead to immediate exfiltration of stored sensitive data.
* *Persistence Risk:* Data remains stored even after logout or session termination.
* *Privacy Violation:* Unauthorized access to personally identifiable information (PII) may lead to compliance issues.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application using a valid user account.
# Open the browser developer tools.
# Navigate to the "Application" tab and access the Local Storage section.
# Observe that sensitive user data such as name, contact details, and other information is stored in plaintext.
# Verify that the data persists even after logout or browser restart.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Storage of sensitive information in client-side local storage without encryption.
* Lack of secure data handling practices for browser storage mechanisms.
* Failure to restrict sensitive data exposure to secure, server-side storage.

----

h2. Solution

* Avoid storing sensitive user data in browser local storage.
* Use secure, HTTP-only cookies for session-related information where applicable.
* If client-side storage is required, store only non-sensitive data.
* Implement encryption for any data that must be stored on the client side.
* Regularly clear sensitive data from storage upon logout or session termination.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-922:* Insecure Storage of Sensitive Information

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API3:2023 – Broken Object Property Level Authorization*

Excessive exposure and improper handling of sensitive user attributes increase the risk of unauthorized access to personal data.

*A02 – Security Misconfiguration*

*Justification:*  
The application improperly stores sensitive user data in an insecure client-side storage mechanism without appropriate safeguards, increasing the risk of unauthorized access and data exposure.

Summary: Username Enumeration via Forgot Password Functionality
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Username Enumeration via Forgot Password Functionality

*Severity:* Low  
*CVSS Score:* 5.3  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Forgot Password API

*Affected Path:*  
PUT /api/v1/users/auth/forgot-password/link

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the forgot password functionality exposes a discrepancy in server responses based on whether a user account exists in the system. When a password reset request is submitted for a valid (registered) user, the application responds with a {{200 OK}} status. However, when the request is made using an unregistered or invalid user identifier, the application responds with a {{400 Bad Request}}.

This difference in response behavior allows an attacker to determine whether a given username or account exists in the system. By automating such requests, an attacker can enumerate valid user accounts without authentication.

----

h2. Impact

* *User Enumeration:* Attackers can identify valid user accounts in the system.
* *Increased Attack Surface:* Enumerated accounts can be targeted for brute-force or credential stuffing attacks.
* *Privacy Concern:* Exposure of registered users may lead to targeted phishing or social engineering attacks.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Navigate to the forgot password functionality.
# Send a password reset request using a valid registered user identifier:

            PUT /api/v1/users/auth/forgot-password/link

# Observe that the response returns {{200 OK}}.
# Send the same request using an unregistered or invalid user identifier.
# Observe that the response returns {{400 Bad Request}}.
# Compare both responses and confirm that the discrepancy reveals user existence.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Application returns different HTTP status codes based on user existence.
* Lack of uniform response handling for authentication-related endpoints.
* Absence of controls to prevent user enumeration.

----

h2. Solution

* Return a consistent response for both valid and invalid user identifiers (e.g., generic success message).
* Avoid revealing whether a user account exists in the system.
* Implement rate limiting on forgot password requests to prevent automated enumeration.
* Log and monitor repeated password reset attempts for anomaly detection.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-203:* Observable Discrepancy

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*

The application exposes user enumeration through inconsistent authentication-related responses, enabling attackers to identify valid accounts.

*A07 – Identification and Authentication Failures*

*Justification:*  
The system reveals differences in response behavior based on account validity, which can be leveraged to enumerate users and facilitate further authentication attacks.

Summary: Excessive Rate Limits on Sensitive and Unauthenticated Endpoints Enable Abuse
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Excessive Rate Limits on Sensitive and Unauthenticated Endpoints Enable Abuse

*Severity:* High  
*CVSS Score:* 8.0  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Authentication and Sensitive APIs

*Affected Path:*  
Sign-up, Sign-in, Forgot Password, Change Password, Payment-related APIs (e.g., /api/v1/users/auth/*, payment endpoints)

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application enforces overly permissive rate limits on sensitive and unauthenticated endpoints. The current configuration allows approximately 100 requests per minute globally, which is significantly higher than secure baseline expectations for endpoints handling authentication, account recovery, and financial operations.

Sensitive endpoints such as sign-up, sign-in, forgot password, change password, and payment-related APIs are exposed without adequate throttling controls. This allows an attacker to send a high volume of automated requests within a short time frame without being effectively restricted. The lack of strict rate limiting reduces the effectiveness of protections against brute-force attacks, credential stuffing, enumeration, and abuse of financial operations.

----

h2. Impact

* *Brute Force and Credential Stuffing:* High request limits enable rapid password guessing attempts.
* *User Enumeration Amplification:* Weak rate limits can be combined with enumeration flaws to discover valid accounts at scale.
* *Abuse of Sensitive Workflows:* Attackers can repeatedly trigger password reset, signup, or payment flows.
* *Financial Risk:* Payment-related endpoints may be abused for transaction manipulation or denial of service scenarios.
* *Resource Exhaustion:* Excessive requests may impact system performance and availability.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Identify a sensitive or unauthenticated endpoint (e.g., login or forgot password).
# Use a tool such as Burp Suite Intruder or a script to send multiple requests.
# Send requests at a rate approaching or exceeding 100 requests per minute.
# Observe that the application continues to process requests without blocking, throttling, or enforcing strict limits.
# Verify that no effective account lockout, CAPTCHA, or progressive delay mechanism is triggered.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Misconfigured rate-limiting thresholds for sensitive and unauthenticated endpoints.
* Lack of endpoint-specific throttling policies.
* Absence of adaptive controls such as IP-based rate limiting, CAPTCHA, or account lockout mechanisms.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement strict rate limits for sensitive endpoints (e.g., 5–10 requests per minute per user/IP).
* Apply different rate-limiting policies based on endpoint sensitivity.
* Introduce progressive delays or temporary account lockouts after repeated failed attempts.
* Implement CAPTCHA or challenge-response mechanisms for unauthenticated endpoints.
* Monitor and log abnormal request patterns for detection and response.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-770:* Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API4:2023 – Unrestricted Resource Consumption*

The application allows excessive request rates to sensitive endpoints, enabling abuse of resources and increasing the risk of brute-force and automated attacks.

*A04 – Insecure Design*

*Justification:*  
The system lacks appropriate rate-limiting controls for sensitive operations, allowing attackers to abuse authentication and financial workflows at scale.

Summary: Broken Business Logic Allows Modification of Approved Grievances by Partner and Customer
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Broken Business Logic Allows Modification of Approved Grievances by Partner and Customer

*Severity:* Critical  
*CVSS Score:* 9.0  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Grievance Management APIs

*Affected Path:*  
PUT /api/v1/partner/policy-cases/p_case_01{id}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the grievance management workflow does not enforce proper state validation after administrative approval of a grievance. According to the intended business logic, once a grievance is reviewed and approved by the administrator, the record should become immutable to ensure the integrity of the finalized decision.

However, during testing it was identified that partner and customer accounts are still able to modify grievance details even after the grievance has been approved by the administrator. By sending modification requests to the grievance update endpoint with the approved grievance identifier, the application processes the request successfully without validating the current state of the grievance.

Due to the absence of backend state validation and workflow enforcement, users are able to alter grievance details after approval, which compromises the integrity and reliability of the grievance resolution process.

----

h2. Impact

* *Integrity Violation of Approved Records:* Approved grievances can be modified after administrative approval.
* *Financial and Regulatory Risk:* In fintech systems, grievance records may be tied to financial disputes, compliance workflows, or regulatory reporting.
* *Audit Trail Manipulation:* Users may alter grievance details post-approval, potentially manipulating historical records.
* *Trust and Compliance Impact:* Unauthorized modification of finalized grievance decisions may undermine auditability and regulatory compliance.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application as a partner or customer user.
# Create or interact with a grievance that is pending approval.
# Allow the grievance to be reviewed and approved by an administrator.
# Capture the request used to update grievance details.
# Send a modification request to the grievance update endpoint using the approved grievance identifier: PUT /api/v1/partner/policy-cases/p_case_01{id}

# Observe that the grievance details are updated successfully even though the grievance status is already approved.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Missing server-side validation of grievance workflow state after administrative approval.
* Lack of enforcement of immutable state for finalized grievance records.
* Absence of backend business logic checks restricting modifications after approval.

----

h2. Solution

* Enforce strict workflow state validation for grievance records.
* Prevent modification of grievances once the status is marked as *Approved* or *Finalized*.
* Implement backend validation to ensure update operations are allowed only in permitted workflow states.
* Maintain tamper-resistant audit logs for grievance lifecycle events.
* Implement role-based restrictions and immutable record enforcement for regulatory and financial workflows.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-285:* Improper Authorization  
* *CWE-840:* Business Logic Errors

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API6:2023 – Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows*

The application allows users to manipulate a sensitive business process (approved grievance workflow) without enforcing the intended lifecycle restrictions.

*A04 – Insecure Design*

*Justification:*  
The system fails to enforce proper workflow and state validation for grievance processing, allowing unauthorized modification of finalized records and violating the integrity requirements expected in financial systems.

Summary: Broken Object Level Authorization Allows Clients to Access and Modify Other Clients’ Store Details
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Broken Object Level Authorization Allows Clients to Access and Modify Other Clients’ Store Details

*Severity:* High  
*CVSS Score:* 8.2  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Store Management API

*Affected Path:*  
GET     /api/v1/stores/cs_01{ID}/info

PATCH     /api/v1/stores/cs_01{ID} 

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the store management APIs do not properly enforce authorization checks to ensure that clients can only access stores belonging to their own account. The application allows clients to retrieve and modify store details using the store identifier passed in the request path.

During testing, it was identified that if a client obtains or guesses the identifier of another client's store ({{cs_01{ID}}}), the backend processes the request without validating whether the store belongs to the authenticated client. As a result, a client can directly invoke the store information API to retrieve sensitive store details or update store information associated with another client.

This behavior indicates that the application relies solely on the identifier supplied in the request and fails to validate ownership of the resource before performing the requested operation.

----

h2. Impact

* *Cross-Client Data Exposure:* Clients can access store details belonging to other organizations.
* *Unauthorized Data Modification:* Attackers can modify store configuration or metadata of another client.
* *Tenant Isolation Failure:* The system fails to enforce proper separation between client resources.
* *Integrity Impact:* Unauthorized modifications may disrupt store operations or introduce incorrect data.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application using a valid client account.
# Intercept requests made to retrieve store details.
# Modify the store identifier in the request to another valid store ID.

Example request to retrieve store details:

GET /api/v1/stores/cs_01{ID}

# Observe that the application returns the store details associated with another client.

# Attempt to modify the store information using the update endpoint:

PATCH /api/v1/stores/cs_01{ID}

# Observe that the request is processed successfully and the store information is updated.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Missing server-side authorization validation for store ownership.
* The application relies on user-supplied identifiers without validating whether the resource belongs to the authenticated client.
* Lack of tenant-scoped access control enforcement in store management APIs.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement strict server-side ownership validation to ensure clients can only access stores belonging to their own account.
* Enforce tenant-scoped authorization checks before processing store retrieval or modification requests.
* Avoid relying solely on resource identifiers provided by the client.
* Implement centralized authorization middleware to consistently validate access permissions across APIs.
* Monitor and log cross-resource access attempts for anomaly detection.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control  
* *CWE-639:* Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API1:2023 – Broken Object Level Authorization*

The application allows a client to access and manipulate store resources belonging to other clients by directly referencing their identifiers.

*A01 – Broken Access Control*

*Justification:*  
The backend does not validate ownership of store resources before processing requests, allowing unauthorized clients to retrieve and modify data belonging to other tenants.

Summary: Broken Tenant Isolation Allows Tenant to Access and Modify Client Users
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Broken Tenant Isolation Allows Tenant to Access and Modify Client Users

*Severity:* High  
*CVSS Score:* 8.2  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* User Management APIs

*Affected Paths:*  
-/api/v1/users/tn_{ID}?page=1&limit=10  (added by mistake)-
/api/v1/users/u_01{ID}/info  
/api/v1/users/u_01{ID}/activate  
/api/v1/users/u_01{ID}/deactivate  
/api/v1/users/u_01{ID}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application does not properly enforce tenant-level authorization boundaries in the user management APIs. The application follows a hierarchical structure where tenants can manage their own clients and users, while clients can manage their own users within their respective scope.
