According to the intended business logic, tenants should not have the ability to perform CRUD operations on users belonging to client accounts, and similarly, clients should not have access to tenant users.

During testing it was identified that these restrictions are enforced at the frontend level but are not properly validated on the backend APIs. If a tenant obtains the {{user_id}} associated with a client user, it becomes possible to directly interact with the underlying APIs to retrieve and manipulate the client user's data. By invoking the user listing API, the tenant can enumerate users and subsequently perform operations such as retrieving user details, activating or deactivating the account, and updating user information using the corresponding API endpoints.

Due to the absence of proper tenant isolation and authorization validation on the backend, a tenant user is able to access and modify user accounts belonging to client tenants, violating the intended access control boundaries of the multi-tenant system.

----

h2. Impact

* *Tenant Isolation Bypass:* Tenants can access user accounts belonging to client organizations.
* *Unauthorized User Management:* Tenants can activate, deactivate, view, and modify client user accounts.
* *Data Confidentiality Violation:* Sensitive client user information can be accessed without authorization.
* *Integrity Impact:* Tenant users can modify account details of client users.
* *Multi-Tenant Security Failure:* Lack of proper tenant boundary enforcement compromises the security model of the platform.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application using a tenant account.
# Intercept requests made by the application while interacting with user management functionality.
# Trigger the user listing API to enumerate users:

GET /api/v1/users/tn_{ID}?page=1&limit=10

# Obtain a client user identifier (`u_01{ID}`).

# Use the obtained identifier to retrieve the user details:

GET /api/v1/users/u_01{ID}/info

# Observe that the client user details are returned successfully.

# Attempt to modify the status of the client user:

PATCH /api/v1/users/u_01{ID}/activate

PATCH /api/v1/users/u_01{ID}/deactivate

# Observe that the request is processed successfully.

# Modify the user details using:

PATCH /api/v1/users/u_01{ID}

10. Observe that the client user information is updated successfully despite the tenant not having permission to manage client users.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Missing backend authorization checks enforcing tenant-level access boundaries.
* Reliance on frontend restrictions instead of validating permissions at the API layer.
* Lack of proper validation to ensure that the requested user belongs to the same tenant scope as the authenticated user.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement strict server-side tenant isolation checks for all user management APIs.
* Validate that the authenticated user belongs to the same tenant context as the target user before performing any operation.
* Enforce role-based access control (RBAC) and tenant-scoped access validation for all CRUD operations on user resources.
* Avoid relying solely on frontend restrictions for enforcing security controls.
* Implement centralized authorization middleware to consistently enforce tenant boundaries across all APIs.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control  
* *CWE-639:* Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API1:2023 – Broken Object Level Authorization*

The application allows a tenant user to access and manipulate objects (client users) belonging to another tenant by directly referencing their identifiers.

*A01 – Broken Access Control*

*Justification:*  
The backend fails to enforce tenant-level authorization boundaries, allowing unauthorized users to access and modify resources belonging to other tenants within the system.

Summary: Improper Authorization Allows Users to Modify Their Own Profile by Manipulating User Identifier
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Improper Authorization Allows Users to Modify Their Own Profile by Manipulating User Identifier

*Severity:* Medium  
*CVSS Score:* 6.5  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* User Profile Management API

*Affected Path:*  
/api/v1/users/{userId}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application does not properly enforce authorization checks when processing profile update requests within the tenant-based user management functionality. According to the intended business logic, users are not permitted to modify their own profile details through the user management interface. Only users with appropriate permissions can edit details of other users within the same tenant.

However, during testing it was identified that the backend does not validate whether the user identifier being modified corresponds to the currently authenticated user. When a request used to edit another user’s profile was intercepted, replacing the target {{userId}} parameter with the identifier of the currently authenticated user allowed the request to be processed successfully.

Due to the absence of proper authorization validation, the application permits users to bypass the intended restriction and update their own profile details by manipulating the user identifier within the request.

----

h2. Impact

* *Business Logic Bypass:* Users can circumvent restrictions that prevent self-profile modification.
* *Improper Enforcement of Role Policies:* The application fails to enforce the intended permission model governing profile updates.
* *Integrity Impact:* Users can alter personal account information in ways that are intended to be restricted by the system.
* *Potential Abuse of Profile Controls:* If profile attributes include sensitive fields (e.g., display name, identifiers, or operational metadata), users may manipulate them contrary to business policy.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application using a valid user account.
# Navigate to the user management section where other users can be edited (if permitted by role).
# Intercept the request used to edit another user’s profile.
# Modify the {{userId}} parameter in the request to the identifier of the currently authenticated user.
# Send the modified request to the server.
# Observe that the application processes the request successfully and updates the currently logged-in user’s profile details.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Missing authorization validation to ensure that users cannot modify their own profile through restricted endpoints.
* Backend logic relies solely on the user identifier provided in the request without validating whether the operation violates business rules.
* Lack of enforcement of tenant role policies at the API layer.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement server-side authorization checks to enforce business rules preventing users from modifying their own profile through this endpoint.
* Validate that the target {{userId}} in the request does not match the identifier of the authenticated user when self-modification is restricted.
* Enforce role-based access control (RBAC) consistently for all profile modification operations.
* Implement centralized authorization logic to prevent similar issues across other user management APIs.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-285:* Improper Authorization  
* *CWE-639:* Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API5:2023 – Broken Function Level Authorization*

The application fails to enforce proper authorization checks when processing profile modification requests, allowing users to bypass business rules by manipulating user identifiers.

*A01 – Broken Access Control*

*Justification:*  
The application does not properly enforce access restrictions on user profile modification functionality, allowing users to bypass intended business restrictions by altering request parameters.

Summary: Race Condition in Resource Creation APIs Allows Bypass of Subscription Limits
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Race Condition in Resource Creation APIs Allows Bypass of Subscription Limits

*Severity:* Critical  
*CVSS Score:* 9.1  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Resource Creation APIs

*Affected Paths:*  
/api/v1/users  
/api/v1/vouchers/redeem  
All other resource creation APIs enforcing subscription-based limits

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application does not properly handle concurrent requests when enforcing subscription-based resource creation limits. The system relies on a check-then-create mechanism without implementing adequate locking or atomic validation. By sending multiple parallel requests simultaneously, the limit validation logic can be bypassed due to a race condition in the backend processing.

As a result, multiple resource creation requests are processed successfully before the system updates the quota counters associated with the subscription. This behavior allows an attacker to exceed the maximum number of resources permitted under the subscription plan. The issue was reproducible across multiple endpoints responsible for resource creation, including user creation and voucher redemption APIs.

----

h2. Impact

* *Subscription Limit Bypass:* Attackers can create more resources than allowed under their subscription plan.
* *Business Logic Abuse:* Platform usage restrictions designed to enforce subscription tiers can be bypassed.
* *Financial Impact:* Users may gain access to features or resources without paying for higher-tier subscriptions.
* *System Resource Exhaustion:* Large volumes of concurrent resource creation requests may consume backend resources and impact platform stability.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Identify an API endpoint that enforces subscription-based resource limits (e.g., user creation or voucher redemption).
# Observe the allowed resource limit under the active subscription plan.
# Use a tool capable of sending parallel requests (such as Burp Intruder or a custom script).
# Send multiple concurrent requests to the resource creation endpoint.
# Observe that multiple resources are created successfully even after the subscription limit is exceeded.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Lack of atomic validation for subscription quota enforcement.
* Absence of concurrency controls such as database locking, transactions, or distributed locks.
* The application performs limit validation and resource creation in separate operations without ensuring transactional integrity.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement atomic quota validation using database transactions or row-level locking.
* Enforce resource creation limits at the database level where possible.
* Use distributed locking mechanisms or queue-based processing to prevent concurrent quota bypass.
* Apply rate limiting to sensitive resource creation endpoints.
* Conduct concurrency testing on all APIs enforcing subscription-based limits.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-362:* Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization (Race Condition)

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API6:2023 – Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows*

The application allows attackers to abuse business processes by issuing high volumes of concurrent requests to bypass subscription enforcement mechanisms.

*A04 – Insecure Design*

*Justification:*  
The application design fails to implement proper concurrency control and atomic validation mechanisms for enforcing subscription limits, allowing attackers to exploit race conditions and bypass intended business restrictions.

Summary: Broken Function Level Authorization Allows Partner to Delete Customers via HTTP Method Tampering
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Broken Function Level Authorization Allows Partner to Delete Customers via HTTP Method Tampering

*Severity:* High  
*CVSS Score:* 8.8  
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* /api/v1/partner/customers/{id} 

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the customer management API does not properly enforce function-level authorization checks for destructive operations. While interacting with the customer management functionality, the application typically processes update operations using the {{PUT}} HTTP method. However, by modifying the request method from {{PUT}} to {{DELETE}}, the backend processes the request as a valid customer deletion request.

The application fails to validate whether the requesting user role has permission to invoke the deletion functionality. According to the intended business logic, partner accounts are not authorized to delete customer records under any circumstances, regardless of whether the customers are associated with them or not. Due to missing authorization enforcement on the backend, a partner user can perform unauthorized deletion of associated customers simply by manipulating the HTTP request method.

----

h2. Impact

* *Unauthorized Customer Deletion:* Partner users can delete customer records despite lacking the required administrative privileges.
* *Violation of Business Logic Controls:* The system fails to enforce role-based restrictions intended to prevent partners from performing destructive actions.
* *Integrity Impact:* Customer data can be permanently removed without proper authorization.
* *Operational Disruption:* Deletion of customer records may impact dependent systems, reporting processes, and service operations.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application using a valid partner account.
# Navigate to the customer management functionality where customer details can be updated.
# Intercept the request used to update a customer (typically using the {{PUT}} method).
# Modify the HTTP request method from {{PUT}} to {{DELETE}} while keeping the same endpoint and customer identifier.
# Send the modified request to the server.
# Observe that the application processes the request and deletes the customer associated with the partner account.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Missing function-level authorization checks on destructive API operations.
* Backend logic processes HTTP methods without validating whether the requesting role is permitted to execute the action.
* Lack of strict enforcement of business rules restricting partners from deleting customer records.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement strict role-based access control (RBAC) for all destructive operations such as deletion of customer records.
* Ensure backend authorization checks validate whether the requesting role has permission to invoke specific API functions.
* Restrict the {{DELETE}} operation for customer resources to authorized administrative roles only.
* Implement centralized authorization middleware to enforce permission validation consistently across all APIs.
* Log and monitor all destructive operations such as customer deletion for audit and anomaly detection.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-285:* Improper Authorization  
* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API5:2023 – Broken Function Level Authorization*

The application fails to enforce role-based authorization for sensitive operations such as customer deletion. As a result, a partner account is able to invoke a restricted destructive function by manipulating the HTTP request method.

*A01 – Broken Access Control*

*Justification:*  
The application does not properly enforce access restrictions on sensitive functionality. As a result, a user with insufficient privileges is able to perform unauthorized destructive actions by invoking restricted API functionality.

Summary: Missing Access Controls in Support Tickets Leading to Unauthorized Cross-User Data Exposure
Description:
h1. Vulnerability: Missing Access Controls in Support Tickets Leading to Unauthorized Cross-User Data Exposure

*Severity:* High  
*CVSS Score:* 8.6  
*Vulnerable Endpoints:* 

GET      /api/v1/tickets/{id} 

POST   /api/v1/tickets/st_01{id}/messages [Allows to post messages and attach files]

*Vulnerable apks:*

Customer

Partner

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the support ticket functionality does not properly enforce access control checks when retrieving ticket details. The application allows authenticated users to access ticket records belonging to other users by modifying the ticket identifier in the request.

Due to the absence of server-side authorization validation, the application returns ticket information regardless of the ownership of the ticket. As support tickets often contain sensitive information such as user details, issue descriptions, attachments, or internal responses, this behavior results in exposure of sensitive data across different user accounts.

----

h2. Impact

* *Cross-User Data Exposure:* Attackers can access support tickets created by other users.
* *Sensitive Information Disclosure:* Tickets may contain personally identifiable information (PII), account details, or confidential communications.
* *Loss of Data Confidentiality:* Unauthorized users can view private user interactions with the support system.
* *Potential Targeted Abuse:* Attackers may leverage exposed data for social engineering, account compromise attempts, or privacy violations.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application using a valid user account.
# Navigate to the support ticket section and open any existing ticket.
# Intercept the request used to fetch the ticket details.
# Modify the {{ticketId}} (or similar identifier) to another valid ticket ID belonging to a different user.
# Send the modified request to the server.
# Observe that the application returns ticket information associated with another user account.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Missing server-side authorization checks to validate ticket ownership.
* Direct object reference used for ticket retrieval without proper access control validation.
* Application relies on client-side controls instead of enforcing authorization logic on the backend.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement strict server-side authorization checks to ensure that users can only access tickets associated with their own accounts.
* Validate the ownership of each ticket before returning ticket data.
* Replace direct object references with indirect references or enforce access checks within the ticket retrieval logic.
* Implement centralized access control middleware to validate user permissions for sensitive resources.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control  
* *CWE-639:* Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

----

h2. OWASP Category

*API1:2023 – Broken Object Level Authorization*

Failure to validate ownership of ticket resources allows attackers to access objects belonging to other users by manipulating identifiers.

*A01 – Broken Access Control*

*Justification:*  
The application fails to enforce proper authorization checks when accessing user-specific resources, allowing unauthorized users to retrieve support ticket data belonging to other accounts.

Summary: Missing Backend Input Validation and Sanitization in Multiple Parameters
Description:
h2. Missing Backend Input Validation and Sanitization in Multiple Parameters (Please ensure appropriate defenses on all input params)

h3. Severity

*Medium*

h3. CVSS v3.1 Vector

*CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N*

h3. CVSS Score

*6.4*

----

h1. Description

The application fails to implement proper *server-side input validation and sanitization* across multiple API parameters. During testing, it was observed that several endpoints accept *unsanitized user-supplied input* without enforcing strict validation rules at the backend.

Although the frontend application enforces certain input restrictions, these controls can be easily bypassed by interacting directly with the backend APIs. As a result, malicious or unexpected input can be submitted directly to the server without any filtering or validation.

This behavior may allow attackers to inject unexpected payloads into the application, potentially enabling *client-side or server-side injection attacks*, manipulation of stored data, or other unintended behaviors depending on how the input is later processed or rendered by the application.

----

h1. Affected Path

Multiple API endpoints and parameters that accept user-controlled input without backend validation.

Examples include parameters accepting:

* User profile information
* Message content
* Address fields
* Other textual inputs submitted through API requests

----

h1. Impact

Because backend validation is missing, attackers can bypass frontend restrictions and submit arbitrary input directly to the APIs.

Potential impacts include:

* Injection of malicious payloads into stored data
* Manipulation of application behavior
* Possible exploitation of downstream components that process this data
* Increased risk of other injection vulnerabilities (HTML Injection, XSS, template injection, etc.)

If such inputs are later rendered in administrative panels, reports, generated documents, or logs, the vulnerability could lead to *secondary injection attacks or data integrity issues*.

----

h1. Root Cause

Input validation and sanitization are implemented only at the *frontend application layer*, while the backend APIs do not enforce equivalent validation rules.

Since frontend controls can be bypassed by direct API interaction, relying solely on client-side validation leaves the system exposed to malicious input.

----

h1. CWE-ID

*CWE-20 – Improper Input Validation*
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/20.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/20.html]

----

h1. OWASP Classification

*A05:2025 – Injection*
[https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2025-Injection/|https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2025-Injection/]

The vulnerability falls under Injection risks because unvalidated input can be processed by interpreters, templates, or rendering engines without proper filtering.

----

h1. Solution

# *Implement strict server-side input validation*
All API endpoints must validate user-supplied input against expected formats, lengths, and allowed characters.
# *Apply input sanitization before processing or storing data*
Remove or encode potentially dangerous characters such as HTML tags, scripts, or special characters where not required.
# *Use allowlist-based validation*
Validate inputs against defined schemas or patterns rather than attempting to block known malicious payloads.
# *Treat frontend validation only as a usability control*
Client-side validation should improve user experience but must never be relied upon for security enforcement.
# *Adopt centralized validation mechanisms*
Implement validation logic at middleware or service layers to ensure consistent protection across all endpoints.

Summary: HTML Injection in Invoice Generator Leading to Server-Side Requests During PDF Rendering
Description:
h1. HTML Injection in Invoice Generator Leading to Server-Side Requests During PDF Rendering ---- h2. Affected Component *Invoice Generation Functionality* h3. Affected API Invoice creation API endpoint responsible for generating invoices. h3. Affected Parameters * {{fullName}} * {{address}} ---- h2. Severity *High* *CVSS Score:* 8.3 *Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:L/A:N}} ---- h2. Description It was identified that the invoice generation API allows user-controlled input to be rendered directly in server-side HTML templates used for generating invoice PDFs. The mobile application enforces input validation restrictions on the {{fullName}} and {{address}} fields; however, the backend API does not perform server-side validation or sanitization. By directly interacting with the API endpoint, an attacker can bypass client-side controls and inject arbitrary HTML into these fields. The injected HTML is processed when the server generates the invoice PDF. During rendering, the PDF generation engine interprets HTML elements such as {{}} tags and attempts to fetch external resources referenced in the markup. This behavior causes the server to initiate outbound network requests to attacker-controlled infrastructure, demonstrating that the server-side rendering engine processes external URLs embedded in attacker-supplied HTML. This condition introduces a *Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) risk*, as the rendering engine may attempt to access internal services or network-restricted resources during document generation. ---- h2. Impact Successful exploitation may allow an attacker to: h3. 1. Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) An attacker could cause the server to make HTTP requests to arbitrary destinations, including internal services such as: * {{http://127.0.0.1}} * {{http://localhost}} * {{http://169.254.169.254}} (cloud instance metadata) If accessible, these endpoints may expose sensitive infrastructure data or internal service responses. h3. 2. Internal Network Interaction Because the renderer executes within the server environment, it may have network access to: * Internal APIs * Microservices * Administrative panels * Cloud metadata services h3. 3. Document Content Manipulation Since attacker-controlled HTML is rendered inside generated invoices, an attacker could: * Inject misleading content into official documents * Embed external resources to track document access * Manipulate invoice structure or layout ---- h2. Proof of Concept h3. Step 1 — Create Invoice via API Send a direct request to the invoice creation API containing HTML in the {{fullName}} field. *Example Payload* {noformat}fullName: Pentest User address: Test Address
Ahmedabad {noformat} ---- h3. Step 2 — Generate Invoice Trigger the invoice generation process through the application workflow. The server processes the injected HTML while generating the PDF. ---- h3. Step 3 — Observe Outbound Request The PDF rendering engine attempts to retrieve the external resource referenced in the injected HTML. *Observed Evidence* * Outbound HTTP/DNS interaction received on attacker-controlled collaborator server * Source IP: {{}} * Request path: {{/test}} * Timestamp: {{}} This confirms that the server performs external network requests during document rendering. ---- h2. Root Cause * Input validation enforced only at the client (Flutter application). * Backend API accepts unsanitized HTML input. * Server-side PDF renderer processes external resources referenced in HTML templates. * Lack of server-side sanitization and output encoding before rendering user input. ---- h2. Recommendations # *Implement strict server-side input validation and sanitization* for all user-controlled fields before storing or rendering them. # Ensure the application passes sanitized *raw text to the PDF generation service.* # *Sanitize HTML content* to remove unsafe elements such as: #* {{}} #* {{

Summary: IDOR via Policy Number in "Know Your Policy" QR Code Generation
Description:
h1. IDOR via Policy Number in "Know Your Policy" QR Code Generation

----

h3. Affected Endpoints

* {{/api/v1/know-your-policy/qr-code}} (Method: GET)
{{?policyNumber={value}&type=url}}
* {{/api/v1/know-your-policy/verify}} (Method: GET)
{{?token={jwt}&type=url}}

----

h3. Severity

*Critical*

*CVSS Score:* 9.6
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

The "Know Your Policy" feature generates a QR code that contains a URL with an embedded JWT token valid for *30 days*.

The endpoint:

{noformat}GET /api/v1/know-your-policy/qr-code?policyNumber={value}&type=url{noformat}

returns JSON containing {{qrData}}, which includes a URL structured as:

{noformat}https://app.com/api/v1/know-your-policy/verify?token=REDACTED&type=url{noformat}

The vulnerability arises because:

# The {{qr-code}} endpoint accepts a {{policyNumber}} parameter.
# There is no proper object-level authorization validation to ensure the requesting user owns that policy.
# Any authenticated user can supply *any valid policy number*.
# The system generates a valid 30-day access token for that policy.
# The {{/verify}} endpoint then discloses full policy details.

This constitutes a classic *Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)* combined with long-lived token misuse.

Since signup is open, any individual can create an account and enumerate policy numbers.

----

h2. Impact

An attacker can:

* Enumerate policy numbers
* Generate valid 30-day access tokens for arbitrary policies
* Retrieve complete policy data of other customers

Exposed sensitive information includes:

* Policy Number
* Policy Holder Name
* Email Address
* Contact Number
* Policy Type & Category
* Start & End Date
* Sum Assured
* Term Period
* Status
* Insurance Company
* Nominee Details (Name, DOB, Contact)

This results in:

* Massive PII disclosure
* Financial and insurance data exposure
* Nominee identity leakage
* Regulatory non-compliance (privacy/data protection laws)
* Potential social engineering and fraud exploitation

The scope is changed ({{S:C}}) because one user can access another user's data across tenant boundaries.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Missing Object-Level Authorization (BOLA)
* Trusting user-supplied {{policyNumber}} parameter
* Long-lived JWT (30 days) for sensitive document access
* Absence of ownership validation before token issuance
* No rate limiting or enumeration protection

----

h2. Recommendation

h3. Immediate Remediation

# Enforce strict ownership validation before generating QR tokens.
# Ensure {{policyNumber}} is derived from authenticated session context instead of request parameter.
# Reduce token validity to a short duration (e.g., 5–10 minutes).
# Bind tokens to:
#* User ID
#* Policy ID
#* Device fingerprint (if applicable)
# Implement rate limiting on QR generation endpoint.
# Add monitoring for abnormal access patterns.
# Invalidate all previously generated long-lived tokens.

h3. Secure Design Enhancement

* Replace static policy number access with opaque, random identifiers.
* Implement one-time-use signed URLs.
* Use server-side session validation instead of self-contained long-lived JWTs.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key (IDOR)*
* *CWE-285: Improper Authorization*
* *CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration*

----

h2. OWASP Classification

h3. OWASP Top 10:2025

* *A01:2025 – Broken Access Control*
* *A02:2025 – Cryptographic Failures*

h3. OWASP API Security Top 10:2023

* *API1:2023 – Broken Object Level Authorization*
* *API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*
* *API3:2023 – Broken Object Property Level Authorization*

----

h2. One-Line Summary

The QR code generation endpoint allows any authenticated user to generate 30-day valid access tokens for arbitrary policy numbers, leading to full cross-user policy data disclosure via IDOR and long-lived token abuse.

Summary: Race Condition Bypass of Partner User Onboarding Limit
Description:
h1. Race Condition Bypass of Partner User Onboarding Limit

----

h3. Affected Endpoints

* {{/api/v1/partner/customer}} (Method: POST)

----

h3. Severity

*Critical*

*CVSS Score:* 9.4
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:H}}

----

h2. Description

It was identified that the partner customer creation endpoint does not properly enforce the configured subscription limit on the number of users a partner is allowed to onboard.

From the admin panel, a custom subscription was configured to allow the partner to create a maximum of *5 users*. However, by sending multiple concurrent POST requests at high speed (race condition scenario) to {{/api/v1/partner/customer}}, the partner was able to create *11 distinct active users*, exceeding the configured limit.

This indicates that the system performs a non-atomic validation check (e.g., checking current count before insert) without implementing proper transactional locking or concurrency control. As a result, multiple parallel requests bypass the limit before the system updates the user count.

All created users were active and fully functional, confirming a successful bypass of subscription enforcement.

----

h2. Impact

An attacker (malicious partner) could:

* Bypass subscription limits
* Onboard unlimited users beyond licensed capacity
* Abuse platform resources
* Cause direct revenue loss
* Undermine subscription integrity controls
* Potentially exhaust backend resources through automated onboarding

Since subscription limits are part of core business enforcement logic, bypassing them directly impacts revenue protection and system integrity.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Non-atomic validation of subscription user limit
* Absence of transactional locking or row-level locking
* Missing concurrency control on user creation
* Business rule enforcement implemented only at application logic level
* Lack of rate limiting or request throttling

----

h2. Recommendation

# Enforce subscription limits within a database transaction.
# Implement row-level locking ({{SELECT ... FOR UPDATE}}) on subscription records.
# Maintain an atomic counter for onboarded users.
# Apply unique constraints or quota enforcement at database level.
# Introduce rate limiting on user creation endpoints.
# Implement idempotency keys to prevent rapid repeated execution.

Summary: Improper Success Response on Unauthorized Notification Deletion
Description:
h1. Improper Success Response on Unauthorized Notification Deletion

----

h3. Affected Endpoints

* {{/api/v1/notifications}} (Method: DELETE)
* Mark-as-read API <Add-here>

----

h3. Severity

*Low*

*CVSS Score:* 3.7
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:L}}

----

h2. Description

It was identified that the notification deletion and mark-as-read APIs return an HTTP {{200 OK}} response even when an authenticated user attempts to modify notifications belonging to another user.

Although backend authorization checks appear to prevent cross-user modification (as the action is not reflected in the APK), the API does not return an appropriate error response such as {{403 Forbidden}}. Instead, it responds with a success status, creating a false-positive indication of successful operation.

This behavior indicates improper error handling and inconsistent response management for authorization failures.

----

h2. Impact

An attacker could:

* Infer backend validation behavior through response discrepancies
* Mislead client applications into assuming state changes occurred
* Cause data inconsistency between frontend and backend state
* Exploit logic flaws in clients that rely solely on HTTP status codes

While unauthorized deletion is not successfully executed, improper success responses degrade API integrity and may enable logic abuse in integrated systems.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Authorization checks performed without proper error response handling
* Incorrect use of HTTP status codes
* Lack of standardized error management for access control violations
* Business logic not aligned with RESTful response semantics

----

h2. Recommendation

# Return {{403 Forbidden}} when a user attempts to modify another user’s notifications.
# Ensure consistent HTTP status codes for authorization failures.
# Implement centralized exception handling for access control violations.
# Avoid returning generic {{200 OK}} for failed operations.
# Log unauthorized access attempts for monitoring and auditing.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-285: Improper Authorization*
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/285.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/285.html]

Summary: Role Enumeration via Overly Descriptive Signup Error Message
Description:
h1. Role Enumeration via Overly Descriptive Signup Error Message

----

h3. Affected Endpoints

* {{/api/v1/customers/signup}}

----

h3. Severity

*Medium*

*CVSS Score:* 6.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was identified that the customer signup API reveals excessive role-based information when a user attempts to register using a phone number that is already associated with an existing account across any of the three platforms (Web, Admin APK, Partner APK).

When a previously registered phone number is used, the application responds with:

{quote}"You already have a {{Role-Name}} account. Please log in to continue."{quote}

The {{{{Role-Name}}}} dynamically discloses the exact role associated with the phone number (e.g., Admin, Partner, or other privileged roles).

This behavior enables role enumeration by unauthenticated users. By submitting arbitrary phone numbers, an attacker can determine whether a number is associated with a privileged role, thereby exposing sensitive authorization structure information across platforms.

----

h2. Impact

An attacker could:

* Enumerate valid phone numbers registered in the system
* Identify high-privilege accounts (e.g., Admin, Partner)
* Perform targeted phishing or social engineering attacks
* Prioritize credential stuffing attacks against privileged roles
* Map internal role architecture across platforms

While this does not directly grant access, it significantly reduces uncertainty for attackers and increases the likelihood of targeted compromise.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Overly verbose and role-specific error messaging
* Lack of uniform response handling for existing accounts
* Disclosure of authorization-level metadata to unauthenticated users
* Insufficient consideration of information disclosure risks in authentication flows

----

h2. Recommendation

# Replace role-specific messages with generic responses such as:
#* “An account already exists with this number. Please log in.”
# Ensure consistent responses regardless of associated role.
# Avoid exposing authorization-level metadata in public endpoints.
# Implement rate limiting and monitoring on signup endpoints.
# Consider adding abuse detection for enumeration patterns.

----

h2. CWE-ID

[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html]

Summary: Purchase Token Reuse Allowing Unlimited Subscription Activation
Description:
h1. Purchase Token Reuse Allowing Unlimited Subscription Activation

----

h3. Affected Endpoints

* {{/api/v1/subscriptions}}

----

h3. Severity

*Critical*

*CVSS Score:* 9.1
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:H/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was identified that the subscription validation mechanism improperly trusts a purchase token without binding it to the purchasing user account. After purchasing a valid subscription, the purchase token can be intercepted from the application traffic.

By replacing the authentication token (Auth Token) with another user’s token while reusing the same captured purchase token in requests to {{/api/v1/subscriptions}}, the backend activates the subscription for the new account. The system does not verify whether the purchase token has already been redeemed, nor does it validate ownership or enforce one-time usage constraints.

This allows a single legitimate subscription purchase to be reused across multiple user accounts, effectively bypassing subscription payment controls.

----

h2. Impact

An attacker could:

* Purchase one subscription and reuse it for unlimited accounts
* Share a valid purchase token publicly
* Bypass monetization controls entirely
* Cause significant revenue loss
* Undermine licensing and subscription enforcement mechanisms

Since no additional authentication checks are performed against the purchase token ownership, exploitation is trivial and scalable.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Purchase token not bound to specific user identity
* Absence of one-time token redemption validation
* No server-side verification of token consumption status
* Missing integrity checks against payment provider validation response
* Trusting client-supplied purchase artifacts without strict backend reconciliation

----

h2. Recommendation

# Bind purchase tokens to the original purchasing user account.
# Enforce strict one-time redemption of purchase tokens.
# Maintain server-side purchase token consumption state.
# Validate purchase tokens directly with the payment provider for each redemption.
# Reject reuse attempts with proper error responses.
# Implement anomaly detection for multiple activations from same purchase token.
# Log and monitor suspicious subscription activation attempts.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-287: Improper Authentication*
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/287.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/287.html]
* *CWE-345: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity*
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/345.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/345.html]

----

h2. OWASP Classification

h3. OWASP Top 10:2025

* *A01:2025 – Broken Access Control*
* *A04:2025 – Insecure Design*

h3. OWASP API Security Top 10:2023

* *API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*
* *API3:2023 – Broken Object Property Level Authorization*

----

h2. One-Line Summary

The subscription API allows reuse of a single purchase token across multiple accounts, enabling unlimited subscription activation from a single valid purchase and resulting in critical revenue and integrity impact.

Summary: Excessive JWT Expiry in “Know Your Policy” QR Share Feature
Description:
h1. Excessive JWT Expiry in “Know Your Policy” QR Share Feature

----

h3. Affected Endpoints

* {{/api/v1/know-your-policy/verify?token={jwt}&type=url}}

----

h3. Severity

*High*

*CVSS Score:* 7.4
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was identified that the Android application provides a *“Know Your Policy”* feature that generates a QR code containing a JWT-based verification token. Upon scanning, the QR code resolves to a URL in the format:

{noformat}/api/v1/know-your-policy/verify?token={jwt}&type=url
{noformat}

The JWT embedded within the URL remains valid for *30 days*. This token grants access to sensitive policy details intended for controlled sharing.

A 30-day validity period for a token embedded in a sharable QR code constitutes excessive token lifetime for sensitive insurance documentation. Since the token is transmitted via URL, it may be exposed through browser history, referer headers, logs, screenshots, messaging apps, or intermediary systems. If intercepted, the token can be reused by any party until expiration without additional authentication.

----

h2. Impact

An attacker who obtains the QR code or URL could:

* Access complete policy details without authentication
* Share the link publicly or forward it to unauthorized parties
* Retain long-term access to sensitive insurance information
* Harvest personal and policy-related data for social engineering or fraud

Because the token remains valid for 30 days, the attack window is significantly extended, increasing the likelihood of misuse.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Excessive JWT expiration duration for sensitive document sharing
* Use of bearer token in URL query parameter
* Lack of short-lived or one-time-use token enforcement
* No contextual binding (device/IP/session) applied to token validation

----

h2. Recommendation

# Reduce token validity to a minimal duration (e.g., 5–15 minutes).
# Implement one-time-use tokens invalidated after first verification.
# Avoid placing JWTs in URL query parameters; use secure POST flows where possible.
# Bind tokens to contextual attributes (IP/device fingerprint) if business permits.
# Implement server-side revocation capability.
# Log and monitor token usage anomalies.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration*
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/613.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/613.html]
* *CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor*
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html]

----

h2. OWASP Classification

h3. OWASP Top 10:2025

* *A07:2025 – Identification and Authentication Failures*
* *A02:2025 – Cryptographic Failures*

h3. OWASP API Security Top 10:2023

* *API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*
* *API8:2023 – Security Misconfiguration*

----

h2. One-Line Summary

The “Know Your Policy” QR feature generates a JWT-based shareable URL valid for 30 days, creating an extended unauthorized access window for sensitive policy data if the link is exposed or intercepted.

Summary: JSON Injection Allowing Modification of Immutable policyNumber
Description:
h1. JSON Injection Allowing Modification of Immutable {{policyNumber}}

----

h3. Affected Endpoints

* {{/api/v1/customer/policies/pol_{id}}}

----

h3. Severity

*Medium*

*CVSS Score:* 5.4
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:L/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was identified that the customer policy update endpoint accepts arbitrary JSON attributes without strict server-side field allowlisting. The {{policyNumber}} attribute, which is defined as a system-controlled immutable business identifier, can be supplied within the request body by an authenticated user.

Although the API response returns {{null}} for the injected {{policyNumber}} field, the application does not explicitly reject the request or enforce attribute-level authorization. The absence of validation or rejection indicates improper control over dynamically supplied object properties. Business-critical identifiers should never be client-modifiable, even if backend logic later nullifies or ignores the value.

Allowing such injection attempts increases the attack surface for mass assignment vulnerabilities, logic bypass scenarios, and future regression risks if backend validation logic changes.

----

h2. Impact

An authenticated attacker could:

* Attempt to override system-controlled business identifiers
* Exploit potential backend logic flaws if validation weakens in future releases
* Manipulate policy associations if persistence logic is later altered
* Abuse mass assignment patterns to target additional protected fields

While current behavior returns {{null}}, the improper acceptance of immutable attributes reflects broken object property-level control and weak enforcement of business logic boundaries.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Absence of strict server-side allowlisting for updatable fields
* Missing attribute-level authorization checks
* Improper separation between client-controlled DTOs and internal persistence models
* Business rule immutability not enforced at service layer

----

h2. Recommendation

# Implement strict allowlisting of permitted update fields.
# Reject requests containing immutable attributes (e.g., return {{400 Bad Request}}).
# Enforce object property-level authorization checks.
# Separate update DTOs from domain entities to prevent mass assignment.
# Implement schema validation to block unexpected JSON properties.
# Log and monitor attempts to modify protected attributes.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-915: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes*
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/915.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/915.html]

Summary: Clear Text Credential Storage in Message Audit Logs
Description:
h1. Clear Text Credential Storage in Message Audit Logs

----

h3. Affected Endpoints

* {{/monitor/messagelog}}
* {{/api/v1/message-log/ml_{id}/message}}

----

h3. Severity

*High*

*CVSS Score:* 8.2
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:L/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was identified that the message audit/logging functionality stores and exposes sensitive data in clear text via the message log endpoints.

The following event types were observed containing sensitive information:

* Invite Employee
* Employee Forgot Password
* User Created
* Forgot Password
* Employee Details Change Request

The API responses include:

* Plain text credentials (temporary passwords / reset passwords)
* Account activation details
* Sensitive PII (email, name, role, internal identifiers)
* Modified employee profile information

The {{/monitor/messagelog}} and {{/api/v1/message-log/ml_{id}/message}} endpoints return full message bodies without redaction or masking of sensitive fields.

Message audit logs should never contain raw credentials or authentication artifacts. Logging such information significantly increases the impact of any privilege abuse, IDOR vulnerability, or log exposure.

----

h2. Impact

An attacker with access to message log endpoints (including low-privileged authenticated users if access control is weak) could:

* Retrieve temporary or reset passwords in plain text
* Compromise newly created user accounts
* Hijack accounts during password reset flows
* Access and misuse sensitive employee PII
* Perform lateral movement within the application

Because credentials are stored in clear text, exploitation does not require cryptographic cracking — the attacker gains immediate usable authentication data.

If chained with access control weaknesses, this may result in full account takeover and administrative compromise.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Sensitive data (credentials and reset information) logged in application audit messages
* No redaction or masking of confidential fields
* Inadequate data minimization in logging pipeline
* Overexposed internal monitoring endpoints

----

h2. Recommendation

# Immediately stop logging:
#* Plain text passwords
#* Temporary credentials
#* Password reset tokens
#* Authentication secrets
# Implement secure logging controls:
#* Mask sensitive fields (e.g., {{******}})
#* Redact tokens and credentials before persistence
#* Log only event metadata (timestamp, user ID, action type)
# Enforce strict access control on monitoring endpoints.
# Conduct log review and purge existing logs containing credentials.
# Implement secure audit logging guidelines aligned with least data exposure principles.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-532: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File*
* *CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor*

----

h2. OWASP Classification

h3. OWASP Top 10:2025

* *A02:2025 – Security Misconfiguration*
* *A04:2025 – Cryptographic Failures* (due to improper protection of sensitive data)

h3. OWASP API Security Top 10:2023

* *API3:2023 – Broken Object Property Level Authorization* (if accessible beyond intended scope)
* *API8:2023 – Security Misconfiguration*

----

h2. One-Line Summary

Application message audit endpoints store and expose plain text credentials and sensitive PII, enabling immediate account compromise and significant data confidentiality risks.

Summary: Session Does Not Expire Upon Password Change
Description:
h1. Session Does Not Expire Upon Password Change

----

h3. Affected Endpoint(s):

* {{https://app.regalsecurity.com/*}}
* {{/api/*}}

----

h3. Severity:

*High*

*CVSS Score:* 8.8
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that active user sessions remain valid even after a successful password change. When a user updates their account password, previously issued session tokens and authentication cookies are not invalidated, allowing existing sessions across browsers or devices to continue functioning without requiring re-authentication.

If exploited by an attacker who has already obtained a valid session token through session hijacking, device compromise, or shared system access, the attacker would continue to maintain unauthorized access even after the legitimate user changes their password. This could lead to persistent account compromise, unauthorized data access, fraudulent transactions, and potential regulatory non-compliance due to failure in enforcing secure session lifecycle management.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application in Browser A.
# Capture the active session cookie/token.
# Change the account password via {{/account/change-password}}.
# Use the previously captured session token in Browser B or replay it via a proxy.
# Observe that access to authenticated endpoints such as {{/dashboard}} remains valid.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Session invalidation logic is not triggered upon password change.
* The application does not revoke or rotate existing session tokens after credential modification.

----

h2. Solution

* Invalidate all active sessions immediately after a successful password change.
* Revoke and rotate all session tokens associated with the user account.
* Implement centralized server-side session management with active session tracking.
* Force re-authentication across all devices after credential updates.
* Log and notify users regarding session revocation events.

----

h2. CWE-ID

*CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration*

----

h2. OWASP Category

*OWASP Web Top 10:2025*
A07 Authentication Failures

----

h2. References

[https://owasp.org/Top10/A07_Authentication_Failures/|https://owasp.org/Top10/A07_Authentication_Failures/]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/613.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/613.html]

----

h2. One-Line Summary

Active sessions remain valid after password change, allowing persistent unauthorized access if a session token has been previously compromised.

Summary: Broken Authentication & Session Management – Token Misconfiguration Leading to Account Takeover Risk
Description:
h1. Broken Authentication & Session Management – Token Misconfiguration Leading to Account Takeover Risk

----

h3. Affected Endpoint(s):

* [+https://app.regalsecurity.com/forgot-password/verify?token=+|https://app.regalsecurity.com/forgot-password/verify?token=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzdWIiOjgwLCJpYXQiOjE3NzEyNDA2OTcsImV4cCI6MTc3MTI0MTI5N30.1yX9KFNm7aH1W7jgNdd_7Fhfa6dMVWK2GqeXFJQpmH8]<token-here>

----

h3. Severity:

*High*

*CVSS Score:* 8.6
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application implements improper token lifecycle and session management controls within its authentication and password reset mechanisms. The reset tokens remain valid for one hour but are not enforced as single-use, multiple reset tokens can remain valid simultaneously, access tokens are accepted in contexts intended for reset tokens, active sessions are not revoked after password change, and authentication tokens are additionally stored in {{localStorage}} despite the presence of an HttpOnly cookie. These combined weaknesses indicate flawed token purpose validation, token revocation logic, and session lifecycle enforcement.

If exploited by an attacker, these weaknesses could lead to persistent account takeover. An attacker who gains access to a reset token, access token, or compromised browser environment could reuse valid tokens, maintain active sessions even after password updates, and potentially bypass intended reset workflows. Since tokens are not strictly segregated by purpose and are not rotated or revoked properly, a single compromise event could result in long-term unauthorized access, making remediation more complex and increasing systemic security risk.

h1. Finding 1 – Access Token Accepted as Reset Token

The application does not strictly validate token purpose, resulting in access tokens being accepted in reset-password flows. Token claims do not enforce contextual usage restrictions between authentication and password reset mechanisms.

If exploited, an attacker in possession of a valid access token could potentially manipulate reset flows or bypass intended validation boundaries, increasing the likelihood of unauthorized password modification and privilege abuse.

h1. Finding 2 – Tokens Not Revoked After Password Change

It was observed that active sessions remain valid even after a successful password change. The application does not invalidate or revoke previously issued access tokens or sessions.

If exploited, an attacker who already possesses a valid session token could retain persistent access to the account even after the legitimate user changes their password, leading to sustained account compromise.

h1. Finding 3 – Multiple Reset Tokens Valid Simultaneously

The application allows multiple password reset tokens to remain valid concurrently within their one-hour expiry window. Reset tokens are not invalidated when a new reset request is generated.

If exploited, an attacker who obtains any previously issued reset token within the validity window could reuse it even after the user initiates a new reset request, increasing the attack surface and weakening the intended security control of password recovery mechanisms.

h1. Finding 4 – Token Stored in localStorage (Risk Amplification)

Although the application sets authentication tokens in an HttpOnly cookie (which is a secure practice), the same token is also stored in {{localStorage}}. Storing tokens in browser storage exposes them to JavaScript access and increases susceptibility to exfiltration via Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

If exploited in conjunction with an XSS vulnerability or malicious browser extension, an attacker could extract tokens from {{localStorage}}, bypass HttpOnly protections, and perform authenticated actions or maintain session persistence.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Lack of strict token purpose validation (access vs reset).
* Absence of single-use reset token enforcement.
* No token revocation mechanism on password change.
* Improper token storage design (dual storage in cookie and localStorage).
* Inadequate session lifecycle management controls.

----

h2. Recommended Remediation

* Implement strict separation of token types (access tokens vs reset tokens).
* Enforce token purpose claim validation (e.g., {{typ}}, {{aud}}, or custom purpose claim).
* Store access tokens exclusively in {{HttpOnly}}, {{Secure}}, {{SameSite}} cookies.
* Remove authentication tokens from {{localStorage}}.
* Revoke all active sessions immediately upon password change.
* Implement reset token rotation with strict single-use enforcement.
* Invalidate previously issued reset tokens when a new reset request is generated.
* Consider implementing refresh token rotation with reuse detection.
* Introduce centralized token revocation lists or token versioning mechanisms.

----

h2. CWE-ID

*CWE-287: Improper Authentication*
*CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration*
*CWE-522: Insufficiently Protected Credentials*

----

h2. OWASP Category

*OWASP Web Top 10:2025*
A07 Authentication Failures

*OWASP API Security Top 10:2023*
API2:2023 Broken Authentication

----

h2. References

[https://owasp.org/Top10/A07_Authentication_Failures/|https://owasp.org/Top10/A07_Authentication_Failures/]
[https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0x11-t10/|https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0x11-t10/]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/287.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/287.html]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/613.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/613.html]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/522.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/522.html]

----

h2. One-Line Summary

Improper token lifecycle management and session misconfiguration enable reset token reuse, persistent sessions after password change, and increased account takeover risk due to flawed authentication design.

Summary: Missing Multiple Security Headers
Description:
h1. Missing Multiple Security Headers

----

h3. Affected Endpoint(s):

* {{https://app.regalsecurity.com}}
* {{/api/*}}

----

h3. Severity:

*Medium*

*CVSS Score:* 6.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application responses from {{https://app.client.com}} and endpoints under {{/api/*}} are missing several important HTTP security headers, including {{Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS)}}, {{Content-Security-Policy (CSP)}}, {{X-Frame-Options}}, and {{X-Content-Type-Options}}. These headers are essential for enforcing secure browser behavior and mitigating common client-side attack vectors such as downgrade attacks, cross-site scripting, clickjacking, and MIME-type confusion.

If exploited by an attacker, the absence of these headers could increase the likelihood of successful man-in-the-middle attacks, script injection, clickjacking, and content-type manipulation. An attacker could potentially inject malicious scripts, frame the application within a malicious site to trick users into unintended actions, or exploit content sniffing behavior to execute arbitrary code, thereby impacting confidentiality and integrity of user sessions and application data.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Send an HTTP request to:
{noformat}https://app.client.com{noformat}
or any endpoint under:
{noformat}/api/*{noformat}
# Inspect the response headers.
# Observe that the following headers are missing:
#* {{Strict-Transport-Security}}
#* {{Content-Security-Policy}}
#* {{X-Frame-Options}}
#* {{X-Content-Type-Options}}

----

h2. Root Cause

* Secure response headers are not configured at the web server or reverse proxy level.
* Security hardening baseline has not been enforced in production configuration.

----

h2. Solution

* Configure and enable the following security headers:
** *Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS):*
{noformat}Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload{noformat}
** *Content-Security-Policy (CSP):*
{noformat}Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self';{noformat}
(Customize policy based on application requirements.)
** *X-Frame-Options:*
{noformat}X-Frame-Options: DENY{noformat}
or
{noformat}X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN{noformat}
** *X-Content-Type-Options:*
{noformat}X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff{noformat}
* Implement these headers at the reverse proxy (e.g., Nginx) or application server layer.
* Validate header presence through automated security testing pipelines.

Multiple critical HTTP security headers are missing, weakening browser-enforced protections and increasing exposure to client-side attacks such as XSS, clickjacking, and MIME-type confusion.

Summary: Application Vulnerable to Clickjacking
Description:
h1. Application Vulnerable to Clickjacking

----

h3. Affected Endpoint(s):

* {{https://app.regalsecurity.com/}}

----

h3. Severity:

*Medium*

*CVSS Score:* 6.1
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application hosted at {{https://app.client.com/}} can be embedded within an external {{<iframe>}} without any restriction, as protective headers such as {{X-Frame-Options}} or an appropriate {{Content-Security-Policy}} frame-ancestors directive are not configured. This allows the application to be rendered inside a malicious website controlled by an attacker.

If exploited by an attacker, the application could be framed within a deceptive interface to trick users into performing unintended actions such as clicking buttons, submitting forms, or initiating sensitive transactions. This could lead to unauthorized operations being executed in the context of an authenticated user session, potentially compromising account integrity and sensitive data.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Create a malicious HTML page with the following content:
{noformat}<iframe src="https://app.regalsecurity.com/" width="800" height="600"></iframe>{noformat}
# Host and open the page in a browser.
# Observe that the application loads successfully within the iframe.

----

h2. Root Cause

* {{X-Frame-Options}} header is not configured.
* {{Content-Security-Policy}} does not define the {{frame-ancestors}} directive.
* Secure browser embedding restrictions are not enforced at the server level.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement one of the following controls:
** *X-Frame-Options:*
{noformat}X-Frame-Options: DENY{noformat}
or
{noformat}X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN{noformat}
** *Content-Security-Policy (Preferred Modern Approach):*
{noformat}Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'none';{noformat}
or
{noformat}Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'self';{noformat}
* Ensure headers are configured at the reverse proxy or application server level.
* Validate configuration across all sensitive routes.

----

h2. CWE-ID

Summary: Weak Email Authentication Controls Enabling Domain Spoofing Risk
Description:
h1. Weak Email Authentication Controls Enabling Domain Spoofing Risk

----

h3. Affected Endpoint(s):

* {{regalsecurity.com}} (Mail Server / DNS Configuration)

----

h3. Severity:

*High*

*CVSS Score:* 8.1
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the domain’s email authentication configuration is weak due to SPF configured with {{~all}} (SoftFail), DMARC policy set to {{p=none}} (monitoring mode only), and absence of detectable DKIM selectors. The current SPF configuration does not strictly reject unauthorized senders, DMARC is not enforcing quarantine or rejection policies, and outbound emails are not cryptographically signed via DKIM. These combined misconfigurations weaken sender authentication and domain protection mechanisms.

If exploited by an attacker, the domain could be spoofed to send fraudulent emails appearing as legitimate communications from the organization. This could result in phishing attacks, business email compromise (BEC), credential harvesting, financial fraud, and reputational damage. Since DMARC is not enforcing rejection and DKIM is missing, malicious emails may pass receiving server checks more easily, significantly increasing the risk of successful impersonation campaigns.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Query the domain’s SPF record:
{noformat}nslookup -type=txt client-domain.com{noformat}
Observe:
{noformat}v=spf1 include:<spf-provider> ~all{noformat}
# Query the DMARC record:
{noformat}nslookup -type=txt _dmarc.client-domain.com{noformat}
Observe:
{noformat}v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:info@client-domain.com; fo=1{noformat}
# Attempt to identify DKIM selectors:
{noformat}nslookup -type=txt selector1._domainkey.client-domain.com{noformat}
Observe no valid DKIM record returned.

----

h2. Root Cause

* SPF configured in SoftFail mode ({{~all}}) instead of strict enforcement ({{-all}}).
* DMARC policy set to monitoring mode ({{p=none}}) without quarantine or reject enforcement.
* DKIM not implemented or not properly configured.
* Lack of hardened email authentication posture.

----

h2. Solution

* Update SPF record to strict enforcement:
{noformat}v=spf1 include:<spf-provider> -all{noformat}
* Implement DKIM signing for all outbound mail with proper key rotation policy.
* Change DMARC policy progressively:
** Start with {{p=quarantine}}
** Move to {{p=reject}} after validation period
* Implement DMARC alignment (SPF + DKIM alignment with From domain).
* Continuously monitor DMARC reports and tune configuration accordingly.

----

h2. CWE-ID

*CWE-290: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing*

----

h2. OWASP Category

*OWASP Web Top 10:2025*
A07 Authentication Failures

----

h2. References

[https://owasp.org/Top10/A07_Authentication_Failures/|https://owasp.org/Top10/A07_Authentication_Failures/]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/290.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/290.html]

----

h2. One-Line Summary

Weak SPF, non-enforced DMARC policy, and missing DKIM enable domain spoofing risk, increasing exposure to phishing and business email compromise attacks.

Summary: Exposure of Password Hash in Authenticated API Response
Description:
h1. Exposure of Password Hash in Authenticated API Response

----

h3. Affected Endpoint(s):

* {{/api/user/profile}}

----

h3. Severity:

*Low*

*CVSS Score:* 3.7
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the authenticated API response returns the user's bcrypt password hash as part of the serialized user object. Although bcrypt is a strong one-way hashing algorithm designed to resist offline cracking, password hashes remain sensitive authentication artifacts and should never be exposed to client-side applications or API consumers. The presence of {{password_hash}} in the response indicates improper data serialization controls within the authentication or user DTO layer.

If exploited in conjunction with other vulnerabilities such as Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR), or client-side compromise, an attacker could obtain password hashes and attempt offline cracking or reuse them in credential stuffing attacks. While the standalone risk remains limited because users only receive their own hash, this design flaw increases future blast radius in case of additional security weaknesses and contradicts secure development and data minimization principles.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Authenticate using valid credentials via:
{noformat}POST /api/login{noformat}
# Inspect the JSON response body.
# Observe that the response contains a field similar to:
{noformat}{
  "id": 101,
  "email": "user@example.com",
  "password_hash": "$2b$10$XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX"
}{noformat}

----

h2. Root Cause

* Sensitive fields are not excluded from serialization logic.
* Improper DTO (Data Transfer Object) design exposes internal authentication attributes.
* Lack of secure response data filtering for user objects.

----

h2. Recommendation

* Remove {{password_hash}} from all API response payloads.
* Ensure sensitive fields are explicitly excluded in DTOs and serializers.
* Conduct a comprehensive review of all user-related API responses.
* Implement response-level schema validation to prevent accidental exposure of sensitive attributes.
* Apply secure coding guidelines enforcing strict separation between persistence models and API models.

----

h2. CWE-ID

*CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor*

----

h2. OWASP Category

*OWASP Web Top 10:2025*
A06 Insecure Design

*OWASP API Top 10:2023*

API3:2023 - Broken Object Property Level Authorization

----

h2. References

[https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A06_2025-Insecure_Design/|https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A06_2025-Insecure_Design/]

[https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa3-broken-object-property-level-authorization/|https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa3-broken-object-property-level-authorization/]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html]

----

h2. One-Line Summary

The authenticated API response exposes the user’s bcrypt password hash, violating secure design principles and increasing risk if combined with additional vulnerabilities.

Summary: Username Enumeration via Forgot Password Functionality
Description:
h1. Username Enumeration via Forgot Password Functionality

h3. Severity:

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the {{/forgot-password}} functionality returns a distinct response message when an unregistered email address is submitted, specifically indicating “User not found.” This behavior allows an unauthenticated attacker to differentiate between registered and non-registered email addresses based on the application’s response.

If exploited by an attacker, this behavior could enable systematic username enumeration by automating email submissions and observing response differences. The collected list of valid accounts could later be used for targeted phishing, credential stuffing, brute-force attacks, or social engineering campaigns, thereby increasing the overall attack surface of the authentication system.

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Navigate to:
{noformat}/forgot-password{noformat}

{noformat}User not found{noformat}

h2. Root Cause

* Authentication-related responses reveal account existence through descriptive error messages.
* Lack of generic response handling for sensitive account workflows.

----

h2. Recommendation

* Replace specific error messages with a generic response such as:
“If the provided email address is associated with an account, password reset instructions will be sent shortly.”
* Ensure consistent response messaging regardless of account existence.
* Implement rate limiting and monitoring on the {{/forgot-password}} endpoint.
* Consider adding CAPTCHA or abuse detection mechanisms to prevent automated enumeration.

Summary: Sensitive Role Permissions and User Details Stored in Local Storage
Description:
h1. Sensitive Role Permissions and User Details Stored in Local Storage

----

h3. Affected Endpoint(s):

* {{https://app.regalsecurity.com}}

----

h3. Severity:

*Medium*

*CVSS Score:* 6.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:L/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application stores sensitive user-related information in the browser’s {{localStorage}}, including role permissions, access tokens, username, and email address. Since {{localStorage}} is accessible via JavaScript within the browser context, any client-side script executing in the origin—whether legitimate or malicious—can read and exfiltrate this data. Storing authentication tokens and authorization metadata in {{localStorage}} violates secure token handling best practices and increases exposure to client-side threats.

If exploited in the presence of a Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability, malicious browser extension, or compromised third-party script, an attacker could retrieve access tokens and authorization details directly from {{localStorage}}. This could enable session hijacking, privilege misuse, lateral movement within the application, and unauthorized access to sensitive resources, significantly amplifying the impact of otherwise low-to-medium severity client-side vulnerabilities.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application at:
{noformat}https://app.regalsecurity.com{noformat}
# Open browser Developer Tools → Application → Local Storage.
# Observe stored entries containing:
#* Access token
#* User roles/permissions
#* Username
#* Email address

----

h2. Root Cause

* Sensitive authentication and authorization data are stored in client-side persistent storage.
* Lack of secure token storage strategy (e.g., HttpOnly cookies).
* Overexposure of internal authorization metadata to the client environment.

----

h2. Recommendation

* Remove access tokens and sensitive authorization data from {{localStorage}}.
* Store authentication tokens exclusively in {{HttpOnly}}, {{Secure}}, and {{SameSite}} cookies.
* Avoid exposing detailed role-permission mappings to the client unless strictly necessary.
* Implement short-lived access tokens with secure refresh token rotation.
* Conduct a client-side storage audit to eliminate unnecessary sensitive data persistence.

----

h2. CWE-ID

*CWE-922: Insecure Storage of Sensitive Information*

----

h2. OWASP Category

*OWASP Web Top 10:2025*
A06, Insecure Design

----

h2. References
