[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/922.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/922.html]

----

h2. One-Line Summary

Sensitive authentication tokens and role-permission data are stored in browser localStorage, increasing the risk of session hijacking and privilege misuse in the event of client-side compromise.

Summary: Sensitive Token Exposure via Referer Header in Forgot Password Flow
Description:
h1. Sensitive Token Exposure via Referer Header in Forgot Password Flow

----

h3. Affected Endpoint(s):

* {{/img/logo/logo-light-streamline.png}}

----

h3. Severity:

*Medium*

*CVSS Score:* 6.4
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that when a user accesses the forgot password URL received via email, the application loads static resources such as {{/img/logo/logo-light-streamline.png}}. During this request, the full reset URL—including the password reset token embedded in the query string—is transmitted in the {{Referer}} header. This results in sensitive password reset tokens being exposed to any service, CDN, reverse proxy, or logging system handling the image request.

If exploited by an attacker, this behavior could allow interception or logging of valid password reset tokens through web server logs, third-party integrations, analytics tools, or intermediary infrastructure. An attacker with access to these logs or monitoring systems could reuse the exposed reset token within its validity window to perform unauthorized password resets, potentially leading to account takeover.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Initiate a password reset request for a valid account.
# Open the reset link received via email (e.g., {{/reset-password?token=XXXX}}).
# Intercept network traffic using browser developer tools or a proxy.
# Observe the GET request to:
{noformat}/img/logo/logo-light-streamline.png{noformat}
# Inspect the request headers and confirm that the {{Referer}} header contains the full reset URL with the embedded token.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Password reset tokens are included in URL query parameters.
* No {{Referrer-Policy}} header is configured to restrict referrer leakage.
* Sensitive tokens are exposed during resource loading within the same origin.

----

h2. Recommendation

* Avoid placing sensitive tokens in URL query parameters; use POST-based flows or short-lived one-time tokens stored server-side.
* Implement a strict {{Referrer-Policy}} header, such as:
{noformat}Referrer-Policy: no-referrer{noformat}
or
{noformat}Referrer-Policy: same-origin{noformat}
* Ensure reset tokens are single-use and expire immediately after successful password reset.
* Minimize external or third-party resource loading on sensitive pages such as password reset screens.

----

h2. CWE-ID

*CWE-598: Use of GET Request Method With Sensitive Query Strings*

----

h2. OWASP Category

*OWASP Web Top 10:2025*
A06 Insecure Design

----

h2. References

[https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A06_2025-Insecure_Design/|https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A06_2025-Insecure_Design/]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/598.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/598.html]

----

h2. One-Line Summary

Password reset tokens embedded in URLs are exposed via the Referer header during static resource loading, creating a risk of token leakage and potential account takeover.

Summary: APIs Not Verifying JWT Signature Allowing Privilege Escalation and Full Account Compromise
Description:
h1. APIs Not Verifying JWT Signature Allowing Privilege Escalation and Full Account Compromise

----

h3. Affected Endpoint(s):

* {{/api/*}}

----

h3. Severity:

*Critical*

*CVSS Score:* 9.8
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H}}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the backend APIs do not properly verify the JWT signature before processing authenticated requests. During testing, an authenticated API request was captured and replayed with only the {{accessToken}} retained. The endpoint returned {{200 OK}} with valid data. Upon removing the cookie entirely, the API correctly returned {{401 Unauthorized}}, confirming that authentication depends on the access token. However, when the JWT signature portion was removed while retaining the header and payload, the API still returned {{200 OK}} with valid data. This behavior confirms that the server does not validate the integrity or signature of the JWT. Further inspection revealed that the JWT payload only contains three parameters: {{id}}, {{iat}} (issued at), and {{exp}} (expiry). Since the {{id}} field is a predictable natural number integer, it becomes trivial to guess or brute-force other user IDs, including privileged roles such as super-admin.

If exploited by an attacker, this flaw would allow arbitrary token tampering, privilege escalation, and complete authentication bypass. An attacker could modify the {{id}} value in the JWT payload to impersonate any user in the system without possessing the signing secret. Because the signature is not verified, any crafted token would be accepted as valid. This could result in full application compromise, unauthorized access to sensitive data, administrative control, and potential system-wide impact across all API endpoints.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Authenticate and capture a valid API request to:
{noformat}/api/<protected-endpoint>{noformat}
# Replay the request with only the {{accessToken}} cookie/header retained → Response: {{200 OK}}.
# Remove the {{accessToken}} entirely → Response: {{401 Unauthorized}}.
# Re-add the JWT but remove the signature portion (i.e., modify {{header.payload.signature}} to {{header.payload.}}).
# Replay the request → Response: {{200 OK}} with valid data.
# Modify the {{id}} value in the JWT payload to another numeric user ID and resend the request → Observe access to another user’s data (if applicable).

----

h2. Root Cause

* JWT signature verification is not enforced on the server side.
* Absence of cryptographic integrity validation during token parsing.
* Overreliance on client-supplied JWT payload fields.
* Predictable sequential numeric user identifiers.

----

h2. Recommendation

* Enforce strict JWT signature verification using the server-side secret/public key before processing any request.
* Reject tokens with missing or invalid signatures.
* Validate algorithm enforcement (do not allow {{none}} algorithm).
* Avoid trusting user identifiers directly from client-controlled tokens without verification.
* Replace sequential numeric user IDs with non-predictable identifiers (e.g., UUIDs).
* Implement role-based access control validation on every request independent of token claims.
* Conduct a comprehensive review of the authentication middleware implementation.

----

h2. CWE-ID

*CWE-347: Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature*
*CWE-287: Improper Authentication*

----

h2. OWASP Category

*OWASP Web Top 10:2025*
A01 Broken Access Control, A04 Cryptographic Failures, A07 Authentication Failures

*OWASP API Security Top 10:2023*
API2:2023 Broken Authentication, API5:2023 Broken Function Level Authorization 

----

h2. References

[https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A01_2025-Broken_Access_Control/|https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A01_2025-Broken_Access_Control/]

[https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A04_2025-Cryptographic_Failures/|https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A04_2025-Cryptographic_Failures/]

[https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A07_2025-Authentication_Failures/|https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A07_2025-Authentication_Failures/]

[https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa2-broken-authentication/|https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa2-broken-authentication/]

[https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa5-broken-function-level-authorization/|https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa5-broken-function-level-authorization/]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/347.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/347.html]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/287.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/287.html]

----

h2. One-Line Summary

JWT signature verification is not enforced, allowing attackers to forge tokens, escalate privileges, and gain full unauthorized access to the application APIs.

Summary: Guessable JWT Payload Allows Broken Access Control
Description:
h1. Guessable JWT Payload Allows Broken Access Control

----

h3. Affected Endpoint(s):

* {{/api/*}}

----

h3. Severity: *High*

*CVSS Score:* 8.8
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the JWT payload contains only three parameters: {{id}}, {{iat}} (issued at), and {{exp}} (expiry). The {{id}} value corresponds directly to the internal user identifier, which is implemented as a predictable natural number integer. The token does not include role validation claims or contextual authorization constraints, and application authorization logic relies primarily on the {{id}} field provided within the token. Since user IDs are sequential and easily guessable, this creates a predictable identity mapping model.

If exploited by an attacker, a modified token containing a different user {{id}}—such as that of a super-admin—could potentially grant unauthorized access to privileged resources. Because numeric IDs are easily enumerable and brute-forceable, an attacker could attempt privilege escalation by iterating through possible values. If server-side authorization validation is weak or overly dependent on the token’s {{id}} claim, this could lead to horizontal and vertical privilege escalation, resulting in full application compromise.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Authenticate and capture a valid JWT token.
# Decode the JWT and inspect the payload.
# Observe that it contains only:
{noformat}{
  "id": 101,
  "iat": 1700000000,
  "exp": 1700003600
}{noformat}
# Modify the {{id}} value to another valid integer (e.g., {{1}} for super-admin).
# Re-sign the token (if signature validation exists) or replay in cases of weak validation.
# Access privileged API endpoints and observe potential unauthorized access.

----

h2. Root Cause

* JWT payload design exposes predictable user identifiers.
* Authorization decisions rely heavily on client-supplied {{id}} claims.
* Sequential numeric user IDs are used without additional contextual validation.
* Insufficient server-side authorization enforcement.

----

h2. Recommendation

* Do not rely solely on token {{id}} claims for authorization decisions.
* Enforce strict server-side role-based access control (RBAC) independent of client-supplied identifiers.
* Use non-predictable identifiers (e.g., UUIDs) instead of sequential integers.
* Include role claims and validate them against server-side user records.
* Implement object-level authorization checks on all protected endpoints.
* Conduct a full access control review to prevent IDOR-style privilege escalation.

----

h2. CWE-ID

*CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key*
*CWE-284: Improper Access Control*

----

h2. OWASP Category

*OWASP Web Top 10:2025*
A01 Broken Access Control

*OWASP API Security Top 10:2023*
API5:2023 - Broken Function Level Authorization

----

h2. References

[https://owasp.org/Top10/A01_Broken_Access_Control/|https://owasp.org/Top10/A01_Broken_Access_Control/]
[https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa5-broken-function-level-authorization/|https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa5-broken-function-level-authorization/]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/639.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/639.html]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/284.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/284.html]

----

h2. One-Line Summary

Predictable JWT payload design using sequential user IDs enables privilege escalation and broken access control if authorization checks rely on client-controlled claims.

Summary: Cookie Misconfiguration – SameSite Attribute Set to None
Description:
h1. Cookie Misconfiguration – SameSite Attribute Set to {{None}}

----

h3. Affected Domain

* {{https://app.regalsecurity.com}}

----

h3. Severity

*Medium*

*CVSS Score:* 6.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:L/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was identified that authentication cookies are configured with the {{SameSite=None}} attribute.

The {{SameSite}} attribute controls whether browsers include cookies in cross-site requests. When set to {{None}}, cookies are sent with all cross-origin requests, including those initiated from third-party websites.

Unless explicitly required for cross-site functionality (e.g., federated authentication or embedded cross-domain integrations), using {{SameSite=None}} unnecessarily increases exposure to Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks.

If CSRF protections are weak or absent, an attacker could trick an authenticated user into performing unintended actions by causing the victim’s browser to automatically include authentication cookies in malicious cross-site requests.

----

h2. Impact

* Increased exposure to CSRF attacks
* Authentication cookies sent in cross-origin contexts
* Potential unauthorized state-changing requests
* Session misuse if other defenses are not properly implemented

The risk becomes higher if:

* CSRF tokens are missing or improperly validated
* Sensitive endpoints rely solely on cookie-based authentication

----

h2. Root Cause

* Authentication cookies configured with {{SameSite=None}} without strict necessity
* Overly permissive cross-site cookie behavior

----

h2. Recommendation

# Change the cookie attribute from:
{noformat}SameSite=None{noformat}
To:
{noformat}SameSite=Lax{noformat}
# Ensure the following secure cookie attributes are also configured:
#* {{HttpOnly}}
#* {{Secure}}
#* {{SameSite=Lax}} (or {{Strict}} if business logic allows)
# If cross-site usage is required:
#* Implement robust anti-CSRF protections
#* Validate Origin and Referer headers
#* Use CSRF tokens for all state-changing operations

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-352: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)*
* *CWE-1275: Sensitive Cookie with Improper SameSite Attribute*

----

h2. OWASP Category

* *OWASP Top 10:2025 – A02: Security Misconfiguration*

----

h2. One-Line Summary

Authentication cookies configured with {{SameSite=None}} unnecessarily expose the application to CSRF risks and should be restricted to {{SameSite=Lax}} unless cross-site behavior is strictly required.

Summary: Missing CSRF Protection on Sensitive API Endpoints
Description:
h1. Missing CSRF Protection on Sensitive API Endpoints

----

h3. Affected Endpoints

* {{/api/permissions/*}}
* {{/api/roles/*}}
* {{/api/change-password}}
* Other authenticated state-changing endpoints

----

h3. Severity

*Medium*

*CVSS Score:* 4.6
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was identified that sensitive state-changing API endpoints—including role management, permission updates, and password change functionality—do not implement anti-CSRF protections.

The application relies on cookie-based authentication, and these endpoints accept requests without requiring:

* CSRF tokens
* Origin header validation
* Referer header validation

In the absence of CSRF mitigations, an attacker can craft a malicious webpage that triggers authenticated requests from a victim’s browser while the victim is logged in. Since browsers automatically attach session cookies to requests, the server processes these forged requests as legitimate.

----

h2. Impact

An attacker could cause an authenticated user to unintentionally:

* Modify roles or permissions
* Change account passwords
* Update account configurations

Successful exploitation depends on social engineering (user interaction required), which reduces overall exploitability but still presents a meaningful integrity risk—particularly if administrative users are targeted.

----

h2. Root Cause

* No anti-CSRF token implementation
* Missing Origin/Referer validation
* Reliance on cookie-based authentication without request-level validation

----

h2. Recommendation

# Implement the Synchronizer Token Pattern for all state-changing requests (POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE).
# Validate CSRF tokens server-side on every request.
# Enforce {{SameSite=Lax}} (or {{Strict}}) for authentication cookies where business logic permits.
# Validate {{Origin}} and {{Referer}} headers for sensitive endpoints.
# Conduct a broader review of authentication and request validation mechanisms.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-352: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)*

----

h2. OWASP Category

* OWASP Top 10:2025 – A06: Insecure Design

----

h2. References

[https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A02_2025-Security_Misconfiguration/|https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A02_2025-Security_Misconfiguration/]

[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/352.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/352.html]

----

h2. One-Line Summary

Sensitive authenticated endpoints lack CSRF protections, enabling cross-site request forgery attacks that can manipulate user roles, permissions, or account credentials.

Summary: Session Does Not Expire Upon Password Change
Description:
h1. Session Does Not Expire Upon Password Change

----

h3. Affected Endpoint(s):

* {{/api/*}}
* [https://app.regalsecurity.com/|https://app.regalsecurity.com/]

----

h3. Severity:

*High*

*CVSS Score:* 8.8
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N}}

----

h2. Description

It was observed that active user sessions remain valid even after a successful password change. When a user updates their account password, previously issued session tokens and authentication cookies are not invalidated, allowing existing sessions across browsers or devices to continue functioning without requiring re-authentication.

If exploited by an attacker who has already obtained a valid session token through session hijacking, device compromise, or shared system access, the attacker would continue to maintain unauthorized access even after the legitimate user changes their password. This could lead to persistent account compromise, unauthorized data access, fraudulent transactions, and potential regulatory non-compliance due to failure in enforcing secure session lifecycle management.

Note : The same logic applies when super-admin changes password for the user, or forgot password functionality is used to update the password.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application in Browser A.
# Capture the active session cookie/token.
# Change the account password via {{/account/change-password}}.
# Use the previously captured session token in Browser B or replay it via a proxy.
# Observe that access to authenticated endpoints such as {{/dashboard}} remains valid.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Session invalidation logic is not triggered upon password change.
* The application does not revoke or rotate existing session tokens after credential modification.

----

h2. Solution

* Invalidate all active sessions immediately after a successful password change.
* Revoke and rotate all session tokens associated with the user account.
* Implement centralized server-side session management with active session tracking.
* Force re-authentication across all devices after credential updates.
* Log and notify users regarding session revocation events.

----

h2. CWE-ID

*CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration*

----

h2. OWASP Category

*OWASP Web Top 10:2025*
A07 Authentication Failures

----

h2. References

[https://owasp.org/Top10/A07_Authentication_Failures/|https://owasp.org/Top10/A07_Authentication_Failures/]
[https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/613.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/613.html]

----

h2. One-Line Summary

Active sessions remain valid after password change, allowing persistent unauthorized access if a session token has been previously compromised.

Summary: Access Token Remains Valid After Logout
Description:
h2. Access Token Remains Valid After Logout

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 7.5
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application does not invalidate active access tokens upon user logout. After a user logs out from the application interface, previously issued access tokens remain valid until their natural expiration time.

During testing, an authenticated session was established and the access token was captured. After performing a logout operation through the application, the captured access token was reused to access protected API endpoints. The backend continued to accept the token and returned valid responses.

This behavior indicates that logout functionality is handled only at the client side and does not enforce server-side token invalidation or revocation.

----

h2. Impact

* *Session Persistence After Logout:*
Users remain effectively authenticated despite explicitly logging out.
* *Token Reuse Risk:*
If an access token is compromised (e.g., via XSS, proxy interception, shared machine access), it can be reused even after logout.
* *Account Takeover Window:*
Attackers can maintain access until token expiration.
* *Weak Incident Containment:*
Logout does not function as a reliable security control.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in to the application and capture the access token.
# Perform logout via the application interface.
# Send an authenticated API request using the previously captured access token.
# Observe that the request is processed successfully despite logout.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Logout invalidates tokens only on the client side.
* No server-side token revocation mechanism is implemented.
* Access tokens are considered valid solely based on signature and expiry time.

----

h2. Solution

Implement one or more of the following controls:

* *Token Revocation Mechanism:*
Maintain a token blacklist or revocation store for invalidated tokens.
* *Token Versioning (Recommended):*
Maintain a per-user {{token_version}} and increment it on logout.
Reject tokens with outdated versions.
* *Short-Lived Access Tokens:*
Reduce access token validity and rely on secure refresh mechanisms.
* *Invalidate Refresh Tokens on Logout:*
Ensure no new access tokens can be generated post-logout.

Logout must invalidate effective access immediately, not rely solely on token expiry.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-613:* Insufficient Session Expiration
* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control

----

h2. OWASP Top 10 (Web 2025)

* *A01 – Broken Access Control*

----

h3. One-Line Summary

{quote}Access tokens remain valid after logout, allowing continued authenticated API access until token expiration.{quote}

Summary: Broken Access Control in Branch-Based Data Segregation (IDOR via Token Reuse)
Description:
h2. Broken Access Control in Branch-Based Data Segregation (IDOR via Token Reuse)

*Affected Endpoint(s):*
/api/v1/site-groups-general/sg_{id}

/api/v1/branch-general/bg_{id}

/api/v1/employee-general/eg_{id}

*Severity:* Critical
*CVSS Score:* 9.1
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N

----

h2. Description

It was observed that branch-level access control is not properly enforced at the backend. The application allows users to be restricted to a specific branch; however, authorization checks are not consistently validated against branch mappings during API processing.

During testing, two users were configured:

* User A: Access to a single branch only
* User B: Access to all branches

An API request for branch-specific resources (e.g., Employees, Branches, Customers) was captured using User B’s token. The request was then replayed using User A’s access token (restricted to a single branch). Despite the restricted permissions, the API returned valid data from branches outside User A’s authorized scope.

This demonstrates that the backend does not enforce object-level or branch-level authorization controls and relies on client-side restrictions or incomplete role validation.

*Note :* Working for GET endpoints/method only.

----

h2. Impact

* *Unauthorized Access to Sensitive PII:*
Exposure of employee details, customer information, and branch-specific records beyond assigned scope.
* *Privilege Escalation:*
Restricted users can access data intended only for higher-privileged roles.
* *Data Segregation Failure:*
Multi-branch data isolation is ineffective, breaking tenancy boundaries.
* *Compliance Risk:*
Unauthorized exposure of customer and employee data may violate privacy and regulatory requirements.
* *Systemic Authorization Weakness:*
Similar flaws may exist across other branch-scoped APIs.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Configure:
#* User A with access to a single branch.
#* User B with access to all branches.
# Log in as User B.
# Capture an API request fetching branch-specific resources.
# Replace User B’s access token with User A’s token.
# Replay the request.
# Observe that the API returns data outside User A’s authorized branch.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Missing backend enforcement of branch-to-user authorization mapping.
* Lack of object-level access control validation.
* Authorization logic does not validate resource ownership or branch scope before returning data.

----

h2. Solution

* Enforce strict backend authorization checks:
** Validate that the authenticated user is mapped to the requested branch.
** Deny access if branch ownership validation fails.
* Implement object-level access control (BOLA prevention).
* Avoid trusting client-supplied branch identifiers.
* Add centralized authorization middleware for branch-scoped resources.
* Log and monitor cross-branch access attempts.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-639:* Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control

----

h2. OWASP Top 10 (Web 2025)

* *A01 – Broken Access Control*

----

h3. One-Line Summary

{quote}Branch-level authorization is not enforced at the backend, allowing restricted users to access sensitive cross-branch data by replaying API requests with manipulated tokens.{quote}

Summary: Server Name and Version Disclosure (Nginx 1.24.0)
Description:
h2. Server Name and Version Disclosure (Nginx 1.24.0)

*Affected Endpoint(s):*
{{/api/*}}

*Severity:* Low
*CVSS Score:* 3.2
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application exposes server banner information in HTTP responses. Specifically, the {{Server}} header discloses the underlying web server software and version:

{noformat}Server: nginx/1.24.0{noformat}

This information is returned for API endpoints under {{/api/*}}. Exposing server name and version details provides attackers with insights into the underlying infrastructure and may assist in reconnaissance and targeted exploitation efforts.

Although this issue does not directly result in compromise, it increases the application's attack surface by revealing implementation details.

----

h2. Impact

* *Information Disclosure:*
Reveals the web server type and exact version in use.
* *Targeted Exploitation Risk:*
Attackers can correlate the disclosed version with known vulnerabilities affecting that specific server release.
* *Reconnaissance Facilitation:*
Assists attackers in profiling the application stack during initial enumeration.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Send an HTTP request to any endpoint under:
{noformat}/api/*{noformat}
# Inspect the response headers.
# Observe the {{Server}} header disclosing:
{noformat}nginx/1.24.0{noformat}

----

h2. Root Cause

* Default server configuration exposes software name and version.
* Server banner obfuscation or suppression is not configured.

----

h2. Solution

* Disable server version disclosure in Nginx configuration:
{noformat}server_tokens off;{noformat}
* Optionally remove or override the {{Server}} header using appropriate modules.
* Ensure similar banner suppression is configured for upstream services or reverse proxies.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-200:* Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

----

h2. OWASP Top 10 (Web 2025)

* *A02 – Security Misconfiguration*

----

h2. References

* [https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A02_2025-Security_Misconfiguration/|https://owasp.org/Top10/2025/A02_2025-Security_Misconfiguration/]
* [https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html]

----

h3. One-Line Summary

{quote}API responses disclose web server software and version ({{nginx/1.24.0}}), enabling infrastructure fingerprinting and reconnaissance.{quote}

Summary: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) in Role Management API
Description:
h2. Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) in Role Management API

*Affected Endpoint:*
{{/api/v1/roles}}

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.1
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:L

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the Role CRUD API ({{/api/v1/roles}}) is vulnerable to Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR). Although authentication is required to access the portal, the backend does not properly enforce authorization checks to validate whether the authenticated user is permitted to access, modify, or delete specific role records.

By manipulating role identifiers in API requests, an authenticated user can access or perform operations on role objects beyond their assigned privileges. The backend processes the request successfully without verifying ownership or privilege boundaries.

This indicates that access control is either insufficiently implemented or enforced only at the frontend level.

----

h2. Impact

* *Privilege Escalation:*
Attackers may modify role definitions to grant elevated permissions.
* *Unauthorized Role Manipulation:*
Roles may be viewed, edited, or deleted by users lacking appropriate authorization.
* *System-Wide Security Impact:*
Since roles define access permissions across the application, compromise may lead to broad unauthorized access.
* *Integrity Violation:*
Modification of role-permission mappings can disrupt the security model of the entire system.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Authenticate as a valid but lower-privileged user.
# Intercept a request to:
{noformat}/api/v1/roles
{noformat}
# Modify the role identifier in the request payload or URL.
# Forward the request.
# Observe that the role object is accessed or modified successfully without proper authorization checks.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Missing object-level authorization validation.
* Backend does not verify whether the authenticated user has permission to access or modify the specific role resource.
* Reliance on frontend restrictions rather than enforcing access control at the API layer.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement strict *role-based access control (RBAC)* checks at the backend.
* Validate that the authenticated user:
** Has sufficient privileges to perform the requested action.
** Is authorized to access the specific role object.
* Enforce object-level authorization for all CRUD operations.
* Implement centralized authorization middleware to prevent similar issues across endpoints.
* Log and monitor unauthorized access attempts.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-639:* Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control

----

h2. OWASP Top 10 (Web 2025)

* *A01 – Broken Access Control*

----

h3. One-Line Summary

{quote}{{/api/v1/roles}} is vulnerable to IDOR, allowing authenticated users to access or manipulate role objects beyond their authorized scope.{quote}

Summary: Improper Error Handling on Unexpected Input
Description:
h2. Improper Error Handling on Unexpected Input

*Severity:* Low
*CVSS Score:* 3.1
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:L}}

h3. *Description:*

The application responds with a {{500 Internal Server Error}} when provided with unexpected or malformed input instead of returning appropriate {{4xx}} client-side error responses such as {{400 Bad Request}} or {{422 Unprocessable Entity}}.

This behavior indicates a lack of proper input validation or error handling mechanisms on the server side. A {{500}} error suggests that the server encountered an unhandled exception, which could reveal implementation flaws or open the door for more severe exploitation if deeper error contexts are exposed.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Denial of Service Potential:* Repeated malformed inputs can lead to application crashes or resource exhaustion due to unhandled exceptions.
* *Information Disclosure Risk:* If verbose error messages are enabled, they might inadvertently reveal stack traces, file paths, or internal logic.
* *Reduced Security Posture:* Lack of graceful error handling can serve as a reconnaissance tool for attackers to identify vulnerable endpoints.

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Interact with any API endpoint that expects a specific input schema.
# Submit unexpected data types, malformed JSON, or out-of-bound values.
# Observe the HTTP response code and confirm if a {{500}} error is returned.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Insufficient input validation and sanitization.
* Lack of structured exception handling for invalid client requests.

h3. *Solution:*

* Implement comprehensive input validation on all API endpoints before processing.
* Use structured exception handling to catch and respond to unexpected input gracefully.
* Ensure that invalid client requests return appropriate {{4xx}} responses, not {{5xx}} server errors.
* Disable verbose error responses in production environments to prevent leakage of internal logic.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-388:* Error Handling
* *CWE-209:* Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API8:2023 – Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: Excessive Refresh Token Lifespan
Description:
h2. Excessive Refresh Token Lifespan

*Severity:* Low
*CVSS Score:* 3.7
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N}}

h3. *Description:*

It was observed that the backend issues refresh tokens with a lifespan of *one month*, which is significantly longer than recommended for typical authentication systems. Extended validity of refresh tokens increases the window of opportunity for misuse if a token is compromised.

A refresh token, by design, should have a reasonably short lifespan and be stored securely. However, in the event of theft, a long-lived token allows unauthorized users to continue generating new access tokens for an extended period without detection.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Prolonged Risk Window:* If an attacker gains access to the refresh token, they can maintain access to the account for a full month without needing credentials again.
* *Bypass of Short Access Token Expiry:* While short-lived access tokens reduce surface area, they are ineffective if the refresh token remains valid for too long.
* *Increased Threat Persistence:* Token theft through XSS, local storage compromise, or insecure transmission channels becomes more damaging.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Refresh token expiration is set to an excessively long duration (30 days).
* No rotating or revocation mechanism is in place post-authentication events like logout or password change.

h3. *Solution:*

* Reduce the *refresh token validity to 7 days or less* based on business needs.
* Implement *refresh token rotation* and detection of anomalous usage (e.g., reuse of old tokens).
* Revoke all refresh tokens on critical actions such as password change, device de-registration, or logout.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-1255:* Insufficient Session Expiration

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API8:2023 - Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: Insecure Client-Side Storage of Sensitive Administrative Data
Description:
h2. *Vulnerability: Insecure Client-Side Storage of Sensitive Administrative Data*

*Severity:* Critical
*CVSS Score:* 9.6
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* response of my profile

----

h2. *Description*

It was observed that the administrative web panel stores highly sensitive information within the browser’s Local Storage. The stored data includes user permissions and access rights assigned to the currently logged-in user, session state indicators, user profile details such as name and email address, and an authentication access token that remains valid for up to 24 hours.

Local Storage is inherently insecure for storing such sensitive data because it is directly accessible via client-side JavaScript, does not support HTTP-only protections, and lacks automatic expiration or secure scoping mechanisms. If an attacker gains the ability to execute JavaScript in the victim’s browser—via Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), a malicious browser extension, compromised dependency, or shared workstation access—all stored values can be extracted instantly. The exposure of a long-lived access token combined with stored authorization context significantly increases the likelihood of full administrative account compromise.

----

h2. *Impact*

* *Administrative Account Takeover:* The exposed access token can be reused to impersonate an admin user for up to 24 hours.
* *Privilege Abuse:* Stored permission data enables attackers to understand and abuse high-privilege capabilities without additional enumeration.
* *Sensitive Information Disclosure:* Exposure of personally identifiable information (PII) such as name and email addresses.
* *Persistent Session Hijacking:* Local Storage persists across browser restarts, extending the attack window.
* *Chained Attacks:* Any XSS vulnerability immediately escalates to token theft and admin compromise.

----

h2. *Steps to Reproduce*

# Log in to the administrative panel using valid credentials.
# Open the browser’s Developer Tools.
# Navigate to *Application → Local Storage*.
# Observe stored entries containing:
#* User permissions and roles
#* Session state flags
#* User details (name, email)
#* Access token with a 24-hour validity
# Copy the access token and replay it in API requests or another browser session.

----

h2. *Root Cause*

* Sensitive authentication and authorization artifacts are stored in client-side Local Storage.
* Access tokens are long-lived and not adequately protected against client-side compromise.
* Absence of secure session handling mechanisms such as HTTP-only cookies.
* Insufficient client-side security threat modeling.

----

h2. *Solution*

* Remove all sensitive data from Local Storage, including access tokens, permissions, and session state.
* Store authentication tokens in secure, HTTP-only, SameSite cookies.
* Reduce access token lifespan and implement token rotation and refresh mechanisms.
* Invalidate sessions immediately on logout, password changes, or role updates.
* Avoid exposing authorization metadata (permissions/roles) to the client unless strictly required.
* Strengthen XSS protections across the admin panel.

----

h2. *CWE-ID*

* *CWE-922:* Insecure Storage of Sensitive Information
* *CWE-312:* Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information

----

h2. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category*

*API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*
Improper storage of authentication tokens and authorization context compromises session security and enables unauthorized access to protected API resources.

----

h2. *OWASP Web Application Top 10 (2025) Category*

*A02:2025 – Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: Administrative Access Token Remains Active After Logout
Description:
h2. *Vulnerability: Administrative Access Token Remains Active After Logout*

*Severity:* Critical 
*CVSS Score:* 9.2
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Logout Functionality

----

h2. *Description*

It was observed that the administrative access token remains valid and usable even after the logout operation is performed. The application does not invalidate or revoke the active admin session token upon logout.

As a result, logging out does not effectively terminate the authenticated session. Any previously issued administrative token continues to grant access until it expires naturally, indicating improper session termination handling within the authentication lifecycle.

----

h2. *Impact*

* *Administrative Session Persistence:* An attacker or unauthorized party in possession of the token can continue accessing administrative functionality after logout.
* *Session Hijacking Risk:* Stolen or leaked tokens remain usable beyond the intended session boundary.
* *Unauthorized Administrative Access:* Failure to invalidate tokens enables continued privileged access without re-authentication.

----

h2. *Steps to Reproduce*

# Authenticate to the application as an administrative user.
# Capture the issued access token.
# Perform a logout action from the application.
# Reuse the previously captured access token to access protected administrative resources.
# Observe that access is still granted.

----

h2. *Root Cause*

* Logout functionality does not invalidate or revoke issued administrative access tokens.
* Absence of server-side session termination enforcement.

----

h2. *Solution*

* Invalidate access tokens immediately upon logout.
* Implement server-side session revocation mechanisms.
* Enforce re-authentication for all protected administrative actions after logout.
* Monitor and log token reuse after logout events.

----

h2. *CWE-ID*

* *CWE-613:* Insufficient Session Expiration

----

h2. *OWASP Web Top 10 (2021) Category*

*A2:2021 – Broken Authentication*
Improper session termination allows authenticated tokens to remain valid beyond logout, enabling unauthorized continued access.

----

h3. *Administrative Access Token Remains Active After Logout*

*Category:* *A07 – Authentication Failures*
Failure to invalidate authentication tokens on logout represents improper session lifecycle management, directly impacting authentication and session termination guarantees.

Summary: Insecure Direct Object Reference in Role Activation API
Description:
h2. *Vulnerability: Insecure Direct Object Reference in Role Activation API*

*Severity:* Medium
*Priority:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 6.9
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Role Activation API

----

h2. *Description*

It was observed that the role activation API is vulnerable to an Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) condition. The API allows role activation actions to be performed based solely on the request parameters without enforcing sufficient object-level authorization checks.

If the API endpoint and a valid authentication token are leaked or compromised, an unauthorized actor can directly invoke the role activation functionality. The application does not adequately verify whether the requesting user is permitted to activate or modify the targeted role.

----

h2. *Impact*

* *User Lockout:* Unauthorized role activation or deactivation can result in loss of access for multiple or all users.
* *Authorization Bypass:* Attackers can manipulate role states without appropriate privileges.
* *Operational Disruption:* Improper role changes can disrupt normal application access and administrative control.

----

h2. *Steps to Reproduce*

# Authenticate to the application and obtain a valid access token.
# Identify the role activation API endpoint.
# Modify the role identifier or target object in the request.
# Send the request using the valid token.
# Observe that the role activation action is performed without proper authorization validation.

----

h2. *Root Cause*

* Missing object-level authorization checks in the role activation API.
* Reliance on token validity without verifying role ownership or privilege scope.

----

h2. *Solution*

* Enforce strict object-level authorization checks for role activation actions.
* Validate that the requesting user is explicitly permitted to modify the targeted role.
* Restrict role activation APIs to privileged administrative contexts only.
* Log and monitor all role state changes for abuse detection.

----

h2. *CWE-ID*

* *CWE-639:* Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

----

h2. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category*

*API1:2023 – Broken Object Level Authorization*
Failure to enforce object-level authorization allows unauthorized manipulation of role-related resources.

----

h3. *Insecure Direct Object Reference in Role Activation API*

*Category:* *A01 – Broken Access Control*

The role activation API does not enforce object-level authorization checks, allowing unauthorized manipulation of role resources when identifiers or tokens are abused.

Summary: Missing Multiple Security Headers [Devops]
Description:
h1. *Missing Multiple Security Headers*

----

*Severity :* High

*CVSS Score :* 7.8

*Vector :* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:L/A:N

----

h2. Description

It was observed that the application responses do not include multiple industry-recommended HTTP security headers. These headers are designed to enforce browser-side security controls and act as a defensive layer against common client-side attack vectors.

In the absence of these headers, browsers apply default and permissive behaviors, which weakens protections against clickjacking, MIME-type sniffing, reflected and DOM-based XSS, insecure content loading, and unintended information disclosure through referrer leakage. This issue affects both the administrative panel and user-facing endpoints.

While the absence of security headers may not immediately result in a standalone exploit, it significantly increases the likelihood and impact of exploitation when combined with other vulnerabilities such as XSS, open redirects, or third-party script compromise.

----

h2. Impact

* *Increased XSS Exploitability:* Missing {{Content-Security-Policy}} allows unrestricted script execution if injection occurs.
* *Clickjacking Risk:* Absence of {{X-Frame-Options}} enables UI redressing attacks.
* *MIME Sniffing Attacks:* Missing {{X-Content-Type-Options}} allows browsers to interpret malicious content as executable.
* *Information Leakage:* Missing {{Referrer-Policy}} may leak sensitive URLs and tokens to external origins.
* *Weakened Transport Security:* Absence of {{Strict-Transport-Security}} enables SSL-strip downgrade scenarios.
* *Reduced Defense-in-Depth:* Browser-level security controls are not enforced, increasing overall attack surface.

----

h2. Affected Headers (Observed Missing)

* {{Content-Security-Policy}}
* {{X-Frame-Options}}
* {{X-Content-Type-Options}}
* {{Referrer-Policy}}
* {{Strict-Transport-Security}}
* {{Permissions-Policy}}

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Send a request to any application endpoint.
# Inspect the HTTP response headers.
# Observe that one or more recommended security headers are missing.
# Attempt browser-based attacks (e.g., framing, MIME sniffing, injected scripts) which are not restricted by policy.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Security headers are not centrally enforced at the application, API gateway, or reverse proxy level.
* Lack of a standardized baseline security hardening configuration for HTTP responses.
* Security controls rely solely on application logic without browser-enforced safeguards.

----

h2. Solution

* Implement the following HTTP security headers across all application endpoints:
** *Content-Security-Policy:* Restrict script, style, image, and frame sources to trusted origins.
** *X-Frame-Options:* Set to {{DENY}} or {{SAMEORIGIN}}.
** *X-Content-Type-Options:* Set to {{nosniff}}.
** *Referrer-Policy:* Use {{strict-origin-when-cross-origin}} or {{no-referrer}} as per business need.
** *Strict-Transport-Security:* Enforce HTTPS with appropriate {{max-age}} and {{includeSubDomains}}.
** *Permissions-Policy:* Disable unnecessary browser features (camera, microphone, geolocation, etc.).
* Apply headers at a centralized layer (reverse proxy / gateway / middleware) to ensure consistency.
* Validate header effectiveness using browser developer tools and automated security scanners.

----

h2. CWE-ID

* *CWE-693* – Protection Mechanism Failure
* *CWE-1021* – Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames

----

h2. OWASP Top 10 (Web 2025) Category

*A05:2025 – Security Misconfiguration*

Improper or missing security hardening measures, including absent HTTP security headers, weaken browser-enforced protections and increase the likelihood of client-side attacks and exploit chaining.

Summary: Password Fields Allow Copy-Paste During Password Update
Description:
h2. *Password Fields Allow Copy-Paste During Password Update*

*Severity:* Medium
*Priority:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 5.2
*Vulnerable Endpoint:* Update Password Functionality

----

h2. *Description*

It was observed that the application’s password update functionality allows users to copy and paste data within the *Old Password*, *New Password*, and *Confirm New Password* fields. The application does not restrict clipboard operations for sensitive password inputs during the password change workflow.

Allowing copy and paste operations in password fields increases the likelihood of user errors during password updates. If a user mistypes the new password and subsequently pastes the same incorrect value into the confirm password field, the application accepts the change without additional validation or warning. This behavior can result in unintended password updates that the user may not recall.

----

h2. *Impact*

* *User Account Lockout:* Users may unintentionally set an incorrect password and lose access to their account.
* *Poor Security Hygiene:* Permitting clipboard operations on password fields increases the risk of password exposure through clipboard monitoring malware or shared environments.
* *Increased Dependency on Recovery Flows:* Although a forgot password mechanism exists, frequent reliance on it indicates suboptimal password handling design.

----

h2. *Steps to Reproduce*

# Navigate to the application’s update password functionality.
# Enter the current password in the *Old Password* field.
# Enter a new password with a typing mistake in the *New Password* field.
# Copy the value entered in the *New Password* field.
# Paste the copied value into the *Confirm New Password* field.
# Submit the password update request and observe that the password change is accepted.

----

h2. *Root Cause*

* Password input fields allow unrestricted copy and paste operations.
* Lack of additional validation or safeguards to detect accidental password entry errors during updates.

----

h2. *Solution*

* Disable copy and paste operations for sensitive password input fields.
* Implement additional client-side and server-side validation to reduce accidental password mismatches.
* Provide clear user feedback or confirmation mechanisms during password changes.

----

h2. *CWE-ID*

* *CWE-522:* Insufficiently Protected Credentials

----

h2. *OWASP Web Top 10 (2021) Category*

*A2:2021 – Broken Authentication*
Weak password handling controls within authentication-related functionality can negatively impact account security and user access reliability.

----

h2. *Password Fields Allow Copy-Paste During Password Update*

*Category:* *A06 – Insecure Design*

Allowing unrestricted clipboard operations in password fields reflects a design decision that does not adequately account for usability-driven security risks and credential handling best practices.

Summary: Active Sessions Not Invalidated After Password Change
Description:
*Active Sessions Not Invalidated After Password Change*

*Severity:* Medium
*Priority:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 6.4
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:H/A:N

*Description*

It was observed that when a user changes their account password, existing authenticated sessions remain active and are not invalidated. Any previously issued access tokens or active browser sessions continue to function until their natural expiration.

This behavior allows users or attackers with prior access to maintain authenticated sessions even after credentials have been changed. Password changes are commonly performed in response to suspected compromise; however, without invalidating existing sessions, the intended security benefit of the password change is significantly reduced.

This issue affects both standard and privileged users and weakens the application’s session lifecycle management.

*Impact*

* *Persistent Unauthorized Access:* An attacker with an existing session can continue to access the account after a password change.
* *Ineffective Incident Response:* Password changes do not terminate attacker activity during compromise scenarios.
* *Account Takeover Risk:* Stolen tokens remain valid until expiry.
* *User Trust Impact:* Users may assume password change secures the account when it does not.

*Steps to Reproduce*

# Log in to the application using valid credentials in Browser A.
# Open a second browser or device and authenticate using the same account.
# Change the account password from one of the sessions.
# Observe that the other session remains authenticated and continues to access protected functionality without interruption.

*Root Cause*

* Active authentication tokens and sessions are not revoked upon password change.
* Lack of centralized session invalidation logic.
* Password change flow does not trigger token revocation or session termination.

*Solution*

* Invalidate all active sessions and authentication tokens immediately after a password change.
* Force re-authentication across all devices once the password is updated.
* Implement server-side token revocation for access and refresh tokens.
* Log and monitor password change events and session invalidation actions.
* Clearly notify users that all sessions have been logged out after password updates.

*CWE-ID*

* *CWE-613:* Insufficient Session Expiration

*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2025)*

*A02:2025 – Authentication Failures*
Failure to terminate active sessions after credential changes weakens authentication controls and allows continued unauthorized access.

Summary: Cross-Tenant Access to Sensitive Medical Reports via Reusable Signed URLs
Description:
h2. Cross-Tenant Access to Sensitive Medical Reports via Reusable Signed URLs

*Severity:* Critical
*Priority:* Critical

*CVSS Score:* 9.1
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:N/A:N

----

h2. Description

It was observed that *medical case reports containing sensitive patient and PII data* are retrieved from *cloud-based static storage* using *signed URLs*.

While access to the reports requires:

* A valid *authentication token*, and
* A valid *signed URL parameter*

the signed URLs are *not bound to a specific client, tenant, or case owner*.

As a result, *any authenticated user* possessing a *valid signed URL* can successfully access the report, regardless of whether the report belongs to their assigned tenant or client.

Since signed URLs can be:

* Logged,
* Shared,
* Cached,
* Leaked via browser history, emails, tickets, or third-party tools,

*cross-tenant access cannot be technically prevented once the URL is disclosed*, even unintentionally.

This behavior is especially critical given that the application is a *medical case handling system used for regulatory compliance submissions*, and the exposed reports contain *highly sensitive medical and personally identifiable information (PII)*.

----

h2. Impact

h3. Unauthorized Cross-Tenant Data Access

Authenticated users can access medical reports belonging to other clients/tenants if a valid signed URL is obtained.

h3. Exposure of Sensitive Medical & PII Data

Reports may contain:

* Patient identifiers
* Medical case details
* Regulatory submissions

This poses a *severe privacy and confidentiality risk*.

h3. Regulatory & Legal Non-Compliance

This issue may lead to violations of:

* Medical data protection requirements
* Data minimization and access control principles
* Regulatory confidentiality obligations

h3. Breach Escalation Risk

Any signed URL leak effectively becomes a *direct object reference*, bypassing tenant-level authorization controls.

----

h2. Steps to Reproduce

# Authenticate as a valid user under *Tenant A*.
# Obtain a signed URL for a medical report belonging to *Tenant B* (via logs, sharing, or indirect exposure).
# Send a GET request using:
#* The valid signed URL
#* A valid authentication token
# Observe that the report is successfully retrieved despite tenant mismatch.

----

h2. Root Cause

* Signed URLs are generated *without tenant or client binding*.
* Backend authorization does not validate:
** Tenant ownership
** Case ownership
** Client-to-report relationship
* Reliance on signed URLs as an access control mechanism rather than as a *temporary transport mechanism*.

----

h2. Recommendation / Solution

h3. Short-Term Mitigations

* Reduce signed URL validity duration to the minimum possible.
* Disable caching of signed URLs at browser and proxy levels.

h3. Long-Term / Secure Design Fix

* Enforce *server-side tenant validation* before issuing signed URLs.
* Bind signed URLs to:
** Tenant ID
** Case ID
** User context
* Implement a *proxy-based download flow*, where:
** Requests hit the backend first
** Authorization is enforced
** The file is streamed securely
* Log and monitor signed URL usage with anomaly detection.

----

h2. CWE-ID

*CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key*
*CWE-284: Improper Access Control*

----

h2. OWASP Top 10 (Web 2025)

*A01 – Broken Access Control*

h2. Business Risk Statement

Although the application is restricted to internal employees, the current report access design allows *cross-tenant exposure of sensitive medical and patient-related information* when a valid signed URL is available. Internal user status does not equate to authorization to access all client or tenant data, particularly in a regulated medical case-handling environment.

Any unintended disclosure or reuse of a signed report URL—through internal communication channels, logging, or account compromise—can result in *unauthorized access to medical and PII data across tenants*. Such access may go undetected and could impact *regulatory compliance, audit integrity, and organizational accountability*.

Given the sensitivity of the data and the critical nature of the system, even limited internal misuse or accidental exposure presents a *material business and compliance risk*, warranting corrective controls to enforce explicit tenant-level authorization.

Summary: Improper Client Isolation in Module-Wise Audit Trails
Description:
*Vulnerability:* Improper Client Isolation in Module-Wise Audit Trails

*Severity:* Medium
*Priority:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 6.5
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N

----

h3. Description

It was observed that module-wise audit trails intended to be *client-specific* are not properly segregated at the backend. Audit trail data for one client is visible to other clients within the same module.

During testing, audit entries generated under one client were displayed identically when accessing the Audit Trails module for a different client. This behavior was observed across *client-specific sub-modules* configured under the Audit Trails section.

However, *System Audit Trails* and *Login Audit Logs* are expected to be global in nature and were found to behave consistently across all clients, which is acceptable. The issue specifically affects *client-scoped audit trail modules*, where tenant-level isolation is expected but not enforced.

This indicates missing or improper client-level filtering and access control in the audit logging mechanism.

----

h3. Impact

*Confidentiality Risk:*
Audit logs may expose sensitive operational activities of one client to another, violating tenant isolation.

*Compliance & Accountability Issues:*
Audit trails are critical for compliance, investigations, and traceability. Cross-client visibility undermines their reliability and legal defensibility.

*Broken Access Control:*
Users may gain unintended visibility into another client’s actions without explicit authorization.

----

h3. Steps to Reproduce

# Log in as an authorized user under *Client A*.
# Perform actions that generate audit trail entries in a client-specific module.
# Switch context or log in as an authorized user under *Client B*.
# Navigate to the same client-specific Audit Trails module.
# Observe that audit entries from *Client A* are visible for *Client B*.

----

h3. Root Cause

* Missing client/tenant identifier filtering in audit trail queries.
* Improper backend enforcement of tenant-level access control for module-wise audit logs.
* Over-reliance on shared audit log storage without contextual segregation.

----

h3. Solution

* Enforce strict *client/tenant-based filtering* for all client-specific audit trail modules.
* Ensure audit logs include and are queried using a *unique client identifier*.
* Validate access control at the backend to prevent cross-client log exposure.
* Clearly document which audit logs are *global* (e.g., System Audit Trails, Login Logs) versus *client-scoped*.

----

h3. CWE-ID

*CWE-284: Improper Access Control*

----

h3. OWASP Top 10 (Web 2025)

*A01 – Broken Access Control*
