Summary: WEB - Username Enumeration via Login Response
Description:
h2. Username Enumeration via Login Response

*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 6.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N}}

h3. *Affected Endpoint:* [*https://yourelocker.in/api/auth/callback/credentials*|https://yourelocker.in/api/auth/callback/credentials] *(Web) ;* [*https://yourelocker.in/api/common/get-admins*|https://yourelocker.in/api/common/get-admins] *(Web)*

h3. *Description:*

The web application uses Next.js-based session handling ({{__Secure-next-auth.session-token}}) for authentication. Two distinct user roles were identified: *Admin* and *Article User*. Each login flow has a separate endpoint and observable behavioral differences that introduce username enumeration risks.

* *Admin Panel:*
** Upon submitting valid credentials, the server sets a secure session token and redirects to a valid admin dashboard.
** For an invalid *password*, the application returns a URL like:
{noformat}https://scope.com/api/auth/error?error=Incorrect%20Password.%20Please%20try%20again!{noformat}
** For an invalid *email*, the application returns:
{noformat}https://scope.com/api/auth/error?error=No%20user%20found! {noformat}
* *Article User Panel:*
** Uses an initial API ({{get-admins}}) to confirm whether a given admin (typically an Indian first name) exists.
** If the admin does not exist, a direct error is returned: {{No user found!}}.
** If the admin is found, the client is prompted to select a company name (admin organization) before continuing the login process.

The use of role-specific APIs and distinct response messages for each failure case enables an attacker to programmatically enumerate:

* Valid admin email addresses
* Associated company names for Article users
* The exact presence of user records in the system

h3. *Impact:*

* *User Enumeration:* Attackers can determine valid usernames or organization records by analyzing login responses or using the {{get-admins}} API.
* *Targeted Brute-force Attacks:* Once usernames are confirmed, attackers can focus efforts on password brute-forcing.
* *Recon for Privileged Accounts:* Article users could be targeted specifically if their association with admins/companies is leaked.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Verbose error responses during authentication.
* Separation of username and organization validation steps exposes additional information.
* Lack of generic error handling in both Admin and Article login flows.

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

h4. For Admin:

# Navigate to the admin login page.
# Submit the login form using:
#* A valid email and incorrect password.
#* An invalid email address.
# Observe differences in the response URLs, revealing whether the email exists in the system.

h4. For Article User:

# Submit a {{get-admins}} API request with various first names.
# Observe whether the admin exists or not based on a binary response ({{"No user found!"}} vs. company selection).
# Automate requests to enumerate admin first names and associated companies.

h3. *Solution:*

* *Implement Generic Error Messages:*
** Return a unified message for all login failures:
 {{"Invalid username or password"}} or {{"Authentication failed"}}
 regardless of the cause.
* *Consolidate Validation Flow:*
** Eliminate the separate {{get-admins}} lookup step. Instead, require the user to input both *Admin First Name* and *Company Name* in a single request.
** Match both values at once on the server side and return a generic failure message if validation fails.
* *Rate Limit Login Attempts:* Implement IP- and user-based rate limiting to prevent automated attacks.
* *Log Suspicious Activity:* Record failed login attempts and admin/company probing for anomaly detection.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-203:* Observable Discrepancy
* *CWE-204:* Response Discrepancy Information Exposure

Summary: Data Backup Flag Missing
Description:
h2. Data Backup Flag Missing ({{android:allowBackup="true"}})

*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 5.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:P/AC:L/PR:L/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N}}

h3. *Affected: Apk*

h3. *Description:*

The Android application declares the manifest attribute {{android:allowBackup}} as {{true}} (default behavior). This configuration allows application data to be backed up and restored via Android Debug Bridge (ADB), even if the app does not explicitly support or expect such behavior.

This exposes sensitive user data (e.g., authentication tokens, personal preferences, local caches) to any user who has physical access to the device and USB debugging enabled.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Sensitive Data Extraction:* An attacker with physical access to a device and ADB enabled can extract the app's internal data without requiring root access.
* *Credential Exposure:* Session data or cached secrets could be retrieved and used to impersonate users or access protected features.
* *Increased Risk During Theft or Loss:* Lost or stolen devices with USB debugging enabled can be exploited to exfiltrate app data.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* The {{android:allowBackup}} flag is either not explicitly defined or is set to {{true}}, allowing backup functionality by default.

h3. *Solution:*

* Set the {{android:allowBackup}} flag to {{false}} in your {{AndroidManifest.xml}}:

{{<application     android:allowBackup="false"     ... > }}

* Ensure sensitive data is stored securely using Android’s encrypted shared preferences or the Keystore system.
* Educate development teams to always evaluate the implications of default manifest attributes during the build process.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-312:* Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information
* *CWE-276:* Incorrect Default Permissions

h3. *OWASP Mobile Top 10 (2024) Category:*

* *M2: Insecure Data Storage*

Summary: Unrestricted File Upload Size and Quantity
Description:
h2. Unrestricted File Upload Size and Quantity

*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 6.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H}}

h3. *Description:*

The application allows users to upload files without any enforced restrictions on *file size* or *number of files per request/session*. This lack of validation and enforcement on the backend introduces a risk of resource exhaustion on the server.

While client-side restrictions may exist, they are insufficient, as malicious users can bypass them using tools like Burp Suite or Postman to upload arbitrarily large files or spam multiple file uploads.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Denial of Service (DoS):* Uploading very large files or a high volume of files may exhaust server disk space, memory, or CPU, potentially causing service degradation or downtime.
* *Increased Storage Costs:* Without upload limits, storage can be abused for non-legitimate purposes (e.g., file hosting).
* *Resource Exhaustion:* May affect availability for other users, reducing quality of service.
* *Abuse Risk:* Could be used by bots or attackers to flood the server with junk content or perform automated spam uploads.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Absence of server-side validation on:
** File size (e.g., max 5 MB per file).
** Number of files (e.g., max 3–5 uploads per request or per user session).

h3. *Solution:*

* Implement server-side validations for file uploads:
** Enforce *maximum file size* (e.g., 5–10 MB per file).
** Restrict the *number of files per upload* and *per session*.
* Return meaningful error messages when limits are exceeded.
* Consider implementing rate limits on upload endpoints.
* Monitor storage utilization and alert on abnormal upload behavior.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-400:* Uncontrolled Resource Consumption

h3. *OWASP Top 10 (API 2023) Category:*

* *API4:2023 – Unrestricted Resource Consumption*

Summary: WEB - Public Exposure of Services and Server Details on Origin IP
Description:
h2. Public Exposure of Services and Server Details on Origin IP

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.1
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N}}

h3. *Affected IP: 13.127.15.84*

h3. *Description:*

The origin server's public IP address, which is directly associated with a subdomain, is accessible over the internet. Upon probing the IP, several services and open ports were discovered to be directly reachable via browser and network tools.

Additionally, HTTP response headers reveal the backend server software as *Nginx*, along with its version. This may aid an attacker in targeting known vulnerabilities associated with that version.

This exposure undermines the security provided by any reverse proxy or CDN like Cloudflare that may be in place for the main application domain.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Increased Attack Surface:* Services running on the server (e.g., web server, admin panels, APIs) are directly accessible without WAF or proxy protections.
* *Reconnaissance Support:* Attackers can fingerprint the server, enumerate services, and identify specific versions or misconfigurations.
* *Exploit Risk:* Public visibility of software versions (e.g., Nginx 1.22.1) increases the likelihood of exploitation through known CVEs.
* *Bypass of Perimeter Security:* WAF rules, bot mitigation, and traffic monitoring are bypassed if attackers connect directly to the IP.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Origin IP is exposed through subdomain DNS records.
* No network segmentation or firewall rules in place to restrict access to backend services.
* No rate limiting or access control at the origin server level.

h3. *Solution:*

* Place the origin server behind a reverse proxy like Cloudflare and *do not expose it directly* via public DNS records.
* Configure firewall rules to only allow inbound connections from Cloudflare IP ranges.
* Remove any DNS records (A/AAAA) that resolve directly to the origin server.
* Disable or restrict non-essential ports/services from public exposure.
* Use a web server configuration that suppresses software name/version in responses.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-200:* Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control

h3. *OWASP Top 10 (API 2023) Category:*

* *API8:2023 – Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: WEB - Subdomain with Direct Server IP Exposure (Firewall Bypass via Misconfigured DNS)
Description:
h2. Subdomain with Direct Server IP Exposure (Firewall Bypass via Misconfigured DNS)

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 7.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N}}

h3. *Affected: DNS Records for subdomain* [*https://documents.yourelocker.in*|https://documents.yourelocker.in]  

h3. *Description:*

It was identified that the subdomain {{documents.scope.com}} was configured to point directly to the origin server’s public IP address. While the main domain was protected behind Cloudflare’s reverse proxy and Web Application Firewall (WAF), the subdomain bypassed this protection due to DNS misconfiguration.

This exposure reveals the actual IP address of the origin server to the public. As a result, attackers can directly target the backend server, bypassing protections like rate limiting, bot protection, DDoS mitigation, and WAF rules provided by Cloudflare.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Firewall Bypass:* Cloudflare security controls can be completely bypassed by interacting with the server directly via its IP.
* *Targeted Exploitation:* Increases risk of attacks such as brute force, port scanning, and direct exploitation of unpatched services.
* *DDoS Vulnerability:* Direct IP exposure makes the server susceptible to denial-of-service attacks.
* *Reconnaissance Aid:* Attackers can fingerprint server software, enumerate open ports, and plan further exploits.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* DNS A/AAAA record for subdomain points to origin IP directly.
* Lack of proper segmentation or proxy routing through Cloudflare for all subdomains.

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Use subdomain enumeration tools (e.g., {{subfinder}}, {{amass}}) to identify subdomains.
# Perform a DNS lookup ({{dig}}, {{nslookup}}) for the subdomain.
# Observe that the subdomain resolves to a public IP address.
# Confirm that the IP is not protected by Cloudflare by testing for direct access (e.g., curl, Nmap) and comparing response headers.

h3. *Solution:*

* Route all traffic—including subdomains—through Cloudflare or a secured reverse proxy.
* Remove any public A/AAAA DNS entries that point directly to the origin IP.
* Use Cloudflare “Authenticated Origin Pulls” or IP whitelisting to restrict access to only Cloudflare IPs.
* Periodically audit DNS entries for exposed infrastructure.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-200:* Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
* *CWE-693:* Protection Mechanism Failure

h3. *OWASP Top 10 (API 2023) Category:*

* *API8:2023 – Security Misconfiguration*
Improper configuration of network-level protections can expose sensitive infrastructure, bypassing security controls.

Summary: WEB - Nextjs Buildmanifest.js File Leakage via nternet Archieve
Description:
h2. *Vulnerability:* Next.js {{buildManifest.js}} File Leakage via Internet Archive (Wayback Machine)

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.2
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:L/A:N}}

h3. *Affected Endpoint:* [*https://web.archive.org/web/20240301140044/https://yourelocker.in/_next/static/Pmgx7yXPhQpFUvrWNo4cO/_buildManifest.js*|https://web.archive.org/web/20240301140044/https://yourelocker.in/_next/static/Pmgx7yXPhQpFUvrWNo4cO/_buildManifest.js]

h3. *Description:*

It was identified that a historical snapshot of the application, archived on the *Wayback Machine (Internet Archive)*, contains an exposed {{buildManifest.js}} file. This file is automatically generated during Next.js builds and includes critical internal references such as:

* Full list of dynamic and static routes
* JavaScript and chunk files associated with those routes
* API endpoint paths for different user roles

Since {{buildManifest.js}} is accessible from archived versions of the site, malicious actors can enumerate all known API paths, including those not visible in the UI or currently protected by route restrictions.

h3. *Impact:*

* *API Enumeration:* Attackers gain visibility into internal APIs across user roles without authentication.
* *Privilege Escalation Opportunities:* Exposed paths for privileged roles can be targeted for unauthorized access or privilege escalation attempts.
* *Expanded Attack Surface:* Hidden or deprecated routes discovered via the manifest may still be functional and exploitable.
* *Reconnaissance Aid:* Leakage significantly improves the attacker's ability to plan targeted attacks.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* {{buildManifest.js}} was indexed and cached by the Wayback Machine or other web archives.
* Lack of proper *robots.txt* exclusions or archive prevention for sensitive build files.
* Application did not obfuscate or restrict access to build artifacts in past deployments.

h3. *Solution:*

* *Remove/Obfuscate* {{buildManifest.js}}*:* Prevent direct public access to build-related files in production environments.
* *Configure* {{robots.txt}}*:* Explicitly disallow search engines and crawlers from indexing sensitive files ({{/buildManifest.js}}, {{/static}}, etc.).
* *Request Removal from Internet Archive:* Use Wayback Machine removal request to delete cached sensitive content.
* *Regenerate Build with Route Obfuscation:* If possible, avoid exposing sensitive API paths through predictable static analysis of frontend files.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-200:* Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
* *CWE-538:* Insertion of Sensitive Information into Externally-Accessible Files

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API9:2023 – Improper Inventory Management*

Summary: Missing Account Lockout Policy
Description:
h2. *Vulnerability:* Missing Account Lockout Policy

*Severity:* Critical
*CVSS Score:* 9.1
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N}}

h3. *Affected Endpoint:* [*https://yourelocker.in/api/auth/login*|https://yourelocker.in/api/auth/login]

h3. *Description:*

The application lacks an account lockout mechanism, allowing unlimited login attempts without any restriction. During testing, it was observed that users could repeatedly attempt authentication with incorrect credentials without triggering any security control or temporary lockout.

*Critically, the risk is significantly elevated due to the presence of additional authentication-related vulnerabilities* in the application:

* *Username Enumeration:* Specific login responses disclose whether the username/email exists.
* *Missing Rate Limits:* No throttling or delay is enforced on repeated login attempts.
* *No Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA):* A single factor (password) is sufficient to gain access.
* *No Bot/Automation Protection:* CAPTCHA or similar safeguards are absent.

These combined weaknesses create a highly exploitable attack surface for credential stuffing and brute-force attacks.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Brute-force & Credential Stuffing Attacks:* Automated tools can attempt massive login attempts without being blocked.
* *Account Takeover:* Increased probability of unauthorized access to user accounts, especially those using weak or reused credentials.
* *Security Control Bypass:* The absence of secondary authentication (MFA) and automated abuse protection exacerbates the risk.
* *Regulatory Non-Compliance:* Fails to meet requirements under data protection standards such as GDPR, HIPAA, or PCI DSS.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* The backend authentication system does not track or restrict repeated failed login attempts.
* No enforcement of delay, CAPTCHA, or session blocking after consecutive failures.

h3. *Solution:*

* Implement an *account lockout threshold* (e.g., after 5 failed attempts).
* Introduce *progressive backoff* (e.g., exponential delay or time-based lockout).
* *Integrate CAPTCHA* or bot-detection for login attempts.
* Enforce *Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)* for all accounts.
* *Log and monitor* repeated authentication failures to detect abusive patterns.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-307:* Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts
* *CWE-798:* Use of Hard-coded Credentials _(if found applicable during testing)_

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*

Summary: Vertical Broken Object Level Authorization [ GET Article Details & Client Details ]
Description:
h2. Vertical Broken Object Level Authorization

*Severity:* Critical
*CVSS Score:* 9.4
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:N}}

h3. *Description:*

The application does not properly enforce authorization boundaries between users of different privilege levels. It was observed that a *Client-level user* was able to access data and endpoints that are intended to be restricted to *CA-Article-level users*.

This is a classic case of *vertical privilege escalation*, where a user with lower privileges can perform actions or retrieve data reserved for higher-privileged roles by manipulating request parameters or endpoint paths.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Privilege Escalation:* A Client user can impersonate or access privileged resources meant only for CA-Article users.
* *Sensitive Data Exposure:* Confidential or administrative-level data becomes accessible to general users.
* *Business Logic Abuse:* Attackers may misuse functionalities not intended for them, potentially affecting system integrity or user trust.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Absence of role-based access control (RBAC) enforcement at the API level.
* Server trusts client-side role identifiers without verifying the actual privileges on the backend.

h3. *Solution:*

* Implement robust *role-based authorization checks* on all protected API endpoints.
* Ensure each request validates whether the *authenticated user’s role is permitted* to perform the requested action.
* Avoid relying solely on front-end role indicators or UI restrictions to enforce access control.
* Conduct regular security code reviews and automated tests to detect vertical access control bypasses.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-285:* Improper Authorization
* *CWE-269:* Improper Privilege Management

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API1:2023 – Broken Object Level Authorization*

Summary: Horizontal Broken Object Level Authorization [ GET Article Details & Client Details ]
Description:
h2. Horizontal Broken Object Level Authorization

*Severity:* Critical
*CVSS Score:* 9.1
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:N}}

h3. *Description:*

The application fails to enforce proper authorization checks at the object level, allowing users to access resources belonging to other users within the same privilege group. Specifically, a *CA-Article user* can access and interact with data owned by another *CA-Article*, and a *Client user* is able to retrieve sensitive data belonging to other Clients.

This type of flaw is indicative of *horizontal privilege escalation*, where users can gain unauthorized access to objects or resources of peer accounts by modifying object identifiers (e.g., user ID, article ID) in request parameters.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Unauthorized Data Exposure:* Attackers can view personal or business-sensitive information of other users in the same role.
* *Data Integrity Risk:* In some cases, attackers may also be able to modify or delete data not belonging to them.
* *Violation of Data Privacy Standards:* This poses serious compliance issues with data protection regulations like GDPR, HIPAA, etc.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Missing or inadequate object-level access control checks on API endpoints handling user-owned resources.
* Authorization logic only validates user roles but not resource ownership.

h3. *Solution:*

* Implement strict *object-level authorization checks* in all relevant endpoints.
* Ensure that each request validates whether the *authenticated user is the rightful owner* of the resource being accessed or modified.
* Use *centralized access control logic* rather than relying on client-side restrictions.
* Include automated tests and audits to detect similar flaws during development and QA stages.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-639:* Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API1:2023 – Broken Object Level Authorization*

Summary: Excessive Refresh Token Lifespan
Description:
h2. Excessive Refresh Token Lifespan

*Severity:* Low
*CVSS Score:* 3.7
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N}}

h3. *Description:*

It was observed that the backend issues refresh tokens with a lifespan of *one month*, which is significantly longer than recommended for typical authentication systems. Extended validity of refresh tokens increases the window of opportunity for misuse if a token is compromised.

A refresh token, by design, should have a reasonably short lifespan and be stored securely. However, in the event of theft, a long-lived token allows unauthorized users to continue generating new access tokens for an extended period without detection.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Prolonged Risk Window:* If an attacker gains access to the refresh token, they can maintain access to the account for a full month without needing credentials again.
* *Bypass of Short Access Token Expiry:* While short-lived access tokens reduce surface area, they are ineffective if the refresh token remains valid for too long.
* *Increased Threat Persistence:* Token theft through XSS, local storage compromise, or insecure transmission channels becomes more damaging.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Refresh token expiration is set to an excessively long duration (30 days).
* No rotating or revocation mechanism is in place post-authentication events like logout or password change.

h3. *Solution:*

* Reduce the *refresh token validity to 7 days or less* based on business needs.
* Implement *refresh token rotation* and detection of anomalous usage (e.g., reuse of old tokens).
* Revoke all refresh tokens on critical actions such as password change, device de-registration, or logout.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-1255:* Insufficient Session Expiration

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API8:2023 - Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: Missing Session Invalidation on Password Change
Description:
h2. Missing Session Invalidation on Password Change

*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 6.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:L}}

h3. *Description:*

It was observed that when a user changes their password through the mobile application, the application enforces a logout, and the session is invalidated as expected. However, when the same password change functionality is invoked directly via the API (e.g., through Burp Suite Repeater), the session remains active, and the authentication token is still valid.

This indicates inconsistent behavior in session handling between client-triggered and API-level password changes. A valid session remains active even after a password change, which is a critical event that should enforce a session termination to prevent unauthorized use of a potentially compromised session.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Post-Compromise Persistence:* If an attacker had access to a session and the legitimate user changes their password, the attacker can still maintain access unless the session is forcibly invalidated.
* *Security Misalignment:* Different behavior between API and app can lead to exploitation by clients that intentionally avoid the logout mechanism (e.g., through custom scripts or proxies).
* *Account Takeover Risk:* If credentials were compromised, session persistence could allow continued unauthorized access even after user attempts recovery via password change.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Lack of backend logic to revoke or expire the current session token upon a password change.
* Session lifecycle is managed independently from sensitive state changes.

h3. *Solution:*

* Implement backend logic to *invalidate all active sessions* (especially the current token) upon a successful password change.
* Return an appropriate response instructing the client to re-authenticate.
* Optionally implement device/session management to allow users to view and revoke active sessions manually.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-613:* Insufficient Session Expiration
* *CWE-287:* Improper Authentication

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*

Summary: Weak Backend Password Policy (Enforced Only on Client Side)
Description:
h2. Weak Backend Password Policy (Enforced Only on Client Side)

*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 6.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:L}}

h3. *Description:*

The application enforces strong password complexity rules (such as minimum length, special characters, uppercase/lowercase letters) only on the Flutter-based Android client. However, during backend testing, it was observed that the API itself lacks any server-side password validation mechanism.

This allows an attacker to bypass the frontend validation (e.g., by using a proxy or custom HTTP client) and set weak passwords such as {{admin1234}}, {{password}}, or even {{123456}}, leading to extremely insecure accounts being created.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Increased Risk of Account Takeover:* Weak passwords are susceptible to brute-force and dictionary attacks.
* *Bypass of Business Logic:* Client-side controls can be easily circumvented, rendering them ineffective.
* *Violation of Best Practices:* Failing to enforce password strength server-side contradicts widely accepted authentication security standards.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Absence of backend validation for password complexity and strength.
* Reliance on client-side (Flutter) validation which can be bypassed.

h3. *Solution:*

* Enforce password policy validation *on the server side*, ensuring that all account creation and password update requests are validated.
* Recommended backend password policy:
** Minimum length of 8 characters.
** At least one uppercase letter, one lowercase letter, one digit, and one special character.
* Return appropriate HTTP 400 errors with descriptive messages when weak passwords are submitted.
* Optionally, integrate with libraries that assess password entropy and block commonly used or breached passwords.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-521:* Weak Password Requirements
* *CWE-602:* Client-Side Enforcement of Server-Side Security

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*

Summary: Missing Multiple HTTP Security Headers
Description:
h2. Missing Multiple HTTP Security Headers

*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 5.6
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N}}

h3. *Description:*

The application's APIs were found to be missing several essential HTTP security headers in their responses. These headers play a critical role in enhancing the security posture of the application by mitigating common web-based attacks and enforcing best security practices at the transport and response layers.

While the APIs are consumed by an Android client, the absence of these headers still poses a risk if the APIs are accessed via other clients or during MITM (Man-In-The-Middle) attacks in case of SSL termination at proxies or misconfigured devices.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Reduced Defense-in-Depth:* Lack of these headers weakens protection against threats like clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and information leakage.
* *Attack Surface Exposure:* Attackers can more easily probe and exploit behavior if standard protections are not enforced at the HTTP layer.

h3. *Recommended HTTP Security Headers:*

||*Header*||*Purpose*||
|{{Strict-Transport-Security}}|Enforces secure (HTTPS) connections to the server.|
|{{X-Content-Type-Options}}|Prevents MIME type sniffing by browsers.|
|{{Referrer-Policy}}|Controls the amount of referrer information sent with requests.|
|{{Permissions-Policy}}|Restricts access to browser features like geolocation, camera, and more.|
|{{Cache-Control: no-store}}|Prevents caching of sensitive API responses.|

----

h3. *Root Cause:*

* HTTP responses from API endpoints do not include standard security headers due to misconfiguration or oversight during development and deployment.

h3. *Solution:*

* Configure the backend server or reverse proxy (e.g., NGINX, Apache, API Gateway) to include the listed headers in all API responses.
* Review all deployed environments to ensure consistent header policies are enforced.
* Validate header presence using automated tools as part of your CI/CD pipeline.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-693:* Protection Mechanism Failure
* *CWE-16:* Configuration

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API8:2023 – Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: Improper Error Handling on Unexpected Input
Description:
h2. Improper Error Handling on Unexpected Input

*Severity:* Low
*CVSS Score:* 3.1
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:L}}

h3. *Description:*

The application responds with a {{500 Internal Server Error}} when provided with unexpected or malformed input instead of returning appropriate {{4xx}} client-side error responses such as {{400 Bad Request}} or {{422 Unprocessable Entity}}.

This behavior indicates a lack of proper input validation or error handling mechanisms on the server side. A {{500}} error suggests that the server encountered an unhandled exception, which could reveal implementation flaws or open the door for more severe exploitation if deeper error contexts are exposed.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Denial of Service Potential:* Repeated malformed inputs can lead to application crashes or resource exhaustion due to unhandled exceptions.
* *Information Disclosure Risk:* If verbose error messages are enabled, they might inadvertently reveal stack traces, file paths, or internal logic.
* *Reduced Security Posture:* Lack of graceful error handling can serve as a reconnaissance tool for attackers to identify vulnerable endpoints.

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Interact with any API endpoint that expects a specific input schema.
# Submit unexpected data types, malformed JSON, or out-of-bound values.
# Observe the HTTP response code and confirm if a {{500}} error is returned.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Insufficient input validation and sanitization.
* Lack of structured exception handling for invalid client requests.

h3. *Solution:*

* Implement comprehensive input validation on all API endpoints before processing.
* Use structured exception handling to catch and respond to unexpected input gracefully.
* Ensure that invalid client requests return appropriate {{4xx}} responses, not {{5xx}} server errors.
* Disable verbose error responses in production environments to prevent leakage of internal logic.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-388:* Error Handling
* *CWE-209:* Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API8:2023 – Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: Predictable CA-Article Username Pattern (Based on First Name)
Description:
h2. Predictable CA-Article Username Pattern (Based on First Name)

*Severity:* Low
*CVSS Score:* 3.3
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N}}

h3. *Description:*

It was observed that the platform auto-generates or allows the creation of usernames for CA-Article users based on their first names in a predictable format (e.g., {{john}}, {{ankit}}, etc.). This creates a highly guessable naming convention, especially when combined with knowledge of registered individuals.

The usernames appear to lack any additional entropy such as a numeric suffix, last name, or random hash, making brute-force enumeration or social engineering more feasible.

h3. *Impact:*

* *User Enumeration:* An attacker can iterate over common first names to identify registered users, which may aid in subsequent attacks such as password spraying or phishing.
* *Privacy Violation:* Disclosure or inference of users' presence on the platform.
* *Weak Account Protection:* If usernames are predictable and passwords are weak, the chances of account compromise increase.

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Navigate to the login or registration interface.
# Try accessing endpoints or features (e.g., profile lookup or reset flows) using common first names as usernames.
# Observe that valid responses or error messages confirm the existence of such users.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Use of deterministic or easily guessable patterns (e.g., first name only) in assigning usernames.
* No enforcement of uniqueness or entropy in public identifiers.

h3. *Solution:*

* Avoid using only the first name as a username. Incorporate randomness or additional elements (e.g., {{firstname.lastname}}, unique IDs).
* Enforce strong username uniqueness rules and consider making usernames private where possible.
* Apply rate limiting and consistent error responses to avoid confirming valid usernames.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-362:* Guessable User Account Identifiers
* *CWE-203:* Observable Discrepancy

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API1:2023 – Broken Object Level Authorization*

Summary: Missing Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
Description:
h2. Missing Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)

*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 6.8
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N}}

h3. *Description:*

The application does not implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for user accounts, including those with elevated privileges such as administrators or content managers. All users are authenticated solely via username/email and password, with no requirement for an additional verification factor (e.g., OTP, mobile app push, hardware key).

This single-factor authentication approach significantly increases the risk of unauthorized account access in the event of password compromise.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Credential-Based Attacks:* If a user's credentials are phished, leaked, or brute-forced, an attacker can gain full access to the account.
* *Privilege Escalation:* Absence of MFA on admin panels or dashboards can allow attackers to abuse high-privilege features without additional barriers.
* *Regulatory Non-Compliance:* Some industries and jurisdictions require MFA for accounts with access to sensitive data (e.g., under GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS).

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Register or log in with a valid username and password.
# Observe that no additional authentication step is required (such as an OTP or authenticator prompt).
# Even for high-privilege accounts, the login completes after only password validation.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Lack of implementation of a second authentication factor in the login flow.
* No enforcement of MFA for sensitive operations or privileged roles.

h3. *Solution:*

* Implement MFA using time-based one-time passwords (TOTP), SMS OTPs, email OTPs, or hardware/software tokens.
* Enforce MFA for all users, or at minimum for users with administrative, financial, or data export privileges.
* Provide users with options to enable MFA and mandate it after initial login or for sensitive actions (step-up authentication).

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-308:* Use of Single-factor Authentication
* *CWE-287:* Improper Authentication

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*

Summary: Absence of Bot Mitigation on Critical Endpoints
Description:
h2. Absence of Bot Mitigation on Critical Endpoints

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 7.2
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H}}

*Affected Endpoint :* [*https://yourelocker.in/api/login*|https://yourelocker.in/api/login] *(verify)*

h3. *Description:*

The application lacks CAPTCHA or equivalent bot mitigation mechanisms on critical endpoints such as login, registration, password reset, or verification. These endpoints are publicly accessible and process sensitive or state-changing operations without validating whether the user is a human or an automated client.

This makes the platform susceptible to automated abuse, including brute-force attacks, spam account creation, and enumeration attempts.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Automated Account Attacks:* Bots can continuously attempt login or registration using generated or stolen credentials.
* *Spam & Resource Abuse:* Attackers may flood the application with fake accounts or password reset attempts, degrading user experience.
* *Infrastructure Stress:* Lack of throttling or CAPTCHA can result in high traffic volume, leading to increased server load or availability issues.

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Navigate to a public endpoint like {{/api/login}} or {{/api/register}}.
# Use an automated tool (e.g., Burp Intruder, Hydra, or a simple Python script) to send repeated requests.
# Observe that no CAPTCHA challenge or rate-based restriction is triggered.
# The server continues processing all requests normally.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* No CAPTCHA or human-verification logic implemented on sensitive public endpoints.
* Failure to implement bot-detection as part of form validation or request submission.

h3. *Solution:*

* Integrate CAPTCHA (e.g., reCAPTCHA, hCaptcha) on all critical endpoints handling user authentication, registration, and account recovery.
* Apply CAPTCHA after a threshold of invalid attempts (adaptive CAPTCHA).
* Ensure CAPTCHA verification occurs server-side to prevent client-side bypasses.
* Implement additional bot detection layers such as browser fingerprinting or behavioral analysis.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-799:* Improper Control of Interaction Frequency (Rate Limiting)
* *CWE-287:* Improper Authentication (due to lack of validation of client nature)

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API4:2023 – Unrestricted Resource Consumption*

Summary: Missing Rate Limiting on Critical Endpoints
Description:
h2. *Missing Rate Limiting on Critical Endpoints*

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 7.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:N}}

*Affected Endpoint :* [*https://yourelocker.in/**|https://yourelocker.in/*] 

h3. *Description:*

It was observed that the application does not enforce rate limiting on sensitive API endpoints, such as login, signup, password reset, or verification. This allows an attacker to make an unlimited number of requests in rapid succession without any throttling, delay, or blocking mechanism in place.

Such behavior significantly increases the risk of automated attacks, including brute-force login attempts, credential stuffing, and abuse of account-related functionalities.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Brute-force Attacks:* Attackers can systematically try multiple passwords or verification codes without restriction, leading to account takeover.
* *Credential Stuffing:* Known breached credentials can be tested rapidly against the login API to identify valid combinations.
* *Abuse of Functionality:* APIs like signup or password reset can be spammed, potentially causing service degradation or denial of service for legitimate users.
* *Increased Infrastructure Costs:* Excessive requests from automated tools can inflate backend usage and cost.

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Identify a sensitive endpoint such as {{/api/login}} or {{/api/auth/verify}}.
# Send a series of automated requests using tools like Burp Intruder, Hydra, or a custom script.
# Observe that all requests are processed with no apparent rate limiting or IP-based throttling.
# Note absence of error messages, CAPTCHA, or temporary blocking on repeated invalid attempts.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Absence of rate limiting mechanisms on sensitive endpoints.
* No enforcement of request thresholds per IP or user session.

h3. *Solution:*

* Implement rate limiting using middleware or API gateway (e.g., 5 login attempts per IP per minute).
* Introduce exponential backoff or temporary lockout after multiple failed attempts.
* Use CAPTCHA or other bot mitigation techniques after a threshold.
* Log and monitor repeated suspicious activities for automated detection.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-307:* Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts
* *CWE-770:* Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API4:2023 – Unrestricted Resource Consumption*

Summary: Username Enumeration via Login Response
Description:
h2. *Username Enumeration via Login Response*

*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 5.6
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N}}

h3. *Affected Endpoint:* [*https://yourelocker.in/api/auth/login*|https://yourelocker.in/api/auth/login] *(Mobile)* 

h3. *Description:*

The login API reveals distinguishable error messages based on the validity of the username provided. When a valid email or username is entered with an incorrect password, the response is:

{quote}{{"Incorrect Password, please try again!"}}{quote}

Whereas when an invalid email/username is submitted, the response is:

{quote}{{"Invalid Credentials!"}}{quote}

This discrepancy allows an attacker to confirm the existence of registered users by observing the variation in error messages, making it possible to enumerate valid usernames or email addresses.

h3. *Impact:*

* *User Enumeration:* Enables attackers to build a list of valid usernames or emails.
* *Brute Force Facilitation:* Allows targeted brute-force or credential stuffing attacks against known accounts.
* *Social Engineering Risk:* Validated user identities can be leveraged in phishing or impersonation attacks.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Error messages are not uniform for failed login attempts.
* No generic failure response is enforced to obscure user existence.

h3. *Solution:*

* Return a *generic error message* such as:
{{"Invalid username or password."}}
regardless of whether the username or password is incorrect.
* Implement *rate limiting* and *account lockout* mechanisms on repeated failed login attempts.
* Monitor and alert on suspicious login patterns to detect enumeration attempts.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-204:* Response Discrepancy Information Exposure
* *CWE-203:* Observable Discrepancy

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API2:2023 – Broken Authentication*

Summary: Arbitrary File Upload Vulnerability
Description:
h2. *Vulnerability:* Arbitrary File Upload via API Endpoint (Unrestricted Input Handling)

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 7.3
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:L}}

h3. *Affected Endpoint:* [*https://yourelocker.in/api/common/upload-client-documents*|https://yourelocker.in/api/common/upload-client-documents]

h3. *Description:*

The application allows users to upload files directly through an API endpoint without implementing sufficient validation or control over the type and content of the uploaded files. It was observed that the file upload is handled via a custom API endpoint instead of using AWS S3 pre-signed URLs, indicating the server handles the uploaded content directly before passing it to the S3 bucket.

While the files are ultimately stored on AWS S3, which reduces the attack surface for direct code execution, the lack of enforced restrictions on file types, content inspection, or backend sanitization poses a risk. If the backend performs any processing (e.g., parsing, rendering, or scanning of uploaded files), it may still be vulnerable to attacks such as:

* Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
* XML External Entity (XXE) injection
* File metadata manipulation
* Bypass of business logic through crafted files

h3. *Impact:*

* *Security Bypass:* Malicious files could be uploaded to exploit weak backend logic or integrations (e.g., virus scanning services, image converters).
* *Stored Attack Vectors:* Uploaded files could contain embedded payloads (e.g., JavaScript in SVG or PDF) used in phishing, social engineering, or XSS when viewed.
* *Potential Lateral Risk:* If file URLs are shared with privileged systems or staff, they may trigger backend processing or rendering.

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Authenticate as a valid user and navigate to the file upload functionality.
# Intercept the request using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite) and modify the file contents and MIME type (e.g., upload {{.php}}, {{.exe}}, or {{.svg}} with embedded JavaScript).
# Submit the file via the API and inspect the S3 bucket or returned URL.
# Confirm that the application accepts the file without proper content inspection or restriction.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* No validation of file types and MIME types during upload.
* File upload occurs via direct API endpoints without S3 pre-signed URL enforcement.
* Uncertainty around backend-side processing logic after upload, increasing potential for chained exploits.

h3. *Solution:*

* *Prefer Pre-Signed URLs:*
** Move file upload functionality to AWS S3 pre-signed URLs to eliminate backend processing exposure.
* *Enforce File Type Restrictions:*
** Implement strict allowlists for file extensions and MIME types both client-side and server-side.
* *Scan Files on Upload:*
** Use content inspection or antivirus scanning tools before storing uploaded files.
* *Restrict Executable File Types:*
** Block risky extensions (e.g., {{.php}}, {{.js}}, {{.html}}, {{.svg}}, {{.exe}}) even if they’re stored remotely.
* *Harden Backend Logic:*
** Ensure no dynamic or automatic file parsing occurs on the server after upload unless strictly necessary and hardened.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-434:* Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
* *CWE-116:* Improper Encoding or Escaping of Output (relevant for embedded script risk)

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API8:2023 – Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: Disclosure of Password Hash in API Response
Description:
h2. Disclosure of Password Hash in API Response

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 7.4
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N}}

h3. *Affected Endpoint:* [*https://yourelocker.in/api/ca-admin/clients/get-client-documents*|https://yourelocker.in/api/ca-admin/clients/get-client-documents] 

h3. *Description:*

It was identified that one or more API endpoints disclose the password hash of users within the response body. This may occur during profile fetch operations, user listing endpoints, or admin-level queries. Although the password is not revealed in plaintext, the presence of its hashed form still constitutes a significant security flaw.

Disclosure of password hashes—even if properly salted—provides attackers with valuable information that can be used in offline brute-force or dictionary attacks. If any weak hashing algorithm (e.g., MD5 or unsalted SHA1) is used, the risk is substantially higher.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Offline Password Cracking:* Attackers can perform brute-force or dictionary attacks against leaked hashes to recover the original passwords.
* *Credential Stuffing:* Once a password is cracked, it can be tested on other systems where the user might have reused credentials.
* *Reconnaissance for User Targeting:* Patterns in hashes (e.g., length, prefix) may help attackers identify weak passwords or user behavior trends.

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Authenticate as a user or admin with access to a vulnerable endpoint.
# Perform a GET request to the user profile endpoint (e.g., {{/api/user/me}} or {{/api/users}}).
# Observe that the JSON response includes a {{password}}, {{passwordHash}}, or similar field.
# Note that the hash value is exposed in the response.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Inclusion of sensitive fields such as {{password}}, {{password_hash}}, or {{hash}} in API responses.
* Insufficient filtering or sanitization of fields during object serialization before API response.

h3. *Solution:*

* Exclude all sensitive fields (especially password-related) from API responses by default.
* Implement strict response object filtering or whitelisting based on role and context.
* Ensure that database or ORM serialization layers do not auto-include sensitive attributes unless explicitly required (and even then, only server-side).
* Use proven hashing algorithms (e.g., bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2) and ensure salts are used and never disclosed.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-200:* Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
* *CWE-522:* Insufficiently Protected Credentials

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API3:2023 – Broken Object Property Level Authorization*

Summary: Predictable UUID Format Leading to Insecure Object Enumeration
Description:
h2. *Predictable UUID Format Leading to Insecure Object Enumeration*

*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 5.3
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N}}

h3. *Affected endpoint:* [*https://yourelocker.in/api/ca-admin/articles/get-article-details*|https://yourelocker.in/api/ca-admin/articles/get-article-details]

h3. *Description:*

It was observed that the application uses UUIDs (Universally Unique Identifiers) with a predictable and compact format — typically 12-byte strings represented as 24 hexadecimal characters. These identifiers, instead of being cryptographically random, follow a sequential or partially predictable pattern.

This format makes it easier for an attacker to infer nearby UUIDs based on one or two known values. A minor brute-force mutation or numeric incrementation of such UUIDs can reveal other valid identifiers, allowing unauthorized access to adjacent objects or user-specific data.

----

h3. *Impact:*

* *Insecure Object Enumeration:* Attackers can guess valid resource identifiers by modifying observed UUIDs, leading to potential IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) vulnerabilities.
* *Data Exposure:* Sensitive or user-specific data may be retrieved if object access is not properly authorized.
* *Business Logic Abuse:* Predictable UUIDs can be exploited in automation or scraping attacks to harvest internal resources, documents, or users.

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Register or login as a valid user.
# Identify a response or URL containing a UUID (e.g., {{/api/v1/resource/3c7bd18fa36d1f784c4ba123}}).
# Slightly mutate the UUID (e.g., increment the last few hex characters).
# Use the modified UUID to query the same endpoint.
# If access is successful, observe unauthorized access to adjacent resources.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Usage of compact UUIDs derived from timestamp, MAC address, or object index.
* Lack of cryptographic randomness in identifier generation.
* Absence of proper authorization checks on object-level access.

h3. *Solution:*

* Use cryptographically secure, random UUIDs (e.g., UUIDv4) instead of partially sequential or predictable formats.
* Implement strict object-level access controls (e.g., user ownership validation).
* Introduce rate limiting and anomaly detection for repeated access attempts using similar UUID patterns.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-639:* Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control

h3. *OWASP API Top 10 (2023) Category:*

* *API1:2023 – Broken Object Level Authorization*
APIs that use user-supplied object identifiers without proper access control are vulnerable to IDOR-style attacks.

Summary: Server Name and Version Disclosure
Description:
h3. *Vulnerability:* *Server Name and Version Disclosure*
*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 4.3
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N}}

h3. *Affected Endpoint :* [*https://yourelocker.in/**|https://yourelocker.in/*] 

h3. *Description*

The application’s HTTP responses include the {{Server}} header, which discloses both the server type and its version (e.g., {{nginx/1.22.1}}). Although seemingly low risk on its own, this information aids attackers in identifying the underlying technologies, making it easier to launch version-specific attacks.

Such disclosures violate the principle of minimizing the exposure of internal implementation details, which is a recognized security best practice.

h3. *Impact*

* *Facilitates Targeted Attacks:* Disclosure of exact server version helps adversaries map known vulnerabilities to the environment.
* *Supports Reconnaissance Phase:* Attackers gathering open-source intelligence (OSINT) benefit from metadata leaks like server headers.
* *Potential Non-Compliance:* Such exposure may violate industry compliance or baseline security hardening checklists (e.g., CIS benchmarks).

h3. *Root Cause*

The server is configured to expose version and technology stack metadata in the HTTP {{Server}} header by default.

h3. *Solution*

* *Suppress Server Header:*
** For *Nginx*: Set {{server_tokens off;}} and use additional config to clear headers in responses.
** For *Apache*: Set {{ServerTokens Prod}} and {{ServerSignature Off}}.
* *Use a Reverse Proxy or WAF:* Strip or rewrite headers at the gateway level.
* *Avoid Version Exposure Altogether:* Do not include software version or framework identifiers in HTTP response headers.

h3. *CWE-ID*

*CWE-200:* Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

h3. *OWASP Top 10 (2023) – API Category*

*API8:2023 – Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: Vulnerability: Insecure Direct Access to PII Resources on S3
Description:
h2. *Vulnerability: Insecure Direct Access to PII Resources on S3 (Lack of Authorization Check)*

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 7.5
*Vector:* {{CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N}}

h3. Affected Endpoint : [https://yourelocker.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/6596b54757073a92d2a6d887/3c7bd18f-af1f-45bc-950a-36d1f784c4ba?versionId=vpyQd9sFIBRmTpFYUucbJ3hGUHw1Sz0C|https://yourelocker.s3.ap-south-1.amazonaws.com/6596b54757073a92d2a6d887/3c7bd18f-af1f-45bc-950a-36d1f784c4ba?versionId=vpyQd9sFIBRmTpFYUucbJ3hGUHw1Sz0C]

h3. *Description:*

The application stores sensitive user data, including Personally Identifiable Information (PII), in files hosted on an Amazon S3 bucket. These resources are expected to be accessed only by authenticated and authorized users. However, it was identified that the application allows direct access to such files through their S3 URLs, without enforcing any authentication or authorization checks.

This implies that any user, including unauthenticated parties, can retrieve a file directly if the URL is known. While the S3 bucket does not expose directory listings, relying solely on obscurity of file URLs is an insufficient access control mechanism.

h3. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Authenticate as a valid user and access a file (e.g., document or media) that is expected to be protected.
# Capture the direct S3 URL from the network requests (e.g., {{https://bucket-name.s3.amazonaws.com/protected/file.pdf}}).
# Open a new incognito browser window or use a tool such as {{curl}} or Postman.
# Make a request to the same URL without passing any authorization headers or cookies.
# Observe that the file is returned without any access control validation.

h3. *Impact:*

* *Unauthorized Access to PII:* An attacker who obtains the URL of a sensitive file may access it without any authentication, resulting in a significant data leakage risk.
* *Regulatory Non-Compliance:* This behavior may lead to violations of data protection laws such as GDPR, HIPAA, and PCI DSS, which mandate strict access control for personal and financial information.
* *Persistent Exposure Risk:* Since the URLs are stable and long-lived, any accidental leak (e.g., in browser history, support tickets, logs, or referrer headers) could result in unauthorized third-party access.
* *No Auditability:* Accesses made directly to S3 resources bypass the application, resulting in a lack of audit trail or accountability.

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Direct file access from the S3 bucket does not require credentials.
* The application exposes full URLs to protected files without enforcing authorization or session validation.
* No signed URL mechanism or backend verification is implemented for file downloads.

h3. *Solution:*

* *Enforce Signed URLs:*
** Implement AWS S3 pre-signed URLs for file downloads, with short expiry and restricted permissions.
** Ensure these are generated only after verifying the user’s authorization within the application.
* *Restrict S3 Access:*
** Remove all public access permissions from the S3 bucket.
** Configure bucket policies and IAM roles to allow access only from authorized backend services or trusted environments.
* *Validate Authorization Before Link Generation:*
** Ensure users can only request and receive file links after authentication and role-based access control checks.
* *Logging and Monitoring:*
** Enable S3 access logs and integrate them with security monitoring solutions to detect suspicious or mass access attempts.

h3. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control
* *CWE-200:* Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

h3. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 – Broken Access Control*
* *A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: Improper Authorization and Reusable Purchase Tokens in Subscription Endpoint
Description:
*Improper Authorization and Reusable Purchase Tokens in Subscription Endpoint*
*Severity:* Critical
*CVSS Score:* 9.8
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H

*Description:*
The {{/subscription}} endpoint accepts subscription metadata, including a {{purchaseToken}}, {{transactionId}}, and associated product identifiers. However, it fails to validate the *ownership* of the {{purchaseToken}} against the authenticated user, and additionally, *permits the same token to be reused across multiple accounts*.
This allows any user to intercept a valid subscription request (from a friend, attacker’s own prior purchase, etc.), and replay the same request using their own bearer token. As a result, unauthorized users can upgrade their accounts to a premium subscription *without making any valid transaction*.

*Impact:*

* *Privilege Escalation:* Users can gain premium access without payment.
* *Token Reuse Abuse:* A single {{purchaseToken}} can be reused multiple times across multiple accounts.
* *Severe Revenue Loss:* Premium features can be accessed freely by exploiting shared or leaked tokens.
* *Lack of Purchase Binding:* Tokens are not tied to a specific user or transaction context.

*Steps to Reproduce:*

# Capture a legitimate {{/subscription}} request with a valid {{purchaseToken}} and {{transactionId}}.
# Register or use a different user account (non-premium).
# Replace only the {{Authorization}} header with a bearer token of the second account.
# Replay the request.
# Observe that the second account is granted premium access.

*Additional Abuse Case:* The *same token can be reused repeatedly* to grant access to an unlimited number of users, until the purchase token is manually revoked or expires.

*Root Cause:*

* No validation of {{purchaseToken}} ownership.
* Purchase token is not bound to user identity.
* Lack of replay protection or token lifecycle enforcement.

*Solution:*

* *Strictly bind* the {{purchaseToken}} and {{transactionId}} to the user ID at the time of subscription.
* *Verify* purchase tokens with the issuing platform (e.g., Google Play, Apple App Store).
* Implement *one-time use policy* for {{purchaseToken}}.
* Maintain a *server-side mapping* of tokens to user accounts.
* Reject any reuse or mismatched usage of subscription tokens.

*CWE-ID:*

* CWE-285: Improper Authorization
* CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
* CWE-345: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity

*OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* A01:2021 – Broken Access Control
* A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration
* A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures

Affected : [+https://zora-api-dev.webelight.co.in/subscription+|https://zora-api-dev.webelight.co.in/subscription]

Summary: Missing Essential Security Headers on Payment Gateway
Description:
*Missing Multiple Security Headers on Payment Gateway*
*Severity*: Medium
*CVSS Score:* 6.5
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N

h4. *Description*

The application is *missing critical security headers* in HTTP responses, leaving it vulnerable to multiple attack vectors, including *clickjacking, XSS, MIME sniffing attacks, and data exposure*. The following headers were found to be missing or improperly configured:

* *X-Frame-Options*: Prevents Clickjacking attacks.
* *Content-Security-Policy (CSP)*: Mitigates XSS by restricting script execution.
* *X-Content-Type-Options*: Prevents MIME-type sniffing.
* *Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS)*: Enforces HTTPS connections.
* *Referrer-Policy*: Controls the information shared in HTTP headers.
* *Permissions-Policy*: Restricts access to browser features like camera, microphone, geolocation, etc.

h4. *Impact*

* *Clickjacking attacks* can trick users into performing unintended actions.
* *Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities* are more exploitable without CSP.
* *MIME-type sniffing attacks* can lead to execution of unintended file types.
* *Downgrade attacks* can force HTTP connections if HSTS is not set.
* *Information leakage* occurs due to missing Referrer-Policy.

h4. *Solution*

Implement and correctly configure the following security headers:

{{X-Frame-Options: DENY   Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'   X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff   Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload   Referrer-Policy: no-referrer   Permissions-Policy: geolocation=(), microphone=()   }}

h4. *References*

* CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure
* OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration
* OWASP Secure Headers Project

Summary: Clickjacking Vulnerability on Payment Gateway
Description:
*Clickjacking Vulnerability on Payment Gateway*
*Severity*: Medium
*CVSS Score:* 6.2
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N

*Description:*
It was observed that the payment gateway page does not implement any anti-clickjacking protection mechanisms such as the {{X-Frame-Options}} header or an appropriate {{Content-Security-Policy (CSP)}} directive ({{frame-ancestors}}). This allows the page to be embedded inside an iframe on an attacker-controlled domain.
By tricking a user into clicking transparent iframes layered over malicious content, an attacker can potentially cause unintended actions like approving transactions without the user’s awareness.

*Impact:*

* Potential *fraudulent transactions* without the user realizing it.
* *Reputation damage* if users fall victim through external malicious sites.
* *Compliance failures* with standard web security practices.

*Root Cause:*

* Missing {{X-Frame-Options}} or {{Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors}} to prevent iframe embedding.

*Solution:*

* Set {{X-Frame-Options: DENY}} or {{SAMEORIGIN}} header in the server response.
* Alternatively, implement a Content Security Policy with {{frame-ancestors 'none';}} directive.
* Perform periodic security header audits.

*CWE-ID:*

* CWE-1021: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames

*OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration

Affected : {update-soon}

Summary: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Payment Information
Description:
*Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Payment Information*
*Severity:* Critical
*CVSS Score:* 9.8
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H

*Description:*
 It was observed that the application sends sensitive credit card information (card number, expiry date, CVV) in cleartext within HTTP request parameters during the payment process. This data is exposed directly in request payloads, making it vulnerable to interception, logging, or leakage at multiple points in the network path.

*Impact:*

* Full exposure of customer credit card information
* Immediate violation of PCI DSS standards
* High risk of financial fraud, identity theft, and major data breaches
* Potential heavy fines, legal action, and brand reputation damage

*Root Cause:*

* Credit card data handled insecurely within the application layer instead of using direct secure connections to payment gateways
* Absence of encryption or tokenization mechanisms during client-server communication

*Solution:*

* Implement *direct-to-gateway* payment handling (e.g., hosted payment pages, client-side tokenization via PCI DSS certified service providers)
* Never store, transmit, or log raw cardholder data on your servers or applications
* Perform *PCI DSS compliance audit* and remediate immediately

*CWE-ID:*

* CWE-319: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
* CWE-522: Insufficiently Protected Credentials

*OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* A02:2021 – Cryptographic Failures

Affected : {update-soon}

Summary: Sensitive Data Leakage via API
Description:
h3. *Sensitive Data Exposure via Unrestricted API Access (*{{/api/user/all}}*)*

*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 7.5
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N**

*Description:*
It was observed that the endpoint {{/api/user/all}} returns the complete list of users in the system, including their sensitive information, when accessed by any authenticated user, including basic verified subscribers. This endpoint lacks proper access control and does not restrict data visibility based on user roles or permissions.

The returned dataset includes fields such as usernames, email addresses, phone numbers, and other personally identifiable information (PII), which should only be visible to privileged users such as administrators or support personnel.

*Impact:*

* 🔓 Exposure of sensitive user data to unauthorized users
* 📧 Risk of mass email harvesting and phishing attacks
* 🔐 Violation of privacy standards (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA, etc.)
* 🧑‍💼 Unintended access to internal user metadata by unprivileged users

*Root Cause:*

* Lack of *role-based access control (RBAC)* on the {{/api/user/all}} endpoint
* No *authorization checks* to validate the user's privilege level before exposing sensitive data

*Solution:*

* Restrict access to {{/api/user/all}} strictly to users with admin roles
* Implement *granular access control* on all endpoints returning PII
* Log and monitor access to sensitive APIs
* Perform an *access control review* of all authenticated endpoints

*CWE-ID:*

* CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
* CWE-862: Missing Authorization

*OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* A01:2021 – Broken Access Control

Summary: Improper Pricing Validation Allows Free Access and Cashback Abuse
Description:
*Improper Pricing Validation Allows Free Access and Cashback Abuse*
*Severity:* High
 *CVSS Score:* 8.1
 *Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N***Description:*
 The application’s content publishing functionality fails to validate pricing inputs on the server side. This allows content creators (e.g., *Film Labels & Distributors* or *Filmmakers*) to set arbitrary prices—including *zero or negative values*—for movies or series packs.
Once a creator’s *profile is approved by the super admin*, they are allowed to publish content *without further approval on individual content items*. As a result, malicious users can publish a movie or series with negative or manipulated pricing values.
An additional logic flaw is present in the *subscription flow*:

{quote}*If the total amount in the cart matches the price of a movie/series pack, the platform grants free access to the pack without enforcing the payment step.*{quote}

Combining both issues allows attackers to:

* Set a movie's price to a negative value (e.g., -₹50)
* Add it to the cart and trigger free access or even *receive a cashback/discount* equivalent to the manipulated value
* Publish multiple such items, exploiting the pricing logic repeatedly

*Impact:*

* *Free Access to Paid Content*
* *Cashback or Discount Exploitation via Negative Pricing*
* *Loss of Revenue for the Platform and Legitimate Creators*
* *Abuse at Scale by Malicious Actors*
* *Weak Enforcement of Business Logic and Trust Breakdown*

*Root Cause:*

* No *server-side validation* to enforce minimum price thresholds (e.g., price ≥ ₹1)
* Application logic *grants free content* when cart total matches item price
* Platform *does not validate or re-confirm pricing values during checkout*
* *Missing approval requirement for each published item*, despite platform-level security expectations

*Solution:*

* Enforce *backend-side validation* to reject zero and negative pricing
* Introduce a *minimum price floor* for publishable content
* Update cart logic to *enforce checkout validation*, even if the item price equals total cart value
* Require super admin approval for all content with a price below a defined threshold (e.g., ₹10)
* Audit existing published content for *abnormal pricing*

*CWE-ID:*

* CWE-1284: Improper Validation of Specified Quantity in Input
* CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
* CWE-640: Weak Server-Side Enforcement of Business Logic

*OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* A01:2021 – Broken Access Control
* A04:2021 – Insecure Design
* A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration

Summary: Username Enumeration via Forgot Password Mechanism
Description:
*Username Enumeration via Forgot Password Mechanism*
*Severity:* Medium
*CVSS Score:* 5.3
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:N/A:N***Description:*
The application's "Forgot Password" feature exhibits distinct behavior for valid and invalid email addresses. When a registered email is submitted, the server responds with an HTTP {{200 OK}} status and no response body. However, if the email is unregistered, the server responds with a {{400 Bad Request}} status and a clear message: {{"No user found with given email."}}
This discrepancy enables attackers to systematically test email addresses and determine which ones are registered on the platform.*Impact:*

* *User Enumeration:* Malicious actors can harvest valid email addresses registered with the application.
* *Phishing & Social Engineering Risk:* Identified users become targets for tailored phishing campaigns.
* *Brute-force Account Takeover:* Attackers can combine this with password spraying or credential stuffing attacks.

*Root Cause:*

* The system provides different HTTP status codes and error messages for valid vs. invalid user inputs.

*Solution:*

* Standardize responses for both valid and invalid emails during the password reset process.
* Always return a generic message like: {{"If the email exists, you will receive a reset link."}}
* Respond with the same HTTP status code (e.g., {{200 OK}}) regardless of input validity.
* Consider implementing rate-limiting and CAPTCHA to prevent automated enumeration.

*CWE-ID:*

* CWE-203: Observable Discrepancy
* CWE-204: Response Discrepancy Information Exposure

*OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* A01:2021 – Broken Access Control
* A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures

Summary: Unauthenticated API Access Due to Missing Session Token Validation
Description:
*Unauthenticated API Access Due to Missing Session Token Validation*
*Severity:* Critica;
*CVSS Score:* 10.0
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N**

*Description:*
 It was discovered that certain API endpoints function correctly and return user-specific or sensitive data even when the session token is not present in the {{Cookie}} header. This implies that either no authentication is enforced for these endpoints or token validation is improperly implemented.
Such behavior breaks the assumption that authentication is required for accessing protected resources, leading to unauthorized access.

*Impact:*

* *Unauthorized Data Access:* Attackers can access restricted resources without logging in.
* *Privilege Escalation:* If any user-specific or role-based endpoints are exposed, attackers may access or manipulate data not intended for them.
* *Account Takeover Risk:* In absence of token checks, actions can be taken on behalf of other users.
* *Bypass of Access Controls:* Enforcement of session-based authentication is bypassed entirely.

*Root Cause:*

* Missing or incorrectly implemented session/token validation in backend logic.
* API logic trusts requests without checking for valid session cookies or Authorization headers.

*Solution:*

* Enforce authentication on all protected API endpoints.
* Require and validate a valid session token (via {{Cookie}} or {{Authorization}} header) on the server side.
* Implement proper middleware/authentication guards to protect sensitive routes.
* Conduct a review of all endpoints to ensure none are unintentionally exposed.

*CWE-ID:*

* CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
* CWE-862: Missing Authorization

*OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* A01:2021 – Broken Access Control
* A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures

Summary: Improper Session Termination: Logout Doesn’t Clear Cookies (Including Session Token)
Description:
*Improper Session Termination: Logout Doesn’t Clear Cookies (Including Session Token)*
*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.5
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:L/A:N

*Description:*
 It was observed that upon logging out of the application, the session-related cookies, including the session token, remain stored in the browser. The logout action fails to explicitly clear or invalidate these cookies on the client side, nor does it trigger session termination effectively from the server. As a result, reusing the session cookies post-logout allows access to previously authenticated areas of the application.
This indicates a lack of proper session handling and poses a risk of *session fixation* or *unauthorized session reuse*.

*Impact:*

* *Session Hijacking Risk:* If an attacker gains access to a user's device, they could reuse the session token to regain access without needing credentials.
* *Insecure Logout Flow:* The user assumes they have logged out, while their session technically remains active.
* *Non-compliance with Secure Authentication Practices:* May violate compliance requirements (e.g., OWASP, PCI DSS) around session management.

*Root Cause:*

* Failure to clear or expire authentication cookies during logout.
* Backend session/token not invalidated properly upon logout request.

*Solution:*

* On logout, explicitly clear all session-related cookies using proper flags ({{HttpOnly}}, {{Secure}}, and {{SameSite}}).
* Invalidate the session/token on the server to ensure it cannot be reused.
* Set cookie expiration to a past time to remove them from the client browser.
* Verify logout endpoint performs a full session destruction both server and client side.

*CWE-ID:*

* CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration

*OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* A01:2021 – Broken Access Control
* A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures

Affected : [https://dev.festpass.com/*|https://dev.festpass.com/*|smart-link]

Summary: Account Verification Bypass via Manipulation of isApproved Field in Sign-In Response
Description:
*Account Verification Bypass via Manipulation of* {{isApproved}} *Field in Sign-In Response*
*Severity:* High
*CVSS Score:* 8.1
*Vector:* CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:L/UI:N/S:U/C:L/I:H/A:N

*Description:*
 It was identified that the application’s authentication flow is vulnerable to a logic flaw where the account verification status ({{isApproved}}) is determined based on a field received in the *Sign-In API response*, rather than being validated solely on the server side. During sign-in, if the {{isApproved}} field is {{null}}, the frontend restricts access. However, by intercepting the response and changing {{isApproved: null}} to {{isApproved: true}}, the user interface assumes the account is verified and grants access to protected areas.
This client-side trust in server response parameters allows users to bypass the intended account approval or verification flow, gaining access without legitimate authorization.

*Impact:*

* *Bypasses Business Logic:* Allows unverified or disapproved users to gain access to authenticated user functionalities.
* *Privilege Misuse:* May enable users to access resources or initiate actions intended only for verified/approved users.
* *Compliance Risks:* Violates workflow integrity where manual approval or email verification is mandatory.

*Root Cause:*

* Trusting client-side checks on sensitive account status flags.
* Absence of backend-side enforcement for the {{isApproved}} or verification check during login session creation.

*Solution:*

* Ensure account verification ({{isApproved}}) is validated *server-side* before issuing any session or access token.
* Do not expose or rely on modifiable flags in API responses for authentication or access control decisions.
* Reject authentication or limit functionality at the server if the user is not approved, regardless of frontend logic.
* Regularly audit all client-exposed fields to avoid trust on user-controlled values.

*CWE-ID:*

* CWE-290: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
* CWE-302: Authentication Bypass by Assumed-Immutable Data

*OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* A01:2021 – Broken Access Control
* A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures
