Summary: Broken Access Control - Allows to save a shrub without joining it
Description:
h3. Broken Access Control – Unauthorized Download of Paid Content (High)

[https://dev-shrubs.com/vapt/project/testing-access-controls?tab=Members|https://dev-shrubs.com/vapt/project/testing-access-controls?tab=Members|smart-link] 

h4. *Description:*

The application allows users to *subscribe to paid shrubs* before downloading or saving their contents to their project drive. However, it was discovered that *unauthorized users* can bypass this restriction by *modifying session cookies*:

1️⃣ A *legitimate, subscribed user* captures the request when saving content to their *project drive*.
2️⃣ An *unauthorized user* intercepts their own request and *replaces their session cookies* with those from the subscribed user’s request.
3️⃣ The *server does not properly verify the user’s subscription status*, allowing unauthorized users to *save and access paid content without a subscription*.

h4. *Impact:*

⚠️ *Revenue Loss:* Users can *bypass payment requirements*, leading to *financial loss* for the business.
⚠️ *Unauthorized Data Access:* Paid content becomes *freely accessible*, undermining the paywall system.
⚠️ *Privacy Concerns:* Sensitive or *exclusive content* meant for subscribed users is exposed.
⚠️ *Brand Reputation Damage:* Users may *exploit and share* paid content illegally, damaging business credibility.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Session cookies are used for access control instead of server-side validation.*
* *No backend validation of the user’s subscription status* when saving content.
* *Session tokens are not properly tied to user privileges.*

h4. *Suggested Fixes:*

✅ *Enforce Server-Side Subscription Checks:*

* The backend must verify the *user’s subscription status* before allowing content downloads: 
{{if not user.is_subscribed(project_id):     return "Access Denied: You must subscribe to save this content", 403 }}

✅ *Use Token-Based Access Control Instead of Cookies:*

* Implement *JWT (JSON Web Token)* or session-bound tokens that cannot be easily swapped.
* Example JWT payload: 
{{{   "user_id": "12345",   "subscription_status": "active",   "exp": "2025-03-01T00:00:00Z" } }}

✅ *Bind Session Tokens to User Identity:*

* Include *user-agent and IP checks* in session validation: 
{{if request.user.session_id != stored_session_id or request.ip != stored_ip:     return "Session mismatch detected", 401 }}

✅ *Implement Rate Limiting and Session Monitoring:*

* Detect and block *multiple requests with swapped session cookies* from the same IP or device.

✅ *Encrypt and Secure Cookies:*

* Set cookies with *HttpOnly*, *Secure*, and *SameSite=Strict* flags to prevent tampering.
* Example: 
{{response.set_cookie("session_id", session_value, httponly=True, secure=True, samesite='Strict') }}

✅ *Audit Logs for Subscription-Based Actions:*

* Log all content download attempts, *including user ID, subscription status, and session details*.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-639:* Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
* *CWE-384:* Session Fixation

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 – Broken Access Control*

h4. *CVSS Score:* *8.2 (High)*

Summary: Broken Access Control - Delete Shrub Functionality
Description:
h3. Broken Access Control – Unauthorized Project Deletion (Critical)

Method : Delete ; URL : [https://api.dev-shrubs.com/api/projects/9e6bca60-a708-41c5-af18-953d8897c906|https://api.dev-shrubs.com/api/projects/9e6bca60-a708-41c5-af18-953d8897c906]

h4. *Description:*

It was observed that when a *project is shared* with a user, the *project ID is disclosed in the HTTP response* when the shared project URL is accessed.

1️⃣ The *shared user* captures the request while deleting a project *they personally created*.
2️⃣ By *modifying the project ID* in the request to that of a *shared project*, the user was able to *delete the project data* they did not own.
3️⃣ The *frontend dashboard* did not reflect any immediate change, giving the illusion that the project still existed.
4️⃣ However, when the *owner or any user tried accessing the project*, a *server-side error* was thrown, stating that the *data was missing*.

h4. *Impact:*

⚠️ *Unauthorized Data Deletion:* Any user with *shared project access* can *delete critical project data*, disrupting business operations.
⚠️ *Data Integrity Risk:* No authorization checks prevent users from *deleting projects they do not own*.
⚠️ *Business Disruption:* Legitimate users may find their *projects inaccessible*, leading to *loss of important information*.
⚠️ *Potential Abuse:* Malicious users can *target specific users or businesses* by deleting projects *without detection on the frontend*.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Project deletion API does not verify project ownership.*
* *Project ID is disclosed in the response, allowing ID tampering.*
* *No backend validation prevents unauthorized users from deleting shared projects.*
* *Frontend does not reflect deleted projects, delaying detection.*

h4. *Suggested Fixes:*

✅ *Implement Ownership Validation Before Deletion:*

* Ensure *only the project owner* can delete their project.
* Example backend check: 
{{if request.user.id != project.owner_id:     return "Unauthorized: You cannot delete this project", 403 }}

✅ *Use Access Control Lists (ACLs):*

* Allow only *authorized roles* to perform delete actions.
* Example rule-based control: 
{{if not user.has_permission("delete_project", project):     return "Access Denied", 403 }}

✅ *Prevent Project ID Enumeration:*

* *Do not expose project IDs* in responses. Instead, use *hashed or obfuscated identifiers*: 
{{project_uuid = hashlib.sha256(str(project.id).encode()).hexdigest() }}

✅ *Implement Soft Deletion Instead of Hard Deletion:*

* Use a *soft delete flag* ({{is_deleted=True}}) instead of permanently deleting records: 
{{UPDATE projects SET is_deleted = TRUE WHERE id = ?; }}
* This ensures data can be recovered in case of accidental or unauthorized deletion.

✅ *Audit Logs for Deletion Actions:*

* Maintain *logs of all project deletion attempts*, including *user ID, timestamp, and project ID*.
* Example log entry: 
{{{   "user_id": "12345",   "action": "DELETE_PROJECT",   "project_id": "67890",   "timestamp": "2025-02-21T12:34:56Z" } }}

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-922:* Insecure Storage of Sensitive Information
* *CWE-862:* Missing Authorization

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 – Broken Access Control*

h4. *CVSS Score:* *9.1 (Critical)*

Summary: Bypass Suspension Restrictions
Description:
h3. Suspended Project Cloning Bypass – High

h4. [https://dev-shrubs.com/VAPT/project/advanced-tech-pvt-ltd?tab=Members|https://dev-shrubs.com/VAPT/project/advanced-tech-pvt-ltd?tab=Members|smart-link] 

h4. *Description:*

The *project management system* allows admins to *suspend projects* that violate policies. Suspended projects and their inner content are expected to be *inaccessible*.

However, during testing, it was discovered that:
1️⃣ A project owner (*vapt-sh*) created a project and shared it with multiple users.
2️⃣ Some users (*user-3, user-4*) reported the project, leading to *admin suspension*.
3️⃣ The owner (*vapt-sh*) received a *suspension notification* but noticed the option to *clone* the project still existed.
4️⃣ The *clone function* successfully created a duplicate *with full access to all contents*.
5️⃣ Another user (*vapt12*) had the *original URL* saved and *forced browsed it* after suspension.
6️⃣ *Cloned contents were fully accessible*, while the original project remained blurred due to suspension.

h4. *Impact:*

⚠️ *Policy Evasion:* Suspended projects can be revived and accessed freely, *rendering the suspension ineffective*.
⚠️ *Unauthorized Access:* Users with prior URLs can *force browse* and view *cloned versions* of suspended projects.
⚠️ *Data Persistence Risk:* Suspended or *policy-violating content remains available*, creating *compliance risks*.
⚠️ *Potential Abuse:* *Malicious users* can exploit this loophole to *circumvent bans* and keep re-uploading restricted content.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Lack of suspension enforcement on cloned projects.*
* *Clone function does not inherit the "suspended" status of the original project.*
* *Force browsing allows access to cloned versions via previously known URLs.*

h4. *Suggested Fixes:*

✅ *Apply Suspension Inheritance:*

* When a *project is suspended*, all cloned versions should *inherit the suspension* automatically.
* Example SQL update to ensure cloned projects are also marked as suspended: 
{{UPDATE projects  SET status = 'suspended'  WHERE original_project_id = (SELECT id FROM projects WHERE id = ?); }}

✅ *Restrict Cloning for Suspended Projects:*

* *Disable the 'Clone' option* for any project marked as *suspended*.
* Implement a *check in the backend* before allowing cloning: 
{{if project.status == "suspended":     return "Cloning not allowed for suspended projects", 403 }}

✅ *Enforce Authorization on Project Access:*

* Validate *project status before rendering content*.
* Example logic in the API: 
{{if project.status == "suspended":     return "This project is no longer available", 403 }}

✅ *Invalidate Shared URLs After Suspension:*

* *Automatically expire links* when a project is suspended.
* Implement *signed URLs* with expiration timestamps.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-862:* Missing Authorization
* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 – Broken Access Control*

h4. *CVSS Score:* *8.1 (High)*

Summary: Exposure of Admin Path in User's Reporting Request Body
Description:
h3. Exposure of Admin Path in User's Reporting Request Body – High

h4. [https://dev-shrubs.com/mikey/project/ai-the-future-of-tech?tab=Members|https://dev-shrubs.com/mikey/project/ai-the-future-of-tech?tab=Members|smart-link] 

h4. *Description:*

In the *Google Drive-like project management application*, it was observed that when a *user reports a project*, the request body *exposes the admin-only endpoint* named *"flagging"*. Since the *admin and user panels share the same codebase*, only differing in *roles*, an attacker can *force browse* the flagging endpoint and access *reported instances*.

By manually navigating to this endpoint, a *regular user* was able to *view and manipulate reported projects and users*, effectively *escalating privileges* and bypassing access control mechanisms.

h4. *Impact:*

🚨 *Unauthorized Access to Admin Features:* A normal user can *view all reported instances* meant for admins.
🛑 *Data Manipulation:* Attackers can *edit, delete, or modify reports* to remove evidence or falsely report projects.
💀 *Potential Data Exposure:* Since reported projects might contain *sensitive information*, attackers could gain *unauthorized insights*.
⚠️ *Privilege Escalation Risk:* Since roles are not enforced at the backend, attackers could *extend their access beyond intended limits*.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Exposed Admin Endpoint in User Request Body:* The *"flagging"* endpoint is visible in the *user-side reporting request*, revealing its existence.
* *Shared Admin & User Panel with Weak Role Validation:* Instead of a *separate admin panel*, the system relies on *role-based checks*, which were missing or improperly enforced.
* *Improper Access Control Implementation:* The *backend does not restrict access* based on roles, allowing any user to force browse admin endpoints.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

1️⃣ *User Reports a Project:*

* Share a project and subscribe to it.
* Report the project due to false details.
2️⃣ *Intercept the Request Using Burp Suite:*
* Notice the *"flagging"* endpoint exposed in the request body.
3️⃣ *Force Browse to the Admin Flagging Endpoint:*
* Manually navigate to {{/admin/flagging}} or use a tool like *FFUF* or *Burp Intruder* to discover the full path.
* Observe that *reported projects and users* are accessible.
4️⃣ *Modify or Delete Reported Instances:*
* Use *POST/PUT/DELETE* requests to *modify or remove* reported projects.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Implement Proper Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):*

* Ensure the backend enforces *strict role checks* before granting access.
* Example for *RBAC in Laravel*: 
{{if (!Auth::user()->hasRole('admin')) {     abort(403, 'Unauthorized access.'); } }}

✅ *Restrict Access to Admin Endpoints:*

* Admin URLs ({{/admin/*}}) should only be *accessible by admin roles*.
 ✅ *Use Separate Admin Panel Instead of Role-Based Views:*
* Instead of merging admin and user roles in a *single codebase*, create *separate admin routes and views*.
 ✅ *Secure API Endpoints with Middleware:*
* Example in *Express.js*: 
{{app.use('/admin', (req, res, next) => {     if (!req.user || req.user.role !== 'admin') {         return res.status(403).json({ error: 'Access denied' });     }     next(); }); }}

✅ *Validate User Privileges in Every Request:*

* Do *not expose admin paths* in user requests or responses.
* Validate role permissions before serving *any sensitive admin data*.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-285:* Improper Authorization
* *CWE-200:* Exposure of Sensitive Information

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 – Broken Access Control*
* *A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration*

h4. *CVSS Score:* *8.2 (High)*

Summary: No Filetype Validations
Description:
h3. No Filetype Validations – High

h4. [*https://squirrel-ai-api-dev.webelight.co.in/25f9224e-564d-4af4-8a7b-810b54862e50/msme-confirmations/9c1c66cb-5661-4dc0-aa8b-76b3f0cd991a/action/bb1ff362-44cd-4ce1-ad44-97f1b14435db*|https://squirrel-ai-api-dev.webelight.co.in/25f9224e-564d-4af4-8a7b-810b54862e50/msme-confirmations/9c1c66cb-5661-4dc0-aa8b-76b3f0cd991a/action/bb1ff362-44cd-4ce1-ad44-97f1b14435db]

h4. *Description:*

The application allows *MSME vendors* to submit their *Vendor Certificates* for verification. However, during testing, it was observed that *file type validation was missing or weak*, allowing users to *bypass restrictions* and upload potentially malicious files.

By renaming a file from *malicious.php* to *malicious.php.pdf*, the backend still *accepted* the file for upload. This indicates that the application is relying only on *client-side validation* or performing weak *file extension checks*, making it vulnerable to *arbitrary file uploads*.

h4. *Impact:*

🚨 *Remote Code Execution (RCE):* If the server executes uploaded files, attackers could upload and execute *malicious scripts*.
🛑 *Unauthorized Access:* Attackers could upload web shells to gain *unauthorized access* to the system.
💀 *Data Exfiltration:* If stored improperly, sensitive *business/vendor documents* could be accessed and leaked.
⚠️ *Bypassing Business Logic:* Users can upload *non-certificate files*, potentially leading to *fraudulent verifications*.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Missing Server-Side Validation:* The backend *does not verify* the actual file type, relying on *filename extensions*.
* *Client-Side Validation Only:* If only JavaScript or *HTML5 file input filters* are used, attackers can easily *bypass* them.
* *MIME Type Mismatch Not Handled:* The application *does not check* the actual *MIME type* of uploaded files.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

1️⃣ Navigate to the *Vendor Certificate Upload* page.
2️⃣ Select a *valid* certificate file (e.g., {{certificate.pdf}}).
3️⃣ Rename a *malicious script* (e.g., {{malicious.php}}) to {{malicious.php.pdf}}.
4️⃣ Upload the file and intercept the request using *Burp Suite*.
5️⃣ Observe that the file *gets uploaded successfully* despite the invalid file type.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Enforce Server-Side MIME Type Validation:*

* Use {{finfo_file()}} in PHP or similar server-side checks to *validate the file type*.
✅ *Restrict Allowed Extensions & MIME Types:*
* Only allow specific *whitelisted extensions* (e.g., {{PDF, PNG, JPG}}).
* Example for PHP: 
{{$allowed_types = ['application/pdf', 'image/png', 'image/jpeg']; $file_mime = mime_content_type($_FILES['uploaded_file']['tmp_name']); if (!in_array($file_mime, $allowed_types)) {     die("Invalid file type."); } }}

✅ *Rename Files Upon Upload:*

* Store files with *random names and without original extensions* (e.g., {{hashed-filename.dat}}).
 ✅ *Disable Script Execution in Upload Directories:*
* If using Apache, add an {{.htaccess}} file in the upload directory: 
{{<FilesMatch "\.(php|pl|cgi|sh|py)$">     Order Allow,Deny     Deny from all </FilesMatch> }}

✅ *Use Content-Disposition Header:*

* Prevent browsers from executing uploaded files by forcing downloads. 
{{header('Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="document.pdf"'); }}

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-434:* Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
* *CWE-20:* Improper Input Validation

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration*
* *A08:2021 – Software and Data Integrity Failures*

h4. *CVSS Score:* *8.6 (High)*

Summary: OTP Disclosed in Response
Description:
h3. OTP Disclosed in Response – Critical

h4. *Description:*

The application allows an *MSME vendor* to confirm their *status and details* to the *Tenant-Admins* for smooth business operations. When a *tenant initiates a request*, an *email is sent* to the vendor asking them to submit their *MSME Certificate* for verification. After submission, an *OTP is generated and sent* to the vendor's registered email.

However, during testing, it was found that *the OTP is disclosed in the API response itself* when the vendor submits the details. This appears to be a *leftover debugging mechanism* from the development phase that was *not removed from production*, exposing a *critical security flaw*.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Account Takeover:* Attackers can intercept the response and use the OTP to *bypass the vendor verification process*.
* *Business Disruption:* Unauthorized changes to MSME vendor details could *affect business operations* and lead to financial losses.
* *Sensitive Information Exposure:* OTPs should always be kept *confidential*, and revealing them in the API response *compromises authentication security*.
* *Regulatory Non-Compliance:* Many security frameworks (such as *PCI DSS, GDPR, and NIST*) *prohibit the exposure of authentication factors* like OTPs.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Debugging Code Left in Production:* The OTP was *likely logged* in responses during *development and testing* but was *never removed* before deployment.
* *Lack of Secure Logging Practices:* No *proper security checks* were in place to prevent *sensitive data exposure* in API responses.
* *Weak Authentication Flow:* OTPs should *only be delivered securely via email or SMS*, not disclosed in API responses.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

1️⃣ A *Tenant-Admin* sends a verification request to an *MSME vendor*.
2️⃣ The *vendor submits the MSME Certificate* via the web application.
3️⃣ The application triggers an *OTP to the vendor's email*.
4️⃣ Upon submission, inspect the *API response using a proxy tool like Burp Suite*.
5️⃣ Notice that *the OTP is directly included in the response*, allowing *anyone with access to the API response* to retrieve it and *bypass the verification process*.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Remove OTP from API Response:*

* Ensure that *OTPs are never included in API responses* and are only *sent securely via email or SMS*.
✅ *Implement Secure Logging & Debugging Practices:*
* *Strip sensitive data* before sending any response in production environments.
✅ *Enforce OTP Expiry & Rate Limiting:*
* OTPs should expire after a short period (e.g., *5 minutes*) and have a *limit on incorrect attempts*.
✅ *Use Secure Transmission (HTTPS + TLS):*
* Ensure *all OTP transmissions* are *encrypted* and protected from *MITM attacks*.
✅ *Enable Security Headers in API Responses:*
* Use *Content Security Policy (CSP)* and *Referrer Policy* to mitigate *data leakage risks*.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-200:* Exposure of Sensitive Information
* *CWE-522:* Insufficiently Protected Credentials

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 – Broken Access Control*
* *A03:2021 – Sensitive Data Exposure*

h4. *CVSS Score:* *9.7 (Critical)*

Summary: Payment Gateway Bypass – Critical >> False Positive
Description:
h3. Payment Gateway Bypass – Critical

h4. *Description:*

The application offers *multiple subscription plans* and *add-ons* that provide additional storage and features. Once a *payment is completed*, a *POST API request* is triggered to update the user's subscription. However, the application *fails to validate the payment status for each user*, allowing an *attacker to reuse an authenticated request* with a different user's token to grant *unlimited subscriptions without additional payment*.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Revenue Loss:* Attackers can exploit the flaw to *avoid paying for services*, leading to financial losses for the business.
* *Subscription Fraud:* One paid subscription can be *illegally shared* with multiple users.
* *Reputation Damage:* If discovered, this could lead to *trust issues* with legitimate paying customers.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Lack of Payment Verification:* The system *does not check* if a *new payment* was processed for the given *user_id/token*.
* *Flawed Authorization:* The *API accepts authentication tokens from different users*, allowing *subscription transfers*.
* *Client-Side Controlled Logic:* The application relies on a *client-side trigger* instead of *validating purchases on the backend*.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

1️⃣ *User A* (legitimate user) completes a *subscription purchase* and captures the request:

{noformat}pgsql{noformat}

CopyEdit

{{POST https://redacted.com/api/my-subscription-plan/update-subscription Authorization: Bearer {UserA_Token} Body: { "plan": "Premium", "user_id": "12345" } }}

2️⃣ *User B* (attacker) copies *User A’s request* and *replays it in Burp Repeater* with their *own token*:

{noformat}pgsql{noformat}

CopyEdit

{{POST https://redacted.com/api/my-subscription-plan/update-subscription Authorization: Bearer {UserB_Token} Body: { "plan": "Premium", "user_id": "67890" }  }}

3️⃣ *User B* now has *full premium access* without making a payment.
 4️⃣ This method can be *repeated for unlimited users*, granting *free access to anyone* with a valid token.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Enforce Payment Validation:*

* Verify *each subscription purchase* by *checking transaction records* against the payment gateway (e.g., Stripe webhook validation).
 ✅ *Implement Request Authentication:*
* Ensure *only the purchasing user* can trigger the {{update-subscription}} API.
 ✅ *Token Binding:*
* Link authentication tokens to *specific payment sessions* to prevent request replay.
 ✅ *Server-Side Purchase Verification:*
* Implement a *backend check* that confirms the payment status before *activating subscriptions*.
 ✅ *Rate-Limit API Requests:*
* Add *rate limits* and *logging* to detect and prevent *abnormal subscription behavior*.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-285:* Improper Authorization
* *CWE-352:* Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 – Broken Access Control*
* *A06:2021 – Vulnerable and Outdated Components*

h4. *CVSS Score:* *9.8 (Critical)*

Summary: Broken Access Control - Shrub Takeover
Description:
h3. Broken Access Control - Shrub Takeover

h4. [https://app.dev-shrubs.com/projects/%7Bproject-id%7D/edit|https://app.dev-shrubs.com/projects/%7Bproject-id%7D/edit|smart-link] 

h4. *Description:*

The application allows users to create and share shrubs(project/portfolio). However, when opening a *shared shrub URL*, the *project ID* is exposed in the HTTP response. This allows an attacker to *replace their own shrub ID with the shared shrub’s ID* in an *edit request*, effectively gaining unauthorized edit access to any project.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Unauthorized Shrub Modification:* Attackers can modify or delete shrubs they do not own.
* *Data Integrity Issues:* Legitimate users may lose access or have their shrubs tampered with.
* *Potential Data Breach:* If confidential documents are linked, they could be *altered, deleted, or misused*.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* The application does *not properly validate ownership* before allowing edits.
* *Exposed shrub IDs* allow attackers to reference any project.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

1️⃣ *User A* creates a *shrub and shares it for publicity.*
2️⃣ *User B* opens a *shared shrub URL* and extracts the *shrub* from the HTTP response.
3️⃣ *User B* intercepts a request while editing their own shrub.
4️⃣ *User B* *modifies the shrub_id* in the request to *User A's shrub_id*.
5️⃣ Upon sending the modified request, *User B gains edit access* to *User A’s shrub*.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Implement Proper Access Controls:*

* Validate *shrub ownership* on the backend before processing edit requests.
✅ *Use Secure Shrub Identifiers:*
* Instead of *predictable numeric IDs*, use *UUIDs* or other unguessable identifiers.
✅ *Restrict Shared ShrubPermissions:*
* Introduce *read-only links* for shared shrubs, preventing unauthorized modifications.
✅ *Audit and Monitor Access:*
* Log project modifications and *alert admins* on suspicious activity.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 – Broken Access Control*

h4. *CVSS Score:* *9.8 (Critical)*

Summary: Username Enumeration on Login
Description:
h3. Username Enumeration on Login

[https://api.dev-shrubs.com/api/login|https://api.dev-shrubs.com/api/login]

h4. *Description:*

The login functionality reveals whether a username or email exists in the system by returning *different responses* for valid and invalid credentials. This allows an attacker to *enumerate registered usernames or emails*, which can be used for *brute force attacks, credential stuffing, or targeted phishing*.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Account Enumeration:* Attackers can confirm valid usernames or emails.
* *Brute Force & Credential Stuffing:* Attackers can attempt password guessing attacks against known users.
* *Phishing Risks:* Knowing valid accounts, attackers can craft targeted phishing campaigns.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* The application provides *distinct error messages* for existing and non-existing accounts instead of a generic response.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

1️⃣ Navigate to the *Login* page.
2️⃣ Enter a *non-existing username/email* and any password.

* Observe a response: {{"No account found with this username/email."}}
3️⃣ Enter a *valid username/email* but with an incorrect password.
* Observe a different response: {{"Incorrect password."}}
4️⃣ Compare the responses to confirm *username enumeration is possible*.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Use Generic Responses:*

* Display a *consistent error message* for all login failures:
_"Invalid username or password."_
✅ *Rate-Limiting & CAPTCHA:*
* Implement *CAPTCHA* to prevent automated enumeration attacks.
* Apply *rate-limiting* for repeated failed login attempts.
✅ *Monitor Login Attempts:*
* Implement logging & *alert on suspicious activity* (e.g., multiple failed login attempts from the same IP).

Summary: Improper Error Handling Discloses Sensitive Data
Description:
h3. Improper Error Handling Discloses Sensitive Data

h4. [*https://api.dev-shrubs.com/.git/*|https://api.dev-shrubs.com/.git/]

h4. *Description:*

The application *fails to properly handle errors*, leading to exposure of *sensitive server information* and an *open redirect vulnerability* due to HTML injection. When an invalid request is made, the error response *leaks server details such as server name, version, and port number*, providing attackers with reconnaissance information. Additionally, improperly sanitized error messages allow *injection of malicious HTML code*, leading to open redirection.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Information Disclosure:* Attackers can identify server details ({{Apache 2.4.58, Nginx 1.21.6}}, etc.), which helps in targeted attacks.
* *Open Redirect Vulnerability:* Allows phishing attacks by redirecting users to malicious websites.
* *Increased Attack Surface:* Leaked server information may be used for *version-specific exploits*.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Unfiltered Error Messages* displaying stack traces and server metadata.
* *Lack of Proper Output Encoding* in error responses, leading to HTML injection.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

*1️⃣ Check for Server & Port Disclosure:*

* Send a malformed request (e.g., invalid HTTP method): 
{{GET /invalid-page HTTP/1.1 Host: example.com }}
* Observe the response headers or body leaking: 
{{Server: Apache/2.4.58 (Ubuntu) X-Powered-By: PHP/8.2.12 }}

*2️⃣ Exploiting Open Redirect via HTML Injection:*

* Visit an error-prone URL such as: 
{{https://example.com/error?msg=<script>window.location.href='https://attacker.com'</script> }}
* If not sanitized, this will execute in the browser and redirect the user to {{https://attacker.com}}.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Sanitize Error Messages*

* Remove *server details* from HTTP responses ({{ServerTokens Prod}} in Apache, {{server_tokens off;}} in Nginx).
* Implement *custom error pages* instead of raw error dumps.

✅ *Secure Redirection Mechanism*

* Allow redirects only to whitelisted domains.
* Use {{Content-Security-Policy (CSP)}} to prevent injection-based attacks.

✅ *Implement Secure Error Handling*

* Return *generic error messages* ({{"An unexpected error occurred"}}) without exposing stack traces.
* Log detailed errors *only in backend logs*, not in client responses.

h4. *Description:*

The application does not properly restrict access to *sensitive Super Admin endpoints*, allowing unauthorized users to access *restricted functionalities* through direct URL manipulation (forced browsing). Forced browsing attacks exploit predictable URL patterns and insufficient access control mechanisms, allowing attackers to *access admin panels, modify critical settings, and retrieve sensitive information* without authentication.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Unauthorized Super Admin Access:* Attackers can gain access to admin features without valid credentials.
* *Data Exposure & Modification:* Ability to view, edit, or delete sensitive data.

* *Lack of Proper Authentication and Authorization Controls on Restricted Pages.*
* *Predictable URL Structure* allowing attackers to guess sensitive endpoints.
* *Failure to Implement Server-Side Role-Based Access Control (RBAC).*

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Implement Server-Side Authorization Checks*

* Ensure *all admin routes require authentication*.
* Use *JWT tokens* or *session-based authentication* for validation.

✅ *Restrict Direct URL Access with Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)*

* Validate *user roles before granting access*.
* Apply *middleware checks in the backend*.

✅ *Prevent Directory Listing & Implement a Proper Access Policy*

* Disable direct access to admin directories through a *secure .htaccess policy*.
* Configure *permissions in the web server (Nginx/Apache) to prevent unauthorized access*.

✅ *Security Headers & Web Application Firewall (WAF) Rules*

* Implement *Content Security Policy (CSP)* to prevent unauthorized access.
* Use a *WAF* to detect and block forced browsing attempts.

✅ *Monitoring & Logging*

* Enable *detailed logging* of unauthorized access attempts.
* Implement *alerts for suspicious admin page access attempts*.

Summary: Missing DMARC Records
Description:
h3. Missing DMARC Records

h4. *Description:*

The *DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance) record* is missing from the domain’s DNS configuration. *DMARC helps prevent email spoofing and phishing attacks* by allowing domain owners to specify how unauthenticated emails should be handled. Without DMARC, *attackers can send fraudulent emails using the domain, leading to phishing, brand impersonation, and unauthorized access attempts.*

h4. *Impact:*

* *Email Spoofing and Phishing:* Attackers can impersonate the domain to send phishing emails.
* *Brand Reputation Damage:* Users may receive fraudulent emails appearing to come from a trusted domain.
* *Increased Spam Risks:* Without DMARC, the domain is more likely to be used in spam campaigns.
* *Non-Compliance with Security Best Practices:* Many *email providers and security standards (like GDPR and PCI DSS)* recommend DMARC implementation.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* The domain’s *DNS does not contain a valid DMARC (TXT) record*.
* Lack of proper email security configurations.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Use an online tool or terminal command to check DMARC records: 
{{nslookup -type=TXT _dmarc.example.com }}
 OR 
{{dig TXT +short _dmarc.example.com }}
# Observe that *no DMARC record is found* or the record is misconfigured.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Create and Publish a DMARC Record in DNS*

* Add the following TXT record in the domain’s DNS settings: 
{{_dmarc.example.com  TXT  "v=DMARC1; p=reject; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@example.com; ruf=mailto:dmarc-alerts@example.com; fo=1" }}
* Explanation: 
** {{p=reject}} → Blocks spoofed emails from reaching recipients.
** {{rua}} → Email address for aggregate reports.
** {{ruf}} → Email address for forensic reports.
** {{fo=1}} → Generates a forensic report on authentication failures.

✅ *Ensure SPF and DKIM are Configured Correctly*

* SPF Record Example: 
{{example.com  TXT  "v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all" }}
* DKIM Record Example (for Google Workspace): 
{{google._domainkey.example.com  TXT  "v=DKIM1; k=rsa; p=MIGfMA0GCSqG..." }}

✅ *Monitor and Adjust Policies Gradually*

* Start with {{p=none}} for monitoring: 
{{"v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@example.com;" }}
* Move to {{p=quarantine}} and then {{p=reject}} once confident in email security.

Description:
h3. Weak Password Policy for Change Password Functionality

h4. [*https://api.dev-shrubs.com/api/change-password*|https://api.dev-shrubs.com/api/change-password]

h4. *Description:*

The *Change Password* functionality does not enforce strong password policies when users update their passwords. This allows users to set *weak passwords*, making their accounts vulnerable to *brute-force attacks, credential stuffing, and dictionary attacks*.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Increased risk of account compromise* due to easily guessable passwords.
* *Non-compliance with security standards* such as *NIST SP 800-63B, OWASP, and PCI DSS*.
* *Exposes user accounts to credential stuffing attacks*, especially if users reuse compromised passwords.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* The backend does not enforce *minimum password complexity* when users change their passwords.
* Missing validation for *length, uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters*.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Log in to the application.
# Navigate to the *Change Password* section.
# Set a new password such as *"password123"* or *"123456"*.
# Observe that the weak password is *accepted without restrictions*.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Enforce Strong Password Policies in Backend Validation*

* Require *minimum 12 characters*.
* Enforce *at least one uppercase letter, one lowercase letter, one number, and one special character*.
* Block *commonly used passwords* (e.g., "password123", "admin123").

✅ *Implement NIST-Compliant Password Policies*

* Follow *NIST SP 800-63B* password guidelines.
* Use a *password blacklist* to prevent users from using compromised passwords.

✅ *Ensure Backend Validation Matches Frontend Validation*

* Prevent bypassing security by enforcing *server-side password rules*.

✅ *Encourage Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)*

* Even with strong passwords, enabling *MFA* significantly reduces the risk of account compromise.

Summary: Clear Text Credentials Submission
Description:
h3. Clear Text Credentials Submission

[https://api.dev-shrubs.com/api/login|https://api.dev-shrubs.com/api/login] 

h4. *Description:*

The application transmits *user credentials (username and password) in clear text* over an *unencrypted HTTP connection* or *without proper encryption mechanisms in place*. This exposes user credentials to potential *Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) attacks*, allowing attackers to intercept and steal sensitive login information.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Credentials can be intercepted* by attackers if users are on an untrusted network (e.g., public Wi-Fi).
* *Risk of credential theft*, leading to unauthorized access, account takeover, and potential *data breaches*.
* *Violation of security best practices* and *compliance requirements* (e.g., *PCI DSS, GDPR, and OWASP*).

h4. *Root Cause:*

* The application does not enforce *HTTPS* (Secure Sockets Layer/TLS encryption).
* No *encryption mechanisms (e.g., hashing or token-based authentication)* are used when transmitting login credentials.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Open a web browser and navigate to the application's login page.
# Enter *valid login credentials* (username and password) and *capture the request* using a proxy tool like *Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP*.
# Observe that the *credentials are transmitted in clear text* in the HTTP request.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Enforce HTTPS (SSL/TLS)*

* Redirect *all HTTP traffic* to *HTTPS* using *HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)*.
* Use TLS *1.2 or 1.3* to encrypt data transmission.

✅ *Hash and Salt Passwords Before Transmission*

* Use *PBKDF2, bcrypt, or Argon2* to hash passwords before storing them in the database.

✅ *Use Secure Authentication Mechanisms*

* Implement *OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, or JWT tokens* instead of sending passwords in clear text.
* Use *TLS encryption* when transmitting sensitive data.

✅ *Implement HTTP Security Headers*

* Add {{Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload}}.

✅ *Monitor and Log Authentication Requests*

* Implement *intrusion detection and alerting* for suspicious login attempts.

h4. *Description:*

The application does not enforce a *user lockout policy* after multiple *failed login attempts*. This allows attackers to perform *brute-force attacks* or *credential stuffing* without any restrictions, increasing the risk of unauthorized access to user accounts.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Increased risk of account compromise* through password-guessing attacks.
* *Credential stuffing attacks* become easier if users reuse passwords across multiple services.
* *Non-compliance with security standards* such as *OWASP, PCI DSS, and NIST*, which mandate account lockout policies.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *No mechanism to block accounts* after repeated failed login attempts.
* *No rate-limiting or CAPTCHA* to slow down automated login attempts.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Navigate to the login page of the application.
# Enter a valid username and an incorrect password.
# Repeat the login attempt *multiple times* with incorrect credentials.
# Observe that the application *does not lock the account or introduce a delay*, allowing continuous brute-force attempts.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Implement an Account Lockout Mechanism*

* Lock user accounts *after 5-10 failed attempts* for a temporary period (e.g., *5 minutes*).
* Implement *progressive delays* (e.g., increasing lockout time after each failed attempt).
* Allow users to *unlock accounts via email verification or CAPTCHA*.

✅ *Use CAPTCHA after multiple failed login attempts*

* Implement *reCAPTCHA* or similar mechanisms to prevent automated attacks.

✅ *Implement Rate Limiting*

* Restrict the number of login attempts *per IP address or user account*.

✅ *Monitor and Log Login Attempts*

* Implement *logging and alerts* for excessive login failures.
* Alert users about *suspicious login attempts* via email or notifications.

Summary: Sensitive Data Stored in Local Storage
Description:
h3. Sensitive Data Stored in Local Storage

[https://app.dev-shrubs.com/|https://app.dev-shrubs.com/] 

h4. *Description:*

The application stores sensitive user information, including *authentication tokens, usernames, email addresses, and UUIDs*, in the browser's *Local Storage*. Local Storage is *not secure* for storing sensitive data, as it is *accessible via JavaScript* and does not have built-in expiration or protection against XSS attacks.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Exposes user authentication tokens* to potential theft via Cross-Site Scripting (*XSS*) attacks.
* *Allows unauthorized access to user data* if an attacker gains control of the victim’s browser session.
* *Persistent storage of session tokens* increases the risk of *session hijacking* even after logout.
* *Non-compliance with security best practices* and data protection regulations (*GDPR, PCI DSS*).

h4. *Root Cause:*

* Storing sensitive information in *window.localStorage*, which is *easily accessible* by any script running on the same origin.
* Lack of *token expiration* or encryption for stored authentication data.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Open the application and log in with valid credentials.
# Open the browser's *Developer Tools* (F12) → *Application* tab → *Local Storage*.
# Look for stored values like:
#* {{isLogged=true}}
#* {{userToken=<JWT or session token>}}
#* {{username=<User’s name>}}
#* {{email=<User’s email>}}
#* {{uuid=<User’s unique ID>}}
# Copy and use the {{userToken}} in API requests to *bypass authentication* without re-entering credentials.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Use HTTP-only Secure Cookies* for storing authentication tokens instead of Local Storage.
✅ *Implement Secure Session Management*, ensuring tokens are *short-lived and refreshed securely*.
✅ *Encrypt sensitive data* if it must be stored client-side (preferably in memory, not storage).
✅ *Mitigate XSS vulnerabilities* to prevent token theft via injected scripts.
✅ *Ensure tokens are properly invalidated* on logout to prevent persistent access.

* *CWE-315:* Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information
* *CWE-922:* Improper Restriction of Storage in Local Storage

Description:
