h4. *Description:*

When a user changes their password, their active sessions remain valid instead of being *forcefully logged out*. This means that even after a password reset, previous *authenticated sessions* continue to have access until manually logged out.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Compromised accounts remain accessible* even after a password change, making account recovery ineffective.
* *Risk of unauthorized access* if an attacker has already hijacked the session before the password reset.
* *Non-compliance with security best practices* such as *NIST 800-63B* and *OWASP guidelines*, which recommend session revocation upon password changes.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* Failure to *revoke session tokens* after a password reset.
* *Session tokens remain valid*, allowing continued use of an already authenticated session.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Log in to the application from *Device A*.
# Open the same account on *Device B* and navigate to a sensitive page.
# On *Device A*, go to account settings and change the password.
# On *Device B*, refresh the page or navigate to another page.
# Observe that *the session is still active*, even though the password has changed.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Immediately invalidate all active session tokens* when a password change occurs.
✅ *Implement token revocation on backend authentication services* (e.g., AWS Cognito, Keycloak, Firebase, or custom authentication).
✅ *Force logout of all active sessions*, prompting users to log in again using the new password.
✅ *Use a short-lived access token with a refresh token mechanism*, ensuring better session control.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-613:* Insufficient Session Expiration

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures*

h4. *CVSS Score:* *7.5 (High)*

Summary: Old Password Allowed as New Password
Description:
h3. Old Password Allowed as New Password

[https://app.dev-shrubs.com/reset-password/{token}?email={username}|https://app.dev-shrubs.com/reset-password/{token}?email={username}] 

h4. *Description:*

The application allows users to reuse their *previous passwords* when setting a new password. This behavior weakens account security, as attackers who have previously compromised a password can regain access if the user unknowingly reuses it.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Increased risk of credential compromise* if old passwords are leaked in data breaches.
* *Brute-force attacks become easier* since attackers can attempt previously used passwords.
* *Regulatory non-compliance* with standards like *NIST 800-63B, PCI-DSS, and OWASP recommendations*, which discourage password reuse.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Lack of password history enforcement* in authentication mechanisms.
* *Failure to maintain a hashed record of previously used passwords* to compare against new ones.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Log in to the application and change your password (e.g., from {{Password@123}} to {{NewPass@456}}).
# Attempt to change your password back to {{Password@123}}.
# Observe that the system *allows the old password to be reused* without restriction.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Implement a password history policy* to prevent the reuse of the last *5-10 passwords*.
✅ *Store previous passwords in a hashed format* and compare new passwords against them before accepting changes.
✅ *Follow NIST 800-63B guidelines*, which recommend preventing password reuse for a certain period or number of cycles.
✅ *Encourage users to adopt passphrases* instead of simple passwords for better security.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-263:* Password Aging with Insufficient Complexity
* *CWE-521:* Weak Password Requirements

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures*

h4. *CVSS Score:* *5.3 (Medium)*

Summary: Weak Password Policy
Description:
h3. Weak Password Policy

h4. *Description:*

The application enforces a weak password policy, allowing users to create passwords that:

# *Do not require a combination of all character types* (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters).
# *Allow passwords with only one of these categories* instead of enforcing a strong combination.
# *Permit the use of parts of the email address as a password*, making it *easier for attackers to guess credentials*.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Increased risk of brute-force attacks* due to predictable and easily guessable passwords.
* *Credential stuffing attacks* become easier since weak passwords are often reused.
* *Phishing and social engineering attacks* can exploit predictable passwords derived from email addresses.
* *Regulatory non-compliance* with security best practices such as *NIST, PCI-DSS, and OWASP recommendations*.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Lack of strong password enforcement* requiring a mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and special characters.
* *Failure to implement controls preventing email-based passwords.*

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Attempt to set a password containing *only lowercase letters* (e.g., "password").
# Attempt to set a password containing *only numbers* (e.g., "12345678").
# Attempt to set a password containing *part of the email address* (e.g., if email is {{john.doe@example.com}}, set the password as {{john2024!}}).
# Observe that the system *accepts all these weak passwords without restriction*.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Enforce a strong password policy* requiring:

* At least *one uppercase letter (A-Z)*
* At least *one lowercase letter (a-z)*
* At least *one number (0-9)*
* At least *one special character (@, #, $, etc.)*
* Minimum password length of *at least 12 characters*
✅ *Implement a check to prevent the use of parts of the email address* as a password.
✅ *Use password blacklisting* to prevent common weak passwords (e.g., "password123", "admin", "welcome1").
✅ *Encourage multi-factor authentication (MFA)* to add an extra layer of security.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-521:* Weak Password Requirements
* *CWE-308:* Use of a Single-Factor Authentication Mechanism

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 – Identification and Authentication Failures*

Summary: Unlimited Page Scan Bypass
Description:
h3. Unlimited Page Scan Bypass

h4. *Description:*

The application imposes a *50-page limit* for email-based bank statement scanning, enforced via *front-end validations and business logic*. However, during testing, it was observed that *backend validations were missing*, allowing users to *bypass the limit* and scan an *unrestricted number of pages* by manipulating the request.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Resource Exhaustion:* Uncontrolled processing of large documents may *overload the system*, leading to *denial of service (DoS)*.
* *Service Abuse:* Users can *exploit the flaw* to gain more functionality than allowed, potentially causing *financial losses* for the service provider.
* *Violation of Business Rules:* This bypass *undermines billing models* and *affects fair usage policies*.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Backend lacks validation* for the maximum number of pages allowed per scan.
* *Only client-side/front-end restrictions* were implemented, which are easily bypassed.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# *Intercept the request* when submitting a bank statement scan.
# *Modify the page count parameter* to a number greater than 50 (e.g., 500).
# *Forward the request* and observe that the scan proceeds successfully without restriction.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Implement strict backend validation* to enforce the *50-page limit*.
✅ *Reject any requests exceeding the limit*, returning a proper error message.
✅ *Monitor and log anomalies* for excessive usage attempts.
✅ *Apply rate-limiting* to prevent abuse.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-285:* Improper Authorization
* *CWE-601:* Business Logic Errors

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 – Broken Access Control*
* *A04:2021 – Insecure Design*

Summary: Access-Control-Allow-Origin Trusts Arbitrary Domains
Description:
h3. *Access-Control-Allow-Origin Trusts Arbitrary Domains – High*

h4. *Description:*

The application’s CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) policy is *misconfigured*, allowing requests from arbitrary domains. This is indicated by the {{Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *}} header or by dynamically reflecting the {{Origin}} value in the response.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Data theft:* An attacker can *host a malicious website*, trick users into visiting it, and *steal sensitive information* via JavaScript.
* *Account takeover (Session Hijacking):* If authentication tokens are stored in JavaScript-accessible locations (e.g., {{localStorage}}, {{sessionStorage}}), an attacker can extract them from a victim’s session.
* *Privilege escalation:* If internal APIs are accessible from arbitrary origins, attackers could *manipulate user data*.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* The server is *allowing unrestricted access* via {{Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *}}
* It is *reflecting* the request's {{Origin}} header dynamically without validation.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# *Send a malicious request from a third-party domain* 
{{curl -H "Origin: https://malicious.com" -I https://victim.com/api }}
# *Observe the response headers:* 
{{Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://malicious.com }}
 or 
{{Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * }}

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Restrict CORS to trusted domains*

* Instead of {{*}}, explicitly define trusted origins: 
{{Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://trusted-domain.com }}
* If multiple trusted domains exist, maintain a *whitelist* and validate {{Origin}} dynamically.

✅ *Ensure secure CORS headers*

* *Deny credentialed requests from arbitrary origins:* 
{{Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://trusted-domain.com }}

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-942:* Permissive Cross-Domain Policy with Untrusted Domains

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: PHP Version Disclosure
Description:
h3. *PHP Version Disclosure – Low*

h4. *Description:*

The application is disclosing the *PHP version* in HTTP response headers. Although the disclosed version is the latest stable release and *not currently vulnerable*, revealing such information helps attackers identify potential future vulnerabilities when new exploits are discovered.

h4. *Impact:*

* Attackers can *enumerate PHP versions* to check for *publicly known vulnerabilities* in future updates.
* If the server is *not updated regularly*, a disclosed version might become vulnerable over time.
* Attackers can *tailor their attacks* based on the PHP version, increasing the risk of *exploitation*.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* The server is *configured to include the* {{X-Powered-By}} *header*, which reveals the PHP version.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ *Disable PHP version disclosure* in the server configuration:

For *Apache (*{{php.ini}}*)*:

{{expose_php = Off }}

Restart the Apache server after making changes:

{{systemctl restart apache2 }}

For *Nginx (*{{php.ini}}*)*:

{{expose_php = Off }}

Restart PHP-FPM and Nginx:

{{systemctl restart php-fpm systemctl restart nginx }}

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-200:* Exposure of Sensitive Information

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A06:2021 – Vulnerable and Outdated Components*

Summary: Missing Multiple Security Headers
Description:
h3. *Missing Multiple Security Headers – Medium*

h4. *Description:*

The application response is missing critical *security headers*, which help *protect against common web vulnerabilities*. These headers provide additional layers of security by preventing *clickjacking, cross-site scripting (XSS), MIME-type sniffing, and data exposure*.

h4. *Impact:*

Without proper security headers, the application is vulnerable to:

* *Cross-Site Scripting (XSS):* Attackers can inject malicious scripts.
* *Clickjacking:* Users can be tricked into performing actions they didn’t intend to.
* *MIME-type sniffing attacks:* The browser might interpret files incorrectly, leading to security risks.
* *Sensitive Information Exposure:* Lack of CSP allows unrestricted third-party scripts to run.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Web server and application do not include security headers in HTTP responses.*
* *Default configurations missing necessary protections.*

h4. *Headers Found Missing:*

# *Content Security Policy (CSP):* Helps prevent XSS by restricting script sources.
# *X-Frame-Options:* Prevents clickjacking attacks.
# *X-Content-Type-Options:* Prevents MIME-type sniffing.
# *Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS):* Enforces HTTPS connections.
# *Referrer-Policy:* Controls the information sent in the Referer header.
# *Permissions-Policy:* Restricts access to browser features like camera, microphone, and geolocation.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Capture an HTTP response from the application using *Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP, or cURL*: 
{{curl -I https://example.com }}
# Check for missing security headers in the response.

h4. *Solution:*

✅ Configure the *web server* (Nginx/Apache) or *application framework* to include the following headers:

{noformat}add_header Content-Security-Policy "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self';" always; 
add_header X-Frame-Options "DENY" always; 
add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff" always; 
add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload" always; add_header Referrer-Policy "no-referrer-when-downgrade" always; 
add_header Permissions-Policy "geolocation=(), microphone=(), camera=()" always; {noformat}

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-693:* Protection Mechanism Failure
* *CWE-523:* Unprotected Transport of Credentials

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A05:2021 – Security Misconfiguration*

Summary: Residual Account Retention
Description:
h3. *Residual Account Retention – High*

h4. *Description:*

When a user *deletes their account*, they expect their credentials (username and email) to be completely removed from the system. However, attempting to sign up with the same credentials later results in an error stating that the *username and email are already taken*. This indicates that *user data is still being retained* even after account deletion, without a clear mechanism to restore access.

h4. *Impact:*

* *User Confusion & Poor Experience:* Users might assume their data was erased, but it still exists in some form.
* *Privacy & Compliance Risks:* Retaining user data post-deletion *without notifying users* may violate *GDPR, CCPA, or other data protection laws*.
* *Denial of Account Recreation:* Users who accidentally delete their account cannot immediately re-register with the same credentials.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *User credentials (username/email) are still stored in the database* but marked as inactive/deleted.
* *No mechanism to notify users* if their account is in a retention period or provide an option for reactivation.
* *Lack of transparency* in data retention policies for deleted accounts.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# *Sign up* for an account using {{user@example.com}} and a chosen username.
# *Log in* successfully and proceed to *delete the account* from the settings.
# *Try logging in* again → Expected: "Invalid credentials" *(Works as expected)*
# *Try signing up* again using the same email/username →
#* Error: "*Username is already taken.*"
#* Changing the username → Error: "*Email has already been taken.*"

h4. *Solution:*

If user data is *intentionally retained for a few days (e.g., 5, 10, 30 days)* before permanent deletion, the system should:

✅ *Provide a Clear Deletion & Retention Policy:*

* Clearly state that the account will be *soft-deleted for X days* before permanent removal.
* Allow users to *restore access* within the retention period if they change their mind.

✅ *Implement a Recovery Mechanism:*

* When a user tries to *sign up with the same email*, prompt them:
_"This email was recently deleted. Do you want to restore your previous account?"_
* Send a *reactivation link via email*.

✅ *Permanent Deletion After Retention Period:*

* Once the retention period expires, all user data (email, username) should be *completely purged*.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-212:* Improper Removal of Sensitive Data
* *CWE-287:* Improper Authentication

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures*

Summary: Concurrent Login Without Alerts
Description:
h3. *Concurrent Login Without Alerts – Medium*

h4. *Description:*

The application allows multiple simultaneous logins from different devices or locations without notifying the user. This *lacks session monitoring and alerting mechanisms*, making it difficult for users to detect unauthorized access to their accounts.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Account Takeover (ATO):* Attackers can maintain unauthorized access without the victim's knowledge.
* *Session Hijacking Risk:* If an attacker gains session credentials, they can stay logged in indefinitely.
* *User Privacy Violation:* Users remain unaware of active sessions on other devices or locations.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Log into the application from *Device A (e.g., PC).*
# Without logging out, log into the same account from *Device B (e.g., Mobile).*
# Observe that both sessions remain active without any notification or alert.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *No session management mechanism* to track and notify users about concurrent logins.
* *Lack of user session visibility,* allowing attackers to stay logged in unnoticed.
* *No device recognition or session termination options* provided to users.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Send login alerts via email/SMS* when a new session starts from a different device or location.
* *Display active session details* (IP, device, location) in the user account settings.
* *Allow users to terminate active sessions* from their account dashboard.
* *Implement session concurrency controls:*
** *Limit the number of active sessions per user.*
** *Prompt for re-authentication if a new session starts.*
** *Allow users to configure session expiry settings.*

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-384:* Session Fixation
* *CWE-613:* Insufficient Session Expiration

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures*

Summary: Username Enumeration Vulnerability
Description:
h3. *Username Enumeration Vulnerability – Medium*

h4. *Description:*

The application allows attackers to *enumerate valid usernames* by analyzing responses during login, registration, or password reset attempts. This occurs due to *inconsistent system behavior* when a username exists versus when it does not. Attackers can leverage this issue for:

* *Credential stuffing & brute force attacks*
* *Social engineering & phishing campaigns*
* *User account discovery for further exploitation*

h4. *Impact:*

* Attackers can *identify registered users* and target them with phishing attacks.
* *Brute force attacks* become easier by narrowing down valid usernames.
* It enables *automation* of attacks using lists of common usernames and leaked credentials.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Navigate to the *Login*, *Registration*, or *Forgot Password* page.
# Enter a *valid username* (e.g., {{testuser}}) and observe the response.
# Enter a *non-existent username* (e.g., {{randomuser123}}) and compare the response.
# Identify *differences in response messages, HTTP status codes, or response times* that reveal valid usernames.

h4. *Common Indicators of Username Enumeration:*

* *Different error messages:*
** "User does not exist" vs. "Incorrect password."
* *Response timing differences:*
** A valid username results in a delayed response compared to an invalid one.
* *HTTP status code variations:*
** {{200 OK}} for valid usernames and {{404 Not Found}} for invalid ones.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *Authentication system (login, registration, password reset)*
* *User management API responses*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Improper error handling* that leaks the existence of usernames.
* *Lack of uniform response messages for both valid and invalid usernames.*
* *Different HTTP status codes based on username validity.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Use a generic error message* for login and password reset attempts (e.g., "Invalid username or password").
* *Standardize HTTP responses* for both valid and invalid usernames.
* *Implement rate limiting & CAPTCHA* to prevent automated enumeration attempts.
* *Use monitoring & logging* to detect and block enumeration attempts.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-203:* Observable Discrepancy
* *CWE-204:* Response Discrepancy Information Exposure

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures*

Summary: Application Directly Logs in the User Upon Sign-Up
Description:
h3. *Application Directly Logs in the User Upon Sign-Up – High*

h4. *Description:*

Upon completing the *registration process*, the application *automatically logs in* the user without requiring *email verification or explicit authentication*. This security flaw enables:

# *Account Enumeration:* Attackers can mass-register fake accounts and gain access without verification.
# *Session Hijacking Risks:* If a user registers on a *public or shared device*, an attacker can use the pre-authenticated session.
# *Bypassing Email Verification:* If the application does not enforce email confirmation before login, attackers can sign up with *fake or disposable emails* and use the system *without validation*.
# *Privilege Escalation Risk:* If the application assigns default privileges immediately, an attacker could *exploit a misconfigured role* and gain unauthorized access.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Account abuse and fraud:* Attackers can create unlimited fake accounts for *spam, bot attacks, or free-tier abuse*.
* *Credential stuffing risks:* Users assume they are authenticated *without setting a strong password first*.
* *Compromised user security:* A stolen device could grant *immediate unauthorized access* upon registration.
* *Bypassing identity verification:* If email verification is absent, users can impersonate others by using *their email addresses*.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Navigate to the *Sign-Up* page and create a new account.
# Complete the registration process and observe that the system *automatically logs in* the user without requiring authentication.
# Check whether email verification is *enforced or skipped* before granting access.
# If applicable, attempt sign-ups using *temporary email services* (e.g., temp-mail) to check for restrictions.
# Test with different privilege levels to ensure the issue does not result in *automatic admin access* in misconfigured setups.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *User authentication system (auto-login after registration)*
* *Email verification mechanism (missing enforcement)*
* *Session management (immediate session creation)*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Lack of email verification enforcement before login.*
* *Automatic session creation without explicit user authentication.*
* *Default authentication flow logs users in immediately upon registration.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Require email verification before granting access.*
* *After sign-up, redirect users to the login page instead of auto-logging them in.*
* *Invalidate any active session until verification is completed.*
* *Use a secure session management mechanism to prevent unauthorized auto-login.*
* *Enforce CAPTCHA and rate limiting to prevent automated fake account creation.*

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-1390*: Authentication Bypass by Alternate Name
* *CWE-288*: Authentication Bypass Using an Alternate Path or Channel

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures*

Summary: Arbitrary File upload Vulnerability
Description:
h3. *Arbitrary File Upload Vulnerability – High*

h4. *Description:*

The application does not enforce *file type restrictions* when handling file uploads, allowing users to upload *any file type* without validation. This weakness enables attackers to upload *malicious files*, such as *web shells, executable scripts, malware, or phishing pages*, leading to severe security risks.

If the uploaded file is stored in a *publicly accessible directory*, an attacker may directly access and execute it, potentially resulting in *remote code execution (RCE), data breaches, or complete system compromise*. Even non-executable files (e.g., {{.exe}}, {{.bat}}, {{.vbs}}) could be exploited for *social engineering, malware distribution, or privilege escalation*.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Remote Code Execution (RCE):* Uploading a *malicious script (PHP, JSP, ASPX, etc.)* can grant the attacker control over the server.
* *Privilege Escalation:* Attackers may upload malicious files to exploit vulnerabilities and escalate privileges.
* *Malware Distribution:* The server can be used to host trojans, ransomware, or spyware.
* *Phishing Attacks:* Attackers can upload fake login pages to steal user credentials.
* *Denial of Service (DoS):* Uploading large or malformed files can overload the application, leading to crashes.
* *Sensitive Data Exposure:* If uploaded files are publicly accessible, they can be downloaded and misused by unauthorized users.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Navigate to the *file upload functionality* in the application.
# Select a *malicious file* (e.g., {{shell.php}}, {{malware.exe}}, or {{fake-login.html}}).
# Upload the file and observe that the server does not validate or restrict the file type.
# If uploaded successfully, access the file's location and attempt execution.
# If possible, upload multiple payloads ({{.jsp}}, {{.aspx}}, {{.sh}}, {{.bat}}) to check the extent of the vulnerability.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *File upload API (no validation on file type and MIME type)*
* *Storage system (allows unrestricted file types)*
* *Access control (if uploaded files are publicly accessible and executable)*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Lack of file type validation*: The application does not enforce a whitelist of allowed file types.
* *No MIME type verification*: Attackers can bypass extension-based validation by renaming files.
* *Files stored in an executable directory*: Allows direct execution of uploaded scripts.
* *Missing access controls on uploaded files*: Permits unauthorized access and execution.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Enforce strict file type validation*:
** Allow only necessary file types (e.g., {{.jpg}}, {{.png}}, {{.pdf}}).
** Block script and executable files ({{.php}}, {{.jsp}}, {{.exe}}, {{.sh}}, {{.bat}}).
* *Verify file content using MIME type checking* rather than relying solely on extensions.
* *Rename uploaded files* to prevent direct execution (e.g., randomizing filenames).
* *Store files outside the web root* and serve them via secure mechanisms.
* *Apply access controls to uploaded files* to restrict unauthorized execution.
* *Implement virus scanning on uploaded files* using tools like ClamAV or other security solutions.
* *Set file size limits* to prevent DoS attacks.
* *Use Content Security Policy (CSP)* to block execution of uploaded files.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-434*: Unrestricted File Upload
* *CWE-20*: Improper Input Validation

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A06:2021 - Vulnerable and Outdated Components*
*

Summary: Improper Access Controls Allow Renaming of Arbitrary Files – Critical
Description:
h3. *Improper Access Controls Allow Renaming of Arbitrary Files – Critical*

h4. *Description:*

The application allows users to rename files in their workspace. However, we identified a *critical access control vulnerability* where an attacker can rename *any file, including files they do not own*, by modifying the {{file_id}} parameter in the rename request. This issue allows unauthorized users to rename arbitrary files belonging to other users.

Additionally, an attacker can launch a *brute-force attack on* {{file_id}} to rename multiple files across different user accounts, leading to *data inconsistency, confusion, and potential operational disruption*. This flaw can be exploited to *rename sensitive files, making them difficult to locate or rendering applications non-functional* if they rely on specific filenames.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Capture the request to rename a file from the user's workspace.
# Modify the {{file_id}} parameter to reference a file owned by another user.
# Change the {{new_filename}} value to an arbitrary string.
# Send the modified request and observe that the file is successfully renamed with *200 OK* status.
# The legitimate owner of the file now sees an unexpected name change, leading to confusion and disruption.
# Automate the attack by iterating through possible {{file_id}} values to *rename multiple files across different accounts*.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Unauthorized File Modification:* Any user can rename files they do not own.
* *Operational Disruption:* Critical files may be renamed, breaking dependent services or causing application failures.
* *Loss of Data Integrity:* Users may lose track of important files due to unintended renaming.
* *Security Bypass:* Attackers can rename files to misleading names, potentially facilitating *phishing attacks* or *data obfuscation*.
* *Denial of Service (DoS) Risk:* A brute-force attack on {{file_id}} can rename all files, making them unrecognizable and difficult to retrieve.
* *Legal & Compliance Risks:* Unauthorized modification of user data can lead to compliance violations under regulations like *GDPR* or *PCI DSS*.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *Rename file API* (vulnerable endpoint that allows arbitrary renaming).
* *File permission enforcement mechanism*.

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Missing Ownership Validation:* The backend does not verify if the user renaming the file is its legitimate owner.
* *Improper Access Control:* The application allows any authenticated user to rename files belonging to others.
* *Lack of Referential Integrity:* The rename operation executes without validating file ownership constraints.
* *No Rate Limiting on Rename Operations:* The endpoint does not restrict the number of rename requests, allowing brute-force enumeration of {{file_id}} values.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Implement strict access control* to ensure users can only rename files they own.
* *Validate file ownership* before allowing rename operations. The system should check if {{file_id}} belongs to the authenticated user.
* *Use unique, user-specific file identifiers* instead of predictable {{file_id}} values.
* *Implement role-based access control (RBAC)* to restrict rename permissions based on user roles.
* *Enforce rate limiting and monitoring* to prevent brute-force attacks on {{file_id}}.
* *Implement proper error handling* to ensure unauthorized rename attempts return a {{403 Forbidden}} instead of {{200 OK}}.
* *Log and audit file rename activities* to detect and prevent unauthorized modifications.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-862*: Missing Authorization
* *CWE-285*: Improper Authorization
* *CWE-639*: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 - Broken Access Control*

Summary: Application Logic Error - Move File Functionality
Description:
h3. *Application Logic Error – Move File Functionality – Critical*

h4. *Description:*

The application allows users to move files within their workspace. However, we identified a flaw in the move file functionality where an attacker can manipulate the {{file_id}} parameter in the request and move a file that *does not belong to them*. This results in the file disappearing from both the attacker's and the original owner's workspaces, leading to unauthorized file modification and potential data loss.

Additionally, an attacker can *brute-force the* {{file_id}} *parameter* to systematically move or delete files across multiple user accounts, leading to a *denial of service (DoS)* scenario where all files become unavailable.

h4. *Steps to Reproduce:*

# Capture the request for moving a file from the drive to a pre-created folder.
# Modify the {{file_id}} parameter to reference a file that belongs to another user.
# Send the modified request and observe the response returns *200 OK*.
# The file is removed from both users' workspaces, leading to *data loss and unavailability*.
# Automate the attack by iterating through potential {{file_id}} values to systematically move files, making them inaccessible for legitimate users.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Unauthorized File Manipulation:* Any user can move or delete files owned by others.
* *Data Loss:* The file is no longer accessible to either user.
* *Security Bypass:* Lack of proper ownership checks allows users to modify data beyond their privileges.
* *Denial of Service (DoS):* An attacker can launch a brute-force attack on {{file_id}}, making all files unavailable to legitimate users.
* *Business Disruption:* Users will be unable to access critical files, impacting productivity and service availability.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *Move file API* (likely a vulnerable endpoint handling file relocation).
* *File permission enforcement mechanism.*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Missing Ownership Validation:* The application does not verify if the user moving the file is its legitimate owner.
* *Improper Access Control:* The backend does not check if the {{file_id}} belongs to the authenticated user.
* *Lack of Referential Integrity:* The move operation executes without validating file ownership constraints.
* *No Rate Limiting on File Operations:* The endpoint does not restrict the number of file move requests, making brute-force attacks feasible.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Implement strict access control* to ensure users can only move files they own.
* *Validate file ownership* by checking if {{file_id}} belongs to the authenticated user before processing the request.
* *Enforce rate limiting and anomaly detection* to prevent brute-force attacks on {{file_id}}.
* *Use unique session-based tokens* to prevent request tampering.
* *Log and monitor file movement requests* to detect and prevent unauthorized file manipulations.
* *Implement proper error handling* so unauthorized actions return a {{403 Forbidden}} instead of {{200 OK}}.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-862*: Missing Authorization
* *CWE-285*: Improper Authorization
* *CWE-639*: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 - Broken Access Control*

Summary: Improper Input Validation: Unlimited characters Allowed
Description:
h3. *Improper Input Validation: Unlimited Characters Allowed – Medium*

h4. *Description:*

The application does not enforce a maximum character limit for input fields, allowing users to enter *unlimited characters* in text fields such as *username, email, password, and other form inputs*. This improper input validation can lead to *performance issues, database corruption, and potential security risks such as denial of service (DoS) or buffer overflow attacks*.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Denial of Service (DoS)*: Attackers can send excessively long input, leading to high memory and CPU consumption, causing application crashes.
* *Database Corruption*: Unrestricted input length can *overflow database columns*, causing unexpected errors or data truncation.
* *Security Risks*: May enable *XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) or SQL Injection* if combined with poor sanitization.
* *Poor User Experience*: Allowing excessive input may degrade usability and UI rendering.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *Registration, login, and profile update forms*
* *Any text input fields, including search boxes and feedback forms*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *No character limit defined* for input fields in the frontend or backend.
* *Missing server-side validation* to restrict input length.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Enforce character limits* for all input fields based on expected data length. Example:
** Username: *3-50 characters*
** Password: *8-64 characters*
** Email: *5-255 characters*
** Comments/Descriptions: *Max 500 characters*
* *Implement server-side validation* in addition to frontend checks.
* *Sanitize and escape input data* to prevent security risks.
* *Use database constraints* (e.g., {{VARCHAR(255)}}) to prevent data overflow issues.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-20*: Improper Input Validation

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 - Broken Access Control* (if exploited for DoS)
* *A03:2021 - Injection* (if combined with malicious payloads)

Summary: Improper Error Handling leads to Internal Server Error
Description:
h3. *Improper Error Handling Leads to Internal Server Error – Low*

h4. *Description:*

The application fails to handle errors gracefully, resulting in an *Internal Server Error (HTTP 500)* when unexpected conditions occur. This typically happens due to *unhandled exceptions, database query failures, or invalid user input*. Improper error handling can expose sensitive system behavior and degrade user experience.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Information Leakage*: Attackers may infer backend technologies, frameworks, or database structures from verbose error messages.
* *Denial of Service (DoS)*: Repeatedly triggering an unhandled error could cause *server crashes or disruptions*.
* *Poor User Experience*: Users may face unexpected crashes without proper error messages or guidance.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *API endpoints and web application backend*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Lack of proper exception handling* in the application code.
* *Failure to return user-friendly error messages*, exposing raw system errors.
* *Missing input validation*, leading to unhandled scenarios.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Implement proper exception handling* for all API endpoints and backend logic.
* *Use generic error responses* to prevent information leakage (e.g., return HTTP 400/500 with a generic message instead of stack traces).
* *Enable logging and monitoring* to track server errors without exposing details to users.
* *Implement proper input validation* to prevent unexpected crashes due to invalid input.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-209*: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A09:2021 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures*

Summary: Application Using Vulnerable Version of Vue.js
Description:
h3. *Application Using Vulnerable Version of Vue.js – High*

h4. *Description:*

The application is using an *outdated and vulnerable version of Vue.js (2.7.16)*, which is susceptible to a *Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) vulnerability (CVE-2024-9506)*. This issue arises due to *inefficient regex evaluation in the* {{parseHTML}} *function*, which can be *exploited by an attacker to cause excessive resource consumption, leading to potential application crashes or performance degradation*.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Denial of Service (DoS)*: Attackers can craft malicious input to exploit the inefficient regex, causing excessive CPU usage and degrading application performance.
* *Security Risk*: Using outdated libraries increases the attack surface and can lead to further exploitation if additional vulnerabilities are discovered.
* *Regulatory Compliance Issues*: Applications handling sensitive data may be non-compliant with security best practices and standards.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *Vue.js JavaScript Library (Version 2.7.16) used in frontend code*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Application relies on an outdated and vulnerable Vue.js version that has not been patched for known security flaws.*
* *Lack of dependency management and security patching process.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Upgrade Vue.js to the latest secure version* that addresses the vulnerability. If migration to Vue 3 is not feasible, update to the latest *Vue 2 LTS version* where possible.
* *Regularly monitor and update third-party dependencies* to prevent security risks from outdated components.
* *Implement a dependency management process* that checks for known vulnerabilities using tools like:
** {{npm audit}} (for JavaScript dependencies)
** {{Snyk}}, {{Dependabot}}, or {{OWASP Dependency-Check}} for automated vulnerability tracking.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-1104:* Use of Unmaintained Third-Party Components

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A09:2021 - Using Components with Known Vulnerabilities*

Summary: Clickjacking Vulnerability
Description:
h3. *Clickjacking Vulnerability*

h4. *Description*

The application was found to be vulnerable to *Clickjacking* due to missing or improperly configured *X-Frame-Options* and *Content-Security-Policy (CSP) frame-ancestors* headers. Clickjacking allows an attacker to *embed the website within an invisible iframe on a malicious domain*, tricking users into performing unintended actions such as *clicking buttons, submitting forms, or executing transactions*.

This can lead to:

* *Account takeovers* (e.g., forcing users to change their email/password).
* *Unintended fund transfers or actions on financial platforms*.
* *Privilege escalation by tricking admins into executing sensitive operations*.

h4. *Impact*

* *User Interface Redress Attacks* can *trick users into performing unauthorized actions*.
* *Potential phishing attacks* by embedding the application in a malicious site.
* *Compromised sensitive actions* such as *financial transactions or setting changes*.

h4. *Solution*

* *Set the* {{X-Frame-Options}} *header* to {{DENY}} or {{SAMEORIGIN}} to prevent embedding in iframes.
* *Use Content-Security-Policy (CSP)* {{frame-ancestors}} *directive* to explicitly define trusted origins.
* *Implement frame-busting JavaScript* as an additional defense mechanism:
{noformat}javascript{noformat}
