{{if (self !== top) {     top.location = self.location; } }}

h4. *References*

* CWE-1021: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames
* OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration
* Clickjacking Defense Cheat Sheet

h4. *Technical Details*

* *Severity*: Medium
* *CVSS Score*: 6.3
* *CWE-ID*: CWE-1021
* *OWASP Top 10 Category*: A05 - Security Misconfiguration

Summary: Improper Handling of Subscription Plan Downgrading
Description:
h3. *Improper Handling of Subscription Plan Downgrading – High*

h4. *Description:*

The application *fails to properly handle subscription downgrades*, resulting in *immediate loss of access to previously paid services without a refund*. While upgrading correctly requires paying the price difference, *downgrading immediately restricts access without adjusting the billing*. This creates a financial loss for users who may have paid for a higher plan but get locked out before their subscription period ends.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Users lose money* when downgrading, as the previous plan’s paid amount is not prorated or refunded.
* *Negative user experience* leading to frustration and possible legal disputes.
* *Potential compliance issues* with consumer protection laws regarding fair billing practices.
* *Trust issues* that may reduce customer retention and satisfaction.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *Subscription downgrade logic in the billing system*
* *User account service handling plan changes*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Application does not properly prorate or refund the remaining subscription balance when downgrading.*
* *Immediate access restriction instead of allowing the higher plan to remain active until the billing cycle ends.*
* *No clear policy or warning for users about the implications of downgrading.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Implement a prorated refund or credit system* when users downgrade before their current billing period ends.
* *Allow users to continue using their higher-tier plan until the current billing cycle expires,* rather than immediately enforcing the downgrade.
* *Show a clear warning message* before confirming the downgrade, informing users of potential impacts on service access.
* *Follow standard industry practices for subscription management*, ensuring fairness in upgrades and downgrades.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-863:* Incorrect Authorization
* *CWE-285:* Improper Authorization

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 - Broken Access Control*

Summary: User able to modify the Subscription Plan Value
Description:
h3. *User Able to Modify the Subscription Plan Value – Critical*

h4. *Description:*

The application *fails to properly validate subscription plan values on the client-side and server-side*, allowing users to *modify their subscription plan arbitrarily*. By intercepting and modifying API requests, an attacker can upgrade their plan or reduce costs without proper authorization.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Users can access premium features for free* by changing the subscription value.
* *Financial loss for the company* due to unauthorized upgrades.
* *Subscription integrity compromised*, leading to potential misuse of services.
* *May impact legitimate users* if unauthorized modifications result in unexpected behavior.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *Subscription management API*
* *Client-side validation (if applicable)*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Lack of server-side validation for subscription plan values.*
* *Client-side trust for critical business logic.*
* *No integrity checks on subscription data.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Enforce server-side validation* to ensure that subscription values match valid plans.
* *Use an immutable reference ID* for subscription plans instead of accepting user-controlled values.
* *Implement digital signatures* or HMAC-based verification to prevent tampering.
* *Ensure role-based access controls (RBAC)* are properly enforced for subscription modifications.
* *Log and monitor all subscription changes* to detect anomalies.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-285:* Improper Authorization
* *CWE-639:* Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A01:2021 - Broken Access Control*

Summary: No Verification of Email upon Registration
Description:
h3. *No Verification of Email upon Registration – High*

h4. *Description:*

The application *does not enforce email verification* upon user registration. This allows users to create accounts with fake or non-existent email addresses, leading to account abuse and fraud.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Users can create unlimited fake accounts*, bypassing plan upgrades or rate limits.
* *Legitimate users may be prevented from registering* if someone else signs up using their email.
* *Spam and fraudulent account creation* could increase, affecting system performance and integrity.
* *Password recovery and security notifications* become ineffective without email verification.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *User registration process*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *No email verification mechanism implemented before account activation.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Enforce email verification* before granting full access to the account.
* *Use one-time email verification links* that expire after a short duration.
* *Prevent login and sensitive actions* until the email is verified.
* *Rate-limit sign-ups from the same IP* to reduce automated abuse.
* *Implement reCAPTCHA on the registration page* to prevent bot-driven sign-ups.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-601:* URL Redirection to Untrusted Site (‘Open Redirect’)
* *CWE-255:* Credentials Management Errors

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures*

Summary: Session Expiry too Long
Description:
h3. *Session Expiry Too Long – High*

h4. *Description:*

The *Strapi admin panel session tokens have an expiration period of nearly a month*, allowing prolonged access without requiring reauthentication. This significantly increases the risk of *session hijacking* and *unauthorized access*, especially if an attacker gains access to the session token.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Increases exposure time for stolen session tokens*, making them valuable for attackers.
* *Compromised sessions remain valid for an extended period*, even if an administrator forgets to log out.
* *Users who leave their sessions active on shared/public systems are at risk of unauthorized access.*

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *Strapi admin panel authentication mechanism*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Session tokens are set with an excessively long expiration period without forced reauthentication.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Reduce session expiration time* to a reasonable duration (e.g., 15-30 minutes of inactivity).
* *Implement idle session timeout*, requiring reauthentication after inactivity.
* *Use short-lived access tokens with refresh tokens* for better security.
* *Enforce logout on session expiration* to prevent reuse of old tokens.
* *Monitor active sessions* and revoke any suspicious or long-standing sessions.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-613:* Insufficient Session Expiration

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures*

Summary: Admin Email in Session Token
Description:
!image-20250227-165147.png|width=1536,height=794,alt="image-20250227-165147.png"!

h3. *Admin Session Token in Session Storage*

h4. *Description:*

The *admin panel stores session tokens in session storage*, which poses a security risk. *Session storage is accessible via JavaScript* and can be retrieved by an attacker if *Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities exist* in the application. Since session tokens are crucial for maintaining authentication, an attacker who gains access to them can *hijack admin sessions and gain unauthorized access to sensitive functionalities*.

h4. *Impact:*

* *If an XSS vulnerability exists, attackers can steal session tokens and hijack admin accounts.*
* *Session tokens are exposed to client-side JavaScript, increasing attack surface.*
* *Higher risk of privilege escalation if an attacker gains access to an admin session.*

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *Admin panel authentication mechanism*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Session tokens are stored in session storage instead of HTTP-only secure cookies.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Use HTTP-only, secure, and SameSite=strict cookies* to store session tokens instead of session storage.
* *Implement Content Security Policy (CSP)* to mitigate XSS risks.
* *Sanitize all user inputs* to prevent potential XSS attacks.
* *Enable short-lived session tokens* with refresh token mechanisms to reduce exposure time.
* *Monitor session activity* and revoke stolen sessions upon suspicious activity detection.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-565:* Reliance on Cookies without Validation and Integrity Checking

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures*

Summary: Session Token Doesn't Expire After Logout
Description:
h3. *Session Token Doesn't Expire After Logout*

h4. *Description:*

The *admin panel and Strapi panel* were found to retain the session token even after the user logs out. This issue allows an attacker with access to a previously used session token to *re-authenticate and regain access to the application*, even after the original user has logged out.

Failure to invalidate session tokens upon logout increases the risk of *session hijacking* and *persistent unauthorized access* if an attacker gains access to a leaked or stolen token.

h4. *Impact:*

An attacker with access to a user's session token could:

* *Regain access to the admin panel or Strapi panel* after logout.
* *Perform unauthorized actions* under the victim’s account.
* *Maintain long-term access*, even if the user logs out manually.
* *Bypass authentication controls*, leading to privilege escalation if an admin token is compromised.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *Admin Panel*
* *Strapi Panel*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Session tokens are not invalidated on the server-side upon logout.*
* *Lack of session revocation mechanism.*
* *Token expiration is not enforced or refreshed on logout.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Invalidate the session token on logout* by removing it from the server-side session store.
* *Implement token blacklist/whitelist mechanisms* to prevent reuse of old tokens.
* *Set a short expiration time* for access tokens and use refresh tokens with strict expiry policies.
* *Use HTTP-only, Secure, and SameSite attributes* for cookies to reduce session hijacking risks.
* *Ensure the backend explicitly revokes tokens* when a user logs out.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-613:* Insufficient Session Expiration

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures*

Summary: No Forgot Password Functionality
Description:
h3. *No Forgot Password Functionality – Medium*

h4. *Description:*

The application *does not provide a "Forgot Password" functionality*, preventing users from resetting their passwords if they lose access. This lack of a recovery mechanism forces users to *reuse weak passwords* or *create duplicate accounts*, increasing security risks.

Without a password reset feature, *users may remain locked out permanently*, leading to *poor user experience and potential account takeovers* if weak workarounds (e.g., contacting support for manual resets) are exploited.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Account lockout risk* if a user forgets their password.
* *Encourages weak password reuse*, as users may choose memorable but insecure passwords.
* *Higher support overhead* for manual password resets.
* *Security risk from weak manual reset mechanisms* (e.g., customer service-based resets).

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *User authentication mechanism*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *Lack of a self-service password reset feature.*
* *No email or multi-factor recovery options.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Implement a "Forgot Password" feature* with a secure recovery mechanism:
** *Password reset link sent via email*, expiring in a short period.
** *Multi-factor authentication (MFA) for verification* before reset.
** *Security questions or backup codes* as an additional recovery method.
* *Use secure token-based reset links* (e.g., JWT or cryptographic tokens).
* *Rate-limit password reset requests* to prevent abuse.
* *Ensure proper logging and alerts* for unusual password reset activity.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-640:* Weak Password Recovery Mechanism for Forgotten Password

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures*

Summary: Weak Password Policy – No Requirement for Special Characters
Description:
h3. *Weak Password Policy – No Requirement for Special Characters*

h4. *Description:*

The application allows users to set passwords *without requiring special characters*. This weak password policy makes it easier for attackers to *brute-force or guess passwords*, increasing the risk of account compromise.

A lack of complexity requirements *reduces password entropy*, making them susceptible to *dictionary attacks, credential stuffing, and offline cracking* if password hashes are leaked.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Increased risk of brute-force attacks* due to predictable and weak passwords.
* *Higher likelihood of credential stuffing* if users reuse passwords across platforms.
* *Weak protection against password hash cracking* in case of a database breach.
* *Non-compliance with security best practices* for password policies.

h4. *Affected Components:*

* *User authentication mechanism*

h4. *Root Cause:*

* *No enforced policy for special character inclusion in passwords.*
* *Lack of password strength validation.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Enforce a strong password policy* that includes at least:
** *8-12 characters minimum length*
** *Uppercase and lowercase letters*
** *At least one number and one special character* (e.g., {{@, #, $, %}})
* *Implement rate limiting* to prevent brute-force attacks.
* *Use password hashing mechanisms like bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2.*
* *Encourage the use of password managers* for storing strong passwords.

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-521:* Weak Password Requirements

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures*

Summary: Multiple Nginx Server Vulnerabilities
Description:
h3. *Multiple Nginx Server Vulnerabilities*

h4. *Description:*

Multiple vulnerabilities were identified in the *Nginx 1.24.0* web server. These vulnerabilities include *Remote Code Execution (RCE), Denial of Service (DoS), and HTTP/2 attack vectors*. The vulnerabilities have been assigned *CVE IDs and exploit references*, some of which have a *CVSS score of 10.0 (Critical)*, indicating severe impact.

These issues can be exploited by attackers to *execute arbitrary code, disrupt services, or gain unauthorized access to the system*. Notably, *CVE-2023-44487* allows HTTP/2 Rapid Reset attacks, potentially leading to server unavailability.

h4. *Impact:*

An attacker could leverage these vulnerabilities to:

* *Remotely execute arbitrary code* on the server.
* *Perform denial-of-service attacks*, making the web service unavailable.
* *Exploit HTTP/2 misconfigurations* to exhaust server resources.
* *Gain unauthorized access* or escalate privileges depending on the exploit used.

h4. *Affected Versions:*

* *Nginx 1.24.0* (as detected in the scan).

h4. *Exploits & References:*

* *CVE-2023-44487 (HTTP/2 Rapid Reset Attack)* - [Reference|https://vulners.com/cve/CVE-2023-44487]
* *Critical Exploit (CVSS 10.0)* - [Exploit Code|https://vulners.com/githubexploit/2C119FFA-ECE0-5E14-A4A4-354A2C38071A]
* *Multiple Exploits with CVSS 7.5 - 8.8* - [Details|https://vulners.com/]

h4. *Root Cause:*

The vulnerabilities stem from:

* *Unpatched Nginx version* containing security flaws.
* *HTTP/2 protocol weaknesses*, allowing rapid reset attacks.
* *Improper request handling*, leading to potential memory corruption or code execution.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Upgrade Nginx* to the latest stable version (1.25 or higher).
* *Disable HTTP/2* if not required ({{http2_max_requests 1;}} can mitigate HTTP/2 DoS).
* *Apply patches and security updates* from the official Nginx repository.
* *Restrict server information disclosure* ({{server_tokens off;}} in {{nginx.conf}}).
* *Enable a Web Application Firewall (WAF)* to block known exploit patterns.
* *Monitor logs for unusual traffic* ({{access.log}} and {{error.log}}).

h4. *CWE-ID:*

* *CWE-94:* Improper Control of Code Generation
* *CWE-400:* Uncontrolled Resource Consumption

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (2021) Category:*

* *A06:2021 - Vulnerable and Outdated Components*
* *A09:2021 - Security Logging and Monitoring Failures*

Summary: Multiple SSH Vulnerabilities on Application Server
Description:
h3. *Multiple SSH Vulnerabilities on Application Server*

h4. *Severity:* Critical

h4. *Affected Service:* OpenSSH 9.6p1 (Ubuntu 3ubuntu13.8)

h4. *Port:* 22/tcp

h4. *Description:*

The target system is running OpenSSH 9.6p1 on Ubuntu, which has multiple known vulnerabilities. These vulnerabilities could allow remote attackers to execute arbitrary code, escalate privileges, or perform unauthorized actions on the system.
Several exploits are publicly available, increasing the risk of compromise.

h4. *Impact:*

An attacker could leverage these vulnerabilities to gain unauthorized access to the server, execute arbitrary commands, and potentially compromise the entire infrastructure.
Since SSH is typically a critical service for system administration, a compromise could lead to severe consequences, including data breaches and full system takeover.

----

h3. *Technical Details*

* *CVE:* [CVE-2024-6387|https://vulners.com/cve/CVE-2024-6387]
* *CVSS Score:* 8.1 - 10.0 (depending on the exploit)
* *Exploit Availability:* Multiple publicly available exploits
* *Exploitable:* Yes

h4. *Relevant CWE:*

* *CWE-284:* Improper Access Control
* *CWE-89:* Improper Neutralization of Special Elements Used in an SQL Command (‘SQL Injection’)
* *CWE-20:* Improper Input Validation

h4. *OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Mapping:*

* *A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures*
* *A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration*

----

h3. *Root Cause:*

* Running an outdated and vulnerable version of OpenSSH.
* Lack of proper access control mechanisms.
* Potential misconfigurations allowing exploitability.

----

h3. *Recommendation / Remediation:*

# *Update OpenSSH:* Upgrade to the latest secure version available for your distribution.
# *Restrict SSH Access:*
#* Allow SSH connections only from trusted IPs using firewall rules.
#* Disable password authentication and enforce SSH key-based authentication.
# *Disable Root Login:* Set {{PermitRootLogin no}} in {{/etc/ssh/sshd_config}}.
# *Implement Network Segmentation:* Ensure SSH is accessible only from a secure management network.
# *Monitor Logs & Intrusion Detection:* Enable logging and actively monitor {{/var/log/auth.log}} for suspicious activity.
# *Patch Management:* Regularly apply security patches and updates.

Summary: GraphQL Apollo Server Version Disclosure
Description:
h3. *GraphQL Apollo Server Version Disclosure*

h4. *Description*

It was observed that the *GraphQL Apollo Server version* is being disclosed in the HTTP response headers or via introspection queries. This exposes the application to *known vulnerabilities associated with specific Apollo Server versions*, making it easier for attackers to exploit outdated or misconfigured instances.

Attackers can leverage this information to:

* Identify *known vulnerabilities* in the disclosed version.
* Craft *targeted attacks* to exploit security flaws.
* Use automated tools to *enumerate weaknesses* based on the version details.

h4. *Impact*

* *Increases attack surface* by providing attackers with insights into the backend technology.
* *Facilitates zero-day attacks* if an outdated or vulnerable version is in use.
* *Enables reconnaissance attacks*, allowing attackers to plan further exploitation.

h4. *Solution*

* *Disable version disclosure* in Apollo Server by setting {{introspection: false}} in production.
* *Remove version details* from response headers by configuring Apollo Server not to expose them.
* *Regularly update* Apollo Server to the latest stable version to patch security vulnerabilities.
* *Implement proper security headers* to prevent information leakage.

h4. *References*

* [CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html]
* [OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration|https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/]
* [Apollo Server Security Best Practices|https://www.apollographql.com/docs/apollo-server/security/]

h4. *Severity & References*

* *Severity:* *Medium*
* *CWE-ID:* CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information
* *CVSS Score:* *4.3*
* *OWASP Top 10 Category:* A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration

Summary: Missing Multiple Security Headers
Description:
h3. *Missing Multiple Security Headers*

h4. *Description*

The application is *missing critical security headers* in HTTP responses, leaving it vulnerable to multiple attack vectors, including *clickjacking, XSS, MIME sniffing attacks, and data exposure*. The following headers were found to be missing or improperly configured:

* *X-Frame-Options*: Prevents Clickjacking attacks.
* *Content-Security-Policy (CSP)*: Mitigates XSS by restricting script execution.
* *X-Content-Type-Options*: Prevents MIME-type sniffing.
* *Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS)*: Enforces HTTPS connections.
* *Referrer-Policy*: Controls the information shared in HTTP headers.
* *Permissions-Policy*: Restricts access to browser features like camera, microphone, geolocation, etc.

h4. *Impact*

* *Clickjacking attacks* can trick users into performing unintended actions.
* *Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities* are more exploitable without CSP.
* *MIME-type sniffing attacks* can lead to execution of unintended file types.
* *Downgrade attacks* can force HTTP connections if HSTS is not set.
* *Information leakage* occurs due to missing Referrer-Policy.

h4. *Solution*

Implement and correctly configure the following security headers:

{noformat}X-Frame-Options: DENY  
Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'  
X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff  
Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload  
Referrer-Policy: no-referrer  
Permissions-Policy: geolocation=(), microphone=()  
{noformat}

h4. *References*

* [CWE-693: Protection Mechanism Failure|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/693.html]
* [OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration|https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/]
* [OWASP Secure Headers Project|https://owasp.org/www-project-secure-headers/]

Summary: Server Name and Version Disclosure
Description:
h3. *Server Name and Version Disclosure*

h4. *Description*

The application *exposes server name and version information* in HTTP response headers (e.g., {{Server: Apache/2.4.41}} or {{X-Powered-By: Express}}). This disclosure provides *attackers with insights into the underlying technology stack*, making it easier to *identify known vulnerabilities* and craft targeted exploits.

h4. *Impact*

* *Increased attack surface* by revealing software versions with potential security flaws.
* *Facilitates reconnaissance* for attackers looking to exploit outdated or vulnerable services.
* *Enables targeted attacks* using publicly known CVEs against specific server versions.

h4. *Solution*

* *Disable unnecessary HTTP headers* ({{Server}}, {{X-Powered-By}}, {{X-AspNet-Version}}, etc.).
* *Use a reverse proxy or WAF* to mask server details.
* *Regularly update and patch server software* to mitigate known vulnerabilities.
* Configure *strict Content Security Policy (CSP)* to reduce exploitability.

h4. *References*

* [CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html]
* [OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration|https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/]
* [Hardening HTTP Response Headers|https://owasp.org/www-project-secure-headers/]

Summary: Missing DMARC Records
Description:
h3. *Missing DMARC Compliance*

h4. *Description*

It was observed that the application's domain *lacks a properly configured DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting, and Conformance) policy*. DMARC is essential for *email authentication*, preventing *email spoofing, phishing attacks, and business email compromise (BEC)*.

Without DMARC, attackers can *send fraudulent emails appearing to originate from the organization’s domain*, increasing the risk of *phishing attacks and reputational damage*.

h4. *Impact*

* *Increased risk of phishing attacks* using spoofed emails.
* *Brand impersonation* leading to user deception and fraud.
* *Potential business email compromise (BEC)* targeting employees or customers.

h4. *Solution*

* *Implement DMARC with a strict policy* (e.g., {{p=quarantine}} or {{p=reject}}) in the domain’s DNS records.
* Configure *SPF (Sender Policy Framework) and DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail)* to support DMARC authentication.
* Set up *DMARC reporting* ({{rua}} and {{ruf}} tags) to monitor unauthorized email activity.
* Test DMARC configuration using tools like *MXToolbox* or *Google Admin Toolbox*.

h4. *References*

* [CWE-347: Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/347.html]
* [OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration|https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/]
* [DMARC Guide by OWASP|https://owasp.org/www-project-secure-headers/#dmarc]

Summary: Insufficient Error handling
Description:
h3. *Improper Error Handling*

h4. *Description*

The application was found to *return a generic "Internal Server Error" (HTTP 500) response instead of a more appropriate error message* (e.g., "Not Found" - HTTP 404 or "Bad Request" - HTTP 400). Improper error handling can *reveal internal system behavior*, making it easier for attackers to map the application's logic and exploit vulnerabilities.

An attacker can *trigger unexpected errors deliberately*, gathering details about *backend services, database failures, and server misconfigurations*. This can *increase the attack surface*, leading to *further reconnaissance and potential exploits* such as:

* *Denial of Service (DoS) attacks* by causing repeated crashes.
* *Information disclosure* through verbose error messages.

h4. *Impact*

* *User experience degradation* due to unexpected crashes.
* *Potential security risks* from exposed internal errors, aiding attackers in *exploiting weaknesses*.

h4. *Solution*

* *Implement proper error handling* with *custom error pages* for different HTTP status codes.
* *Ensure the application returns appropriate HTTP status codes*:
** *400 Bad Request*: Invalid input.
** *403 Forbidden*: Unauthorized access attempt.
** *404 Not Found*: Non-existent resources.
** *500 Internal Server Error*: Only for actual server-side issues, without exposing stack traces.
* *Disable detailed error messages in production* and log errors securely for internal debugging.

h4. *References*

* [CWE-209: Information Exposure Through an Error Message|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/209.html]
* [OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration|https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/]

h4. *Technical Details*

* *Severity*: Low
* *CVSS Score*: 3.7
* *CWE-ID*: CWE-209
* *OWASP Top 10 Category*: A05 - Security Misconfiguration

Summary: Clickjacking Vulnerability
Description:
h3. *Clickjacking Vulnerability*

h4. *Description*

The application was found to be vulnerable to *Clickjacking* due to missing or improperly configured *X-Frame-Options* and *Content-Security-Policy (CSP) frame-ancestors* headers. Clickjacking allows an attacker to *embed the website within an invisible iframe on a malicious domain*, tricking users into performing unintended actions such as *clicking buttons, submitting forms, or executing transactions*.

This can lead to:

* *Account takeovers* (e.g., forcing users to change their email/password).
* *Unintended fund transfers or actions on financial platforms*.
* *Privilege escalation by tricking admins into executing sensitive operations*.

h4. *Impact*

* *User Interface Redress Attacks* can *trick users into performing unauthorized actions*.
* *Potential phishing attacks* by embedding the application in a malicious site.
* *Compromised sensitive actions* such as *financial transactions or setting changes*.

h4. *Solution*

* *Set the* {{X-Frame-Options}} *header* to {{DENY}} or {{SAMEORIGIN}} to prevent embedding in iframes.
* *Use Content-Security-Policy (CSP)* {{frame-ancestors}} *directive* to explicitly define trusted origins.
* *Implement frame-busting JavaScript* as an additional defense mechanism: 
{noformat}if (self !== top) {
    top.location = self.location;
}
{noformat}

h4. *References*

* [CWE-1021: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/1021.html]
* [OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration|https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/]
* [Clickjacking Defense Cheat Sheet|https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Clickjacking_Defense_Cheat_Sheet.html]

h4. *Technical Details*

* *Severity*: Medium
* *CVSS Score*: 6.3
* *CWE-ID*: CWE-1021
* *OWASP Top 10 Category*: A05 - Security Misconfiguration

Summary: Multiple Portals Accessible Over Public IP of Server
Description:
h3. *Multiple Portals Accessible Over Public IP of Server*

h4. *Description*

Several services and administrative portals were found to be *directly accessible over the server's public IP address*, including:

* *Default Nginx page (Port 80)*
* *Payloads Admin Panel (Port 3001)*
* *MongoDB Database (Port 27017)*
* *SonarQube (Port 9001)*

Exposing these services to the *public internet* significantly increases the risk of *unauthorized access, data breaches, and remote exploitation*. Attackers can scan the *public IP, enumerate open services, and attempt exploits*, leading to *data theft, privilege escalation, or complete system compromise*.

h4. *Impact*

* *Firewall Bypass*: Attackers can access these services *without going through security controls* such as WAFs or VPNs.
* *Unauthorized Database Access*: If *MongoDB* is exposed without authentication, attackers may *query, modify, or delete data*.
* *Admin Panel Takeover*: The *Payloads Admin Panel* may contain *sensitive configuration data, API keys, or control functions*, leading to complete system compromise.
* *SonarQube Exploitation*: If authentication is misconfigured, attackers can *access source code repositories*, extract secrets, or execute *CI/CD pipeline attacks*.

h4. *Solution*

* *Restrict public IP access* by allowing access *only through VPNs or specific internal networks*.
* *Disable unused services* from running on public interfaces.
* *Enforce authentication and access controls* for admin portals and databases.
* *Set up firewall rules* to block direct access to these services from untrusted IPs.
* *Disable default pages (e.g., Nginx default page)* to prevent exposure of server information.

h4. *References*

* [CWE-284: Improper Access Control|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/284.html]
* [CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html]
* [OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration|https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/]

h4. *Technical Details*

* *Severity*: Medium
* *CVSS Score*: 5.2
* *CWE-ID*: CWE-284, CWE-200
* *OWASP Top 10 Category*: A05 - Security Misconfiguration

Summary: Website Directly Accessible via Public IP
Description:
h3. *Website Directly Accessible Over Public IP*

h4. *Description*

The application is accessible directly via its *public IP address*, bypassing the intended *domain-based access controls*. This exposes the website to *attackers, automated scanners, and botnets*, increasing the risk of *unauthorized access, enumeration, and targeted exploits*.

h4. *Impact*

* *Firewall Bypass*: Attackers can directly interact with the application *without passing through security controls* such as *Web Application Firewalls (WAFs)* or *CDN protections* that are typically configured on the domain.
* *IP-Based Targeting*: Exposed public IPs can be used for *DDoS attacks, network-layer exploits, and direct server enumeration*.
* *Domain Restriction Evasion*: If security policies enforce domain-based access controls, attackers can *bypass these restrictions* and directly access internal endpoints.

h4. *Solution*

* *Restrict public IP access* by enforcing access through the *official domain name* only.
* *Configure firewall rules* to allow requests only from *approved networks* and *block direct IP access*.
* *Use security groups or network ACLs* to limit exposure at the infrastructure level.
* *Enable server-side domain verification* to reject requests not originating from the official domain.

h4. *References*

* CWE-284: Improper Access Control
* OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration

h4. *Technical Details*

* *Severity*: High
* *CVSS Score*: 7.5
* *CWE-ID*: CWE-284
* *OWASP Top 10 Category*: A05 - Security Misconfiguration

Summary: GRAPHQL Introspection Query Enabled - Medium
Description:
h3. *GraphQL Introspection Query Enabled*

h4. *Description*

The *GraphQL introspection feature* is enabled, allowing unauthorized users to *query the API schema* and retrieve details about the available queries, mutations, types, and data structures. This can expose *sensitive application logic*, internal API endpoints, and object relationships, increasing the risk of *information disclosure and targeted attacks*.

h4. *Impact*

* *Reconnaissance for Attackers*: Attackers can use introspection to map out the entire API structure, making it easier to find *undocumented, vulnerable, or sensitive endpoints*.
* *API Exploitation*: If introspection reveals *admin-only queries, mutations, or sensitive object types*, attackers could attempt *unauthorized access, privilege escalation, or data extraction*.
* *Increased Attack Surface*: Open introspection *amplifies security risks* by allowing attackers to test for *injection flaws, broken authentication, or misconfigurations* more efficiently.

h4. *Solution*

* *Disable introspection queries in production* to prevent API schema enumeration.
* *Restrict introspection access* to *authenticated and authorized users* via proper role-based access controls (RBAC).
* *Implement proper API rate limiting and monitoring* to detect and block unauthorized GraphQL queries.
* *Sanitize API responses* to ensure that sensitive information is not leaked through error messages or schema details.

h4. *References*

* [CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html]
* [OWASP A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures|https://owasp.org/Top10/A07_2021-Identification_and_Authentication_Failures/]
* [GraphQL Security Best Practices|https://graphql.org/learn/security/]

h4. *Technical Details*

* *Severity*: Medium
* *CVSS Score*: 5.3
* *CWE-ID*: CWE-200
* *OWASP Top 10 Category*: A07 - Identification and Authentication Failures

Summary: Cookies Without Security Flags
Description:
h3. *Cookies Without Security Flags*

h4. *Description*

The application’s cookies *lack critical security flags*, specifically:

* {{HttpOnly}} *flag is missing*, making cookies accessible via JavaScript, which increases the risk of *cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks* stealing session tokens.
* {{SameSite}} *attribute is not properly configured* (instead of {{Lax}}, it is unset or improperly set), making the application vulnerable to *cross-site request forgery (CSRF) attacks*.

h4. *Impact*

* *XSS Exploitation*: Attackers can execute JavaScript code to steal cookies if {{HttpOnly}} is not set.
* *CSRF Attacks*: Without {{SameSite=Lax}} or {{Strict}}, attackers can trick users into making unintended requests on their behalf.
* *Session Hijacking*: Lack of these flags increases the likelihood of *session theft*, unauthorized actions, and privilege escalation.

h4. *Solution*

* *Set* {{HttpOnly}} *flag* for all session-related cookies to prevent JavaScript access.
* *Set* {{SameSite=Lax}} *or* {{SameSite=Strict}} to mitigate CSRF attacks.
* *Ensure* {{Secure}} *flag is enabled* if the application is served over HTTPS to prevent cookies from being transmitted over insecure connections.
* Regularly audit cookie configurations and enforce security best practices.

h4. *References*

* [CWE-1004: Sensitive Cookie Without HttpOnly Flag|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/1004.html]
* [CWE-1275: Sensitive Cookie Without 'SameSite' Attribute|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/1275.html]
* [OWASP A07:2021 - Identification and Authentication Failures|https://owasp.org/Top10/A07_2021-Identification_and_Authentication_Failures/]

h4. *Technical Details*

* *Severity*: Medium
* *CVSS Score*: 6.5
* *CWE-ID*: CWE-1004, CWE-1275
* *OWASP Top 10 Category*: A07 - Identification and Authentication Failures

Summary: Missing Input and File-type Validations on Profile Page Input Fields
Description:
h3. *Missing Input and File-Type Validations on Profile Page Input Fields*

h4. *Description*

The application *lacks proper input validation and file-type restrictions* on multiple input fields within the *Profile page*, allowing users to submit *malformed, excessively long, or potentially malicious data*. Additionally, the *absence of strict file-type validation* exposes the system to *dangerous file uploads*, potentially leading to code execution or unauthorized access.

h4. *Impact*

* *Cross-Site Scripting (XSS):* Malicious scripts injected into text fields can be executed in the browser, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or defacement.
* *SQL Injection:* If backend sanitization is inadequate, attackers may manipulate database queries, leading to data leaks or corruption.
* *Denial of Service (DoS):* Overly long inputs can crash or freeze UI components, overload memory, or disrupt backend processing.
* *Unrestricted File Upload Risks:* Attackers may upload *malicious executables, scripts, or oversized files*, leading to remote code execution, privilege escalation, or disk exhaustion.

h4. *Solution*

* *Enforce strict input validation* on all text fields (e.g., name, email, address, etc.).
* *Set maximum character limits* and restrict input format based on expected values.
* *Implement allow-list validation* to accept only predefined input patterns (e.g., alphanumeric, special characters where required).
* *Sanitize and encode user inputs* to prevent *XSS and SQL injection attacks*.
* *Apply robust file validation*:
** Allow only specific file types (e.g., PNG, JPG, PDF).
** *Verify MIME type and magic bytes* rather than relying on file extensions.
** Set *maximum file size limits* to prevent resource exhaustion.
* *Implement server-side validation* to ensure that inputs are properly validated and sanitized before processing.

h4. *References*

* [CWE-20: Improper Input Validation|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/20.html]
* [CWE-434: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/434.html]
* [OWASP A04:2021 - Insecure Design|https://owasp.org/Top10/A04_2021-Insecure_Design/]
* [OWASP A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration|https://owasp.org/Top10/A05_2021-Security_Misconfiguration/]

h4. *Technical Details*

* *Severity*: High
* *CVSS Score*: 8.1
* *CWE-ID*: CWE-20, CWE-434
* *OWASP Top 10 Category*: A04 - Insecure Design, A05 - Security Misconfiguration

Summary: HTML Injection
Description:
h3. *HTML Injection*

*Severity:* High
*CWE-ID:* CWE-79: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A03: Injection

h4. *Description:*

The application fails to properly sanitize user-supplied input, allowing *HTML injection*. This vulnerability enables an attacker to inject arbitrary HTML code into web pages viewed by other users. If JavaScript execution is possible, it may escalate to *Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)*.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Arbitrary Redirection*: Attackers can inject malicious links leading users to phishing pages.
* *Session Hijacking*: If cookies are accessible via JavaScript, attackers can steal session tokens.
* *Data Leakage*: Sensitive user information may be exposed.
* *Unauthorized Actions (CSRF-like attacks)*: Malicious scripts may trigger unintended actions on behalf of the user.
* *Keylogging*: Injected scripts can log user keystrokes and exfiltrate credentials.

h4. *Root Cause:*

The application *does not properly encode or sanitize user input*, allowing untrusted data to be rendered as HTML.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Sanitize user input*:
** Encode HTML entities (e.g., {{<script>}} should be {{<script>}}).
** Use libraries like *DOMPurify* for client-side sanitation.
* *Implement Content Security Policy (CSP)*: Restrict script execution to trusted sources.
* *Use HTTP-only cookies*: Prevent session hijacking via JavaScript access.
* *Validate and escape output*: Ensure *user-generated content is escaped* before rendering in the browser.

[https://{{slack-communication-url}}/archives/C06A7BHTZG8/p1739527963197119|https://{{slack-communication-url}}/archives/C06A7BHTZG8/p1739527963197119|smart-link]

Summary: Session Expiry too Long
Description:
h3. *Session Expiry Very Long (3 Months)*

*Severity:* High
*CWE-ID:* CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A07: Identification and Authentication Failures

h4. *Description:*

The application sets *session expiration to 3 months*, allowing users to stay logged in for an extended period without requiring re-authentication. This poses a *significant security risk*, especially on shared or compromised devices.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Increased Risk of Session Hijacking*: If an attacker gains access to a user's session token, they can exploit it for an extended period.
* *No Automatic Logout*: Users may remain logged in indefinitely, leading to potential unauthorized access if they leave a session open on a public or shared device.
* *Non-Compliance with Security Standards*: Many security regulations (e.g., OWASP, PCI-DSS, NIST) recommend *shorter session lifetimes*.

h4. *Root Cause:*

The *session timeout is not configured securely*, allowing persistent sessions for an excessive period.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Reduce session expiration*: Limit session lifetime to *15–30 minutes of inactivity*.
* *Implement sliding expiration*: Extend session only on user activity, but with a maximum lifetime of *8–12 hours*.
* *Enforce re-authentication*: Require users to re-enter credentials for sensitive actions after a short period.
* *Use secure session handling*:
** Implement *session invalidation* on logout.
** Bind sessions to *IP address and user agent*.
** Store session tokens securely and avoid exposure in URLs.

Summary: No validation on Terms and Condition from Backend - High 7.2
Description:
h3. *No Validation on Terms and Conditions from Backend*

*Severity:* High (7.2)
*CWE-ID:* CWE-285: Improper Authorization
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A01: Broken Access Control

h4. *Description:*

The application *does not enforce validation* for accepting the *Terms and Conditions* from the backend. This allows users to bypass the requirement by manipulating the request and proceeding without explicitly agreeing to the terms.

h4. *Impact:*

* *Legal & Compliance Risk*: Users can *bypass legal agreements*, leading to compliance violations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA).
* *Fraudulent Activity*: Malicious users can exploit this flaw to engage in activities *without accepting liability or obligations*.
* *Potential Data Abuse*: Users may claim they never agreed to terms, which could lead to legal disputes.

h4. *Root Cause:*

The *backend does not validate* whether the user has accepted the Terms and Conditions before allowing them to proceed.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Enforce backend validation*: The API should strictly check for the *acceptance flag* before processing the request.
* *Use server-side validation*: Do not rely on *frontend-only checks* for enforcing acceptance.
* *Log acceptance events*: Maintain an audit trail of *timestamped user agreements* for legal compliance.
* *Require explicit consent*: Use a *checkbox or digital signature* that cannot be bypassed.

Summary: Localhost and port number is mentioned swagger
Description:
*Severity:* Low (3.5)
*CWE-ID:* CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A05: Security Misconfiguration

h4. *Description:*

The *Swagger API documentation* exposes internal details, including *localhost and port numbers*. This information can be useful for attackers to:

* Identify *internal services and configurations*.
* Gain insights into *microservices or APIs* running on different ports.
* Perform *targeted enumeration and exploitation* of exposed endpoints.

h4. *Impact:*

* Attackers can map internal infrastructure and *recon internal services*.
* In some cases, sensitive APIs or debug endpoints might be *accessible externally* if misconfigured.
* If the application runs in a *containerized or cloud environment*, attackers may try *port scanning attacks* to identify exposed services.

h4. *Root Cause:*

Swagger documentation is publicly exposing *localhost details and port numbers* in the API definition.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Remove localhost and port details* from public Swagger documentation.
* Restrict access to Swagger UI using *authentication and authorization controls*.
* Use *environment variables* to dynamically configure API base URLs instead of hardcoding them.
* Disable Swagger documentation in *production environments* or restrict access to internal users only.

Summary: Sensitive Data being stored in Local Storage(Merged with 207)
Description:
h3. *Sensitive Data Stored in Local Storage*

*Severity:* High (7.5)
*CWE-ID:* CWE-312: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A07: Identification and Authentication Failures

h4. *Description:*

It was observed that sensitive information, including *adminToken, admin email address, and all user details*, is being stored in the browser's *local storage*. Local storage is accessible via JavaScript and remains persistent even after the session ends, making it a high-risk storage method for sensitive data.

An attacker exploiting *Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)* could easily extract these values, leading to *account takeover, privilege escalation, and data leakage*.

h4. *Impact:*

* Exposure of *adminToken* could allow attackers to authenticate as an administrator, leading to complete system compromise.
* *Email address leakage* may facilitate *targeted phishing attacks*.
* Storing *all user details* in local storage exposes personal information, violating *privacy regulations (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA).*

h4. *Root Cause:*

Application stores *adminToken, email address of the admin, and all user details* in local storage instead of using *secure session-based storage mechanisms.*

h4. *Solution:*

* *Never store sensitive data in local storage*; use *HTTP-only, secure cookies* instead.
* Implement *token expiration* and enforce *server-side session management.*
* Apply *Content Security Policy (CSP)* to mitigate XSS risks.
* Encrypt and store sensitive information securely on the backend instead of the client-side.

Summary: Account Lockout Policy leads to Internal Server Error
Description:
h3. *Account Lockout Policy Leads to Internal Server Error*

*Severity:* High (7.2)
*CWE-ID:* CWE-755: Improper Handling of Exceptional Conditions
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

h4. *Description:*

It was observed that after entering an incorrect password 3-4 times, the application throws a *500 Internal Server Error* instead of properly handling the account lockout policy. Instead of displaying a user-friendly message indicating that the account has been locked due to multiple failed login attempts, the server crashes, leading to a potential denial of service (DoS).

h4. *Impact:*

* The error may expose sensitive server details if the response contains stack traces.
* Attackers could exploit this behavior to cause system instability.
* Users may get confused, as they are not properly informed that their account has been locked.

h4. *Root Cause:*

The application does not correctly handle the account lockout mechanism, causing an unexpected error when the threshold for failed login attempts is reached.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Properly handle account lockout errors*: Return a clear error message such as _"Your account has been locked due to multiple failed login attempts. Please try again later or contact support."_
* *Ensure appropriate HTTP response codes*: Use {{403 Forbidden}} instead of {{500 Internal Server Error}}.
* *Log authentication failures securely*: Implement structured logging to detect brute-force attempts and account lockouts.
* *Prevent sensitive data leaks*: Disable debug mode and prevent stack traces from being exposed in error responses.
* *Fix backend exception handling*: Implement proper exception handling to ensure the server does not crash upon reaching the lockout threshold.

Summary: Server Name and Version Disclosure
Description:
h3. *Server Name and Version Disclosure*

*Severity:* Medium (4.5)
*CWE-ID:* [CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/200.html]
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A06: Vulnerable and Outdated Components

h4. *Description:*

During security testing, it was observed that the application discloses server name and version details in response headers and/or error messages. Attackers can use this information to fingerprint the server and identify potential vulnerabilities associated with the disclosed version.

h4. *Root Cause:*

The application is configured to expose detailed server information in HTTP response headers ({{Server}}, {{X-Powered-By}}, or {{X-AspNet-Version}}) or within error messages.

h4. *Solution:*

* *Disable unnecessary headers*: Remove or obfuscate {{Server}}, {{X-Powered-By}}, and similar headers from responses.
* *Configure the web server securely*:
** *For Apache*: Set {{ServerTokens Prod}} and {{ServerSignature Off}} in {{httpd.conf}}.
** *For Nginx*: Use {{server_tokens off;}} in {{nginx.conf}}.
** *For IIS*: Use {{Remove-ItemProperty}} to delete {{X-Powered-By}} and {{Server}} headers.
* *Handle errors securely*: Avoid exposing detailed error messages and stack traces in responses.

Summary: Improper Input Validation: Project Investment in Decimal Shares Allowed
Description:
h3. *Improper Input Validation: Investment in Decimal Shares Allowed*

*Severity:* High (7.8)
*CWE-ID:* [CWE-20: Improper Input Validation|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/20.html]
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A04: Insecure Design

h4. *Description:*

It was observed that the application allows users to invest in a project using fractional ({{decimal}}) share values instead of restricting investments to whole shares. As seen in the request and response, an investment of {{0.5}} shares was processed successfully.

This flaw could lead to financial discrepancies, manipulation of share allocations, or potential abuse of the investment system. An attacker could exploit this by making unintended micro-investments, bypassing minimum thresholds, or causing inconsistencies in financial records.

h4. *Root Cause:*

The application does not enforce integer validation on the {{purchasedShares}} parameter, allowing fractional values to be processed.

h4. *Solution:*

* Implement strict server-side validation to allow only whole numbers (integers) for share purchases.
* Enforce input validation at both the frontend and backend to prevent API manipulation.
* Use appropriate data types (e.g., integer constraints in the database) to ensure consistency.
* Perform business logic validation to prevent unauthorized micro-investments and financial miscalculations.

Summary: Improper Input Validation: Fractional Company Shares Allowed(Reapeated bug)
Description:
h3. *Improper Input Validation: Fractional Company Shares Allowed*

*Severity:* High (7.8)
*CWE-ID:* [CWE-20: Improper Input Validation|https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/20.html]
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A04: Insecure Design

h4. *Description:*

During testing, it was observed that the application allows users to purchase fractional ({{decimal}}) shares of a company, even if the expected behavior is to permit only whole shares. As shown in the request and response, a value of {{0.5}} shares was submitted and successfully processed by the application.

This flaw can lead to financial inconsistencies, unauthorized benefits, or potential exploitation if attackers manipulate share calculations to gain unfair advantages (e.g., bypassing minimum investment requirements or triggering unintended behavior in financial transactions).

h4. *Root Cause:*

The application does not validate and enforce whole number (integer) constraints on the {{purchasedShares}} parameter, allowing fractional values to be processed.

h4. *Solution:*

* Implement strict server-side validation to ensure that only whole numbers (integers) are accepted for {{purchasedShares}}.
* Validate input both at the frontend and backend to prevent manipulation via API requests.
* Use proper data types (e.g., integer constraints in the database) to avoid unintended float calculations.
* Perform business logic validation to prevent incorrect share allocations and financial miscalculations.

Summary: Improper Input Validation: Negative Investment Allowed
Description:
h3. *Improper Input Validation: Negative Investment Allowed*

*Severity:* Low (3.9)
*CWE-ID:* CWE-20: Improper Input Validation
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A04: Insecure Design

h4. *Description:*

While testing the investment functionality, it was observed that the application allowed users to set the investment value ({{purchasedShares}}) to a negative number. This effectively reversed the transaction amount, creating an invalid investment scenario. However, due to restrictions at the payment gateway, the checkout process was blocked.

If the payment gateway had also failed to implement proper backend validation, the system might have processed negative transactions, leading to financial discrepancies, fraudulent gains, or even account manipulation. The issue highlights a lack of proper input validation at the application level, which should not rely solely on third-party services for enforcing restrictions.

h4. *Root Cause:*

The application does not validate user input to prevent negative values for investment transactions.

h4. *Solution:*

* Implement server-side validation to ensure investment amounts are always positive.
* Enforce input validation at both frontend and backend layers.
* Use strong data validation mechanisms (e.g., allow only positive integers for {{purchasedShares}}).
* Perform boundary testing to prevent similar input validation bypass scenarios.

Summary: Improper Error Handling Leading to Runtime Error (Merged with 181)
Description:
h3. *Improper Error Handling Leading to Runtime Error*

*Severity:* Medium (4.0)
*CWE-ID:* CWE-248: Uncaught Exception
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

h4. *Description:*

While testing as an investor user, it was observed that after completing an investment, clicking the back arrow to navigate away from the payment page (where card details need to be added) resulted in a runtime error. The application fails to handle this scenario gracefully, leading to an unexpected crash instead of proper redirection or error messaging.

Improper error handling can degrade user experience and expose the application to potential security risks. Attackers may exploit such runtime errors to identify flaws in the application's logic, potentially leading to further exploits like denial-of-service (DoS) attacks or unauthorized access.

h4. *Root Cause:*

The application does not properly handle navigation state transitions, causing an unhandled exception when attempting to return from the payment page.

h4. *Solution:*

* Implement proper exception handling to catch and manage unexpected errors.
* Ensure proper state management so that navigation between pages does not result in runtime crashes.
* Provide user-friendly error messages instead of exposing raw runtime errors.
* Perform thorough testing on navigation flows to detect and resolve similar issues.

Summary: Excessive Password Length Allowed
Description:
h3. *Excessive Password Length Allowed*

*Severity:* Medium (4.6)
*CWE-ID:* CWE-521: Weak Password Requirements
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A07: Identification and Authentication Failures

h4. *Description:*

The application allows users to set passwords longer than 64 characters. While NIST SP800-63B recommends supporting passwords of at least 64 characters to accommodate passphrases, excessive password lengths can lead to potential denial-of-service (DoS) attacks due to the increased computational overhead of hashing long inputs.

Allowing overly long passwords without proper limits can impact application performance, especially if the hashing algorithm is resource-intensive. An attacker could exploit this by submitting extremely long passwords, leading to service slowdowns or even application crashes.

h4. *Root Cause:*

The application does not enforce a reasonable upper limit on password length, leading to potential DoS risks in hashing computations.

h4. *Solution:*

* Set a maximum password length limit (e.g., 128 characters) to balance security and performance.
* Ensure the hashing mechanism efficiently handles long passwords without excessive CPU or memory usage.
* Implement rate limiting or request validation to prevent DoS attacks using long password inputs.
* Follow best practices outlined in the [OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet|https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html#implement-proper-password-strength-controls].

Summary: Improper Error Handling and Information Disclosure
Description:
Improper Error Handling and Information Disclosure
Severity: High (7.2)
CWE-ID: CWE-209: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category: A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures

Description:
During testing of the password change functionality, it was observed that forcing a password change with a lower complexity string resulted in a 500 Internal Server Error instead of the expected 400 Bad Request, 403 Forbidden or 422 Unprocessible Entity. Additionally, the error response exposed a detailed stack trace in JSON format, revealing sensitive implementation details such as expected HTTP methods, the exact server version, and other potentially exploitable information.

Improper error handling can lead to information disclosure, allowing attackers to gain insights into the application’s internal structure, technologies used, and potential misconfigurations. This increases the attack surface, making it easier to craft targeted exploits or perform reconnaissance for further attacks.

Root Cause:
The application does not properly handle exceptions, leading to server errors and exposure of sensitive debugging information in API responses.

Solution:

* Implement proper exception handling to prevent application crashes.
* Ensure error messages are generic and do not reveal sensitive internal details.
* Return appropriate HTTP status codes.
* Disable verbose error reporting in production environments.
* Log detailed errors securely on the server side instead of exposing them to users.

Summary: Insecure Password Reuse Policy
Description:
h3. *Insecure Password Reuse Policy*

*Severity:* Medium (5.6)
*CWE-ID:* CWE-263: Password Aging with Insufficient Enforcement
*OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category:* A07: Identification and Authentication Failures

h4. *Description:*

The application allows users to set their old password as the new password during the password change process. There is no enforcement of a password history policy, permitting users to reuse their previous credentials without restriction.

This vulnerability weakens the security of the authentication mechanism by allowing users to bypass best practices for password hygiene. Attackers leveraging credential stuffing or previously compromised passwords may find it easier to gain unauthorized access if users habitually reuse their old passwords.

h4. *Root Cause:*

The application does not enforce a password history policy to prevent users from reusing previous passwords.

h4. *Solution:*

* Implement a password history policy restricting the reuse of the last 3–5 passwords.
* Store hashed previous passwords and compare them before allowing a new password update.
* Enforce strong password policies to encourage secure password changes.
* Provide user-friendly feedback when a previously used password is attempted.
* Ideal status Code : 409 Conflict

Summary: Improper Compliance Officer Signout
Description:
Improper Compliance Officer Signout
Severity: Low (2.9)
CWE-ID: CWE-613: Insufficient Session Expiration
OWASP Top 10 (Web 2021) Category: A07: Identification and Authentication Failures

Root Cause : Application doesn’t clears the local storage and cookies upon logout.

Description:
Upon updating the password, a security notification is displayed indicating that due to security reasons, the user is being logged out. However, despite this message, the user interface (UI) remains unchanged, and the data in cookies and localStorage persists. Additionally, when attempting to sign out the user for compliance purposes, clicking the "Signout" button does not log the user out of the application. Although the API properly returns a status code 401 (Unauthorized), the UI continues to reflect the user as logged in, and the session data is not cleared as expected.

This issue can lead to confusion for the user and potential security risks. If the session is not visually terminated, an attacker with physical or remote access to an open session may exploit the inconsistency to extract sensitive information or perform unauthorized actions before the session fully expires.

Solution:


* Ensure the UI properly reflects the logout state by clearing session data and redirecting the user to the login screen.
* Implement client-side session storage cleanup upon logout.
* Verify that authentication tokens and session cookies are properly invalidated.

Summary: Cookies Lacks Security Flags
Description:
Cookies are often used for maintaining user sessions or storing sensitive information. However, if cookies lack important security flags such as HTTPOnly, Secure, and SameSite, they become vulnerable to various attacks. Without the HTTPOnly flag, cookies can be accessed by client-side scripts (e.g., JavaScript), making them susceptible to Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks. The Secure flag ensures that cookies are transmitted only over secure (HTTPS) connections, preventing them from being exposed during man-in-the-middle attacks. If SameSite is set to None, the cookie can be sent in cross-origin requests, making it vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) attacks.

The absence of these flags increases the risk of session hijacking, XSS, and CSRF attacks, which can lead to unauthorized access, data theft, or malicious actions being performed on behalf of users. These vulnerabilities compromise both user privacy and application integrity.

Summary: Graphql Introspection Enabled
Description:
*GraphQL Introspection* allows clients to query the schema of a GraphQL API, providing detailed information about its structure, including types, fields, queries, mutations, and even comments. While introspection is a powerful developer tool, leaving it enabled in production environments can pose a significant security risk.

h3. *Impact*

# *Information Disclosure*:
#* Attackers can use introspection to map out the entire API schema, revealing sensitive queries, mutations, or fields that might not be intended for public exposure.
#* Misconfigured or overly permissive APIs can inadvertently expose sensitive database operations.
# *Attack Surface Expansion*:
#* With detailed schema knowledge, attackers can craft malicious queries, leading to potential SQL injection, unauthorized data retrieval, or Denial of Service (DoS) attacks by sending complex or deeply nested queries.
# *Enumeration*:
#* Attackers can identify deprecated or hidden endpoints that might still process requests, exposing functionality that developers assumed was inaccessible.

h3. *Solution*

# *Disable Introspection in Production*:
#* Modify the GraphQL server configuration to disable introspection queries in production.
# *Schema Whitelisting:*
Restrict sensitive fields and operations by using schema-level access controls or middleware that enforces permissions.
# *Authentication & Authorization:*
Enforce strict authentication and role-based access control (RBAC) on all queries and mutations.
# *Query Complexity Limitation*:
#* Use query depth limits and timeouts to mitigate DoS attacks.

Summary: Misconfigured CORS Trusts Arbitrary Domain
Description:
An HTML5 cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) policy controls whether and how content running on other domains can perform two-way interaction with the domain that publishes the policy. The policy is fine-grained and can apply access controls per-request based on the URL and other features of the request.

Trusting arbitrary origins effectively disables the same-origin policy, allowing two-way interaction by third-party web sites. Unless the response consists only of unprotected public content, this policy is likely to present a security risk.

If the site specifies the header Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, third-party sites may be able to carry out privileged actions and retrieve sensitive information. Even if it does not, attackers may be able to bypass any IP-based access controls by proxying through users' browsers.

h3. Remediation

Rather than using a wildcard or programmatically verifying supplied origins, use a whitelist of trusted domains.

Summary: Missing Strict Transport Security and other security headers
Description: The "Missing Strict Transport Security Header" vulnerability occurs when a web server does not include the (HSTS) header in its HTTP responses. HSTS enforces secure communication by instructing browsers to interact with the server only over HTTPS, preventing protocol downgrade attacks and mitigating man-in-the-middle attacks. Without this header, attackers could exploit insecure connections or trick users into accessing the site over HTTP, exposing sensitive data to interception. Implementing HSTS ensures encrypted traffic, strengthens website security, and builds user trust by reducing the risk of data compromise.

Summary: Server Name and Version Disclosure
Description:
Server name and version disclosure is a vulnerability where a web server reveals sensitive information, such as its software name and version number, through HTTP headers, error messages, or responses. This information can be exploited by attackers to identify known vulnerabilities associated with specific server versions. If the server is running outdated or unpatched software, it may be vulnerable to attacks like remote code execution, denial of service, or unauthorized access. By disclosing the server version, organizations inadvertently provide attackers with the knowledge needed to craft targeted exploits, making it essential to disable such disclosures to enhance security.

Summary: Error message revealing information about the URL structure (FP)
File Upload Restrictions Bypass + Unauthorized file upload
Description:
Pre-requisites: Attacker needs to be in the same network as that of the admin.Steps:

# Attacker intercepts the traffic of the internal network and finds a GET request where adminAccessToken is getting supplied in cleartext.
# Modify the name of the file to be uploaded and trigger the request from a temporary client/browser, here i used Burpsuite repeater.
# This is how I got the signed data of the S3 bucket.
# using this data I created an HTML file which allows me to select the file to be uploaded, here I changed the extension to php just to check (From the admin panel or even the android app one cannot upload arbitrary filetypes).
# Then opened the html created in a browser and selected a php file to be uploaded and upon submitting i was able to upload data to the S3 bucket without having knowledge of the access token, without need to authenticate myself and even upload a restricted filetype in the s3 bucket.

Impact:

* Using the admin access token for a limited time attacker can trigger apis or gain unauthorised access to admin panel.
* Can upload unsupported/restricted filetypes to the S3 bucket
* Being a static storage it doesn't poses critical security risk but if using automation an attacker uploads a large number of files our storage might run out or cause DoS.

Solution:

* Prevent auth token from passing in GET URL.
* Implement proper ACL, and CORS configuration.
* Monitor logs for frequent suspicious uploads.

Summary: Cookie with SameSite Attribute None
Description: A cookie has been set with its SameSite attribute set to "none", which means that the cookie can be sent as a result of a 'cross-site' request. The SameSite attribute is an effective counter measure to cross-site request forgery, cross-site script inclusion, and timing attacks.

Summary: Missing Input Validations from BE
Description: Missing input validations on the backend present significant security risks, even when using a Web Application Firewall (WAF) like Cloudflare. While a WAF can reduce the likelihood of injection-based attacks by filtering out malicious requests, it is not foolproof. Attackers can still craft specially designed exploits that may bypass WAF protections, potentially allowing them to inject malicious code into the application. This can lead to various vulnerabilities, including SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), and remote code execution. Therefore, robust input validation is essential to ensure that only properly formatted and expected data is processed, minimizing the risk of exploitation and enhancing overall application security.

Summary: Loosely Scoped Cookies (Exceptional Close)
Description:
Cookies that are loosely scoped to just the parent domain, rather than specific paths or subdomains, can pose significant security risks, as they become accessible to all subdomains within that parent domain. This broad accessibility increases the attack surface for potential cross-site scripting (XSS) and cross-site request forgery (CSRF) vulnerabilities, allowing malicious scripts or sites to manipulate or steal sensitive user data stored in cookies. Additionally, if a subdomain is compromised, attackers can leverage the cookies intended for other subdomains or the parent domain, leading to unauthorized access to user sessions and personal information. As a result, proper scoping and path restrictions are essential for maintaining cookie security and protecting user data from exploitation.

Always scope cookies to a FQDN (Fully Qualified Domain Name).

Summary: Admin Authentication Token supplied in GET URL
Description:
Using sensitive tokens in a GET request URL, as seen in the provided example, poses significant security risks, including exposure through logs, browser history, and referrer headers, potentially allowing unauthorized access to critical resources. If an attacker gains access to this URL, they can exploit the token to perform actions without consent, leading to data breaches or manipulation. To mitigate the risk consider implementing Authorisation Token header or some other technique to validate the identity of the requesting user rather then supplying the Adminaccesstoken in get request parameters.

Summary: Missing HSTS header on webelight domain server
Description:
The absence of the HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) header on a web application exposes it to significant security vulnerabilities. HSTS is a web security policy mechanism that helps protect websites against man-in-the-middle (MitM) attacks and cookie hijacking by enforcing the use of secure connections (HTTPS). Without this header, users may inadvertently connect to the website over unsecured HTTP, allowing attackers to intercept sensitive data and compromise user sessions.

POC : 

!image-20240920-105710.png|width=1883,height=712,alt="image-20240920-105710.png"!

!image-20240920-105735.png|width=1866,height=713,alt="image-20240920-105735.png"!

+*Solution*+

To mitigate these risks, implement the HSTS header in the web application’s response headers. The following configuration is recommended:
Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload


This configuration specifies:max-age=31536000: Instructs compliant browsers to enforce HTTPS for the next 31536000 seconds (1 year).
includeSubDomains: Ensures that the HSTS policy is applied to all subdomains, enhancing overall security.
preload: Indicates a desire to include the domain in the HSTS preload list maintained by major browsers.

Summary: Spoofable Client IP (Suspicious)
Description:
If an application trusts an HTTP request header like X-Forwarded-For to accurately specify the remote IP address of the connecting client, then malicious clients can spoof their IP address. This behavior does not necessarily constitute a security vulnerability, however some applications use client IP addresses to enforce access controls and rate limits. For example, an application might expose administrative functionality only to clients connecting from the local IP address of the server, or allow a certain number of failed login attempts from each unique IP address. Consider reviewing relevant functionality to determine whether this might be the case.

!image-20240919-052347.png|width=1358,height=398,alt="image-20240919-052347.png"!

!image-20240919-052453.png|width=1374,height=437,alt="image-20240919-052453.png"!

If the platform application server returns incorrect information about the client's IP address due to the presence of any particular HTTP request header, then the server may need to be reconfigured, or an alternative method of identifying clients should be used.

Summary: Cookies without HTTPOnly Flag
Description:
The following cookies were issued by the application and do not have the HttpOnly flag set: 

* CloudFront-Policy
* CloudFront-Signature
* CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id

 The cookies do not appear to contain session tokens, which may reduce the risk associated with this issue.

!image-20240919-051836.png|width=381,height=95,alt="image-20240919-051836.png"!

!image-20240919-051901.png|width=508,height=378,alt="image-20240919-051901.png"!

Solution  :

There is usually no good reason not to set the HttpOnly flag on all cookies. Unless you specifically require legitimate client-side scripts within your application to read or set a cookie's value, you should set the HttpOnly flag by including this attribute within the relevant Set-cookie directive.

Summary: API lacks Security Headers
Description:
The application fails to prevent users from connecting to it over unencrypted connections. An attacker able to modify a legitimate user's network traffic could bypass the application's use of SSL/TLS encryption, and use the application as a platform for attacks against its users. This attack is performed by rewriting HTTPS links as HTTP, so that if a targeted user follows a link to the site from an HTTP page, their browser never attempts to use an encrypted connection. The sslstrip tool automates this process.

To exploit this vulnerability, an attacker must be suitably positioned to intercept and modify the victim's network traffic.This scenario typically occurs when a client communicates with the server over an insecure connection such as public Wi-Fi, or a corporate or home network that is shared with a compromised computer. Common defenses such as switched networks are not sufficient to prevent this. An attacker situated in the user's ISP or the application's hosting infrastructure could also perform this attack.
